rails/rails#54148.body:
Once in a while Trix may have [security issues](https://github.com/basecamp/trix/security/advisories/[REDACTED_GHSA]) and require a release.  Since Rails embeds Trix, we have to do a release as well.

Can we remove Trix and let people install it via source maps, or extract Trix to a gem so that when there is a security issue we don't need to release all of Rails?

There might be another solution as well, but I'm not sure what.
rails/rails#54148.title: Extract or remove Trix

rails/rails#54200.body:
The CSP hash-source is not enclosed in `'` and is ignored by the browser.

### Steps to reproduce
<!-- (Guidelines for creating a bug report are [available
here](https://edgeguides.rubyonrails.org/contributing_to_ruby_on_rails.html#creating-a-bug-report)) -->

<!-- Paste your executable test case created from one of the scripts found [here](https://edgeguides.rubyonrails.org/contributing_to_ruby_on_rails.html#create-an-executable-test-case) below: -->

```ruby
# frozen_string_literal: true

require "bundler/inline"

gemfile(true) do
  source "https://rubygems.org"

  gem "rails"
  # If you want to test against edge Rails replace the previous line with this:
  # gem "rails", github: "rails/rails", branch: "main"
end

require "action_controller/railtie"
require "minitest/autorun"
require "rack/test"

class TestApp < Rails::Application
  config.load_defaults Rails::VERSION::STRING.to_f
  config.root = __dir__
  config.eager_load = false
  config.hosts << "example.org"
  config.secret_key_base = "secret_key_base"
  config.content_security_policy do |policy|
    policy.script_src "sha256-example-script-hash"
  end
  config.logger = Logger.new($stdout)
end
Rails.application.initialize!

Rails.application.routes.draw do
  get "/", to: "test#index"
end

class TestController < ActionController::Base
  include Rails.application.routes.url_helpers

  def index
    render plain: "Home"
  end
end

class BugTest < ActiveSupport::TestCase
  include Rack::Test::Methods

  def test_hash_source_must_be_enclosed_in_single_quotation
    get "/"
    assert_equal last_response.headers["Content-Security-Policy"], "script-src 'sha256-example-script-hash'"
  end

  private
    def app
      Rails.application
    end
end
```

### Expected behavior

When using a hash-source in the Content Security Policy, the correct approach is to enclose the hash-source in `'`.

As shown below, CSP allows inline scripts by specifying the hash of the inline script as a hash-source.

> Unsafe inline script
> 
> you can create hashes from your inline scripts. CSP supports sha256, sha384 and sha512.
> 
> ```
> Content-Security-Policy: script-src 'sha256-B2yPHKaXnvFWtRChIbabYmUBFZdVfKKXHbWtWidDVF8='
> ```
> 
> https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Headers/Content-Security-Policy/script-src#unsafe_inline_script

According to the official W3C definition of a hash-source, it must be enclosed in `'`.

> hash-source    = "'" hash-algorithm "-" base64-value "'"
> hash-algorithm = "sha256" / "sha384" / "sha512"
> https://www.w3.org/TR/CSP3/#grammardef-hash-source


### Actual behavior
<!-- Tell us what happens instead -->

The specified hash-source string is not enclosed in `'` and is ignored by the browser.

Below is the message that was displayed when I checked with Chrome.

```
The source list for the Content Security Policy directive 'script-src' contains an invalid source: 'sha256-example-script-hash'. It will be ignored.
```

For your Infomation, after enclosing it with `'` as follows, the browser now recognizes the `hash-source` in my environment.

```diff
--- a/actionpack/lib/action_dispatch/http/content_security_policy.rb
+++ b/actionpack/lib/action_dispatch/http/content_security_policy.rb
@@ -305,7 +305,13 @@ def apply_mappings(sources)
           case source
           when Symbol
             apply_mapping(source)
-          when String, Proc
+          when String
+            if source.start_with?("sha256", "sha384", "sha512")
+              "'#{source}'"
+            else
+              source
+            end
+          when Proc
             source
           else
             raise ArgumentError, "Invalid content security policy source: #{source.inspect}"
```

ref: https://github.com/rails/rails/blob/07481acd5bf2554ea6ad4446f9d2d06f888b0e8d/actionpack/lib/action_dispatch/http/content_security_policy.rb#L303-L314

### System configuration

**Rails version**: v8.0.1

**Ruby version**: v3.4.1
rails/rails#54200.title: CSP hash-source is being ignored by browser

rails/rails#54335.body:
I've been using ActiveSupport's `Enumerator#sole` method recently and noticed something unfortunate about the implementation:

```ruby
  def sole
    case count
    when 1   then return first # rubocop:disable Style/RedundantReturn
    when 0   then raise SoleItemExpectedError, "no item found"
    when 2.. then raise SoleItemExpectedError, "multiple items found"
    end
  end
```

Unless the specific object has a more efficient implementation, calling `count` results in `enum`erating the entire collection. If there is only one item, it's re-enumerated when calling `first`. For in-memory collections this is mostly not so bad.

Although, if one does something extremely silly it becomes noticeable:

```ruby
puts Benchmark.measure {  [].to_enum.sole rescue nil }.utime
# => 5.800000000277805e-05

a = Array.new(1_000_000) # this is very silly
puts Benchmark.measure {  a.to_enum.sole rescue nil }.utime
# => 0.02890700000000379
```

But the real problem arose when used on a collection that's backed by dynamically fetching resources from an API endpoint. This is problematic for a couple reasons:

* Two requests instead of one.
* The second enumeration could have a different set of results.

Another unfortunate side-effect of using `count` under the covers is `sole` hangs forever when given an infinite sequence. (e.g. `[1].cycle.sole`)

I haven't thought this all the way through, but something like the following seems to have the same qualities as the current implementation but only pulls at most 2 items from the collection:

```ruby
    def sole
      result = nil
      found = false

      self.each_entry do |entry|
        if found
          raise SoleItemExpectedError, "multiple items found"
        end

        result = entry
        found = true
      end

      return result if found

      raise SoleItemExpectedError, "no item found"
    end
```
rails/rails#54335.title: Enumerable#sole is inefficient

rails/rails#54444.body:
### Steps to reproduce
<!-- (Guidelines for creating a bug report are [available
here](https://edgeguides.rubyonrails.org/contributing_to_ruby_on_rails.html#creating-a-bug-report)) -->

<!-- Paste your executable test case created from one of the scripts found [here](https://edgeguides.rubyonrails.org/contributing_to_ruby_on_rails.html#create-an-executable-test-case) below: -->
```ruby
# frozen_string_literal: true

require "bundler/inline"

gemfile(true) do
  source "https://rubygems.org"

  gem "rails"
  # If you want to test against edge Rails replace the previous line with this:
  # gem "rails", github: "rails/rails", branch: "main"
end

require "active_job/railtie"
require "minitest/autorun"

class TestApp < Rails::Application
  config.load_defaults Rails::VERSION::STRING.to_f
  config.eager_load = false
  config.secret_key_base = "secret_key_base"
  config.active_job.queue_adapter = :test

  config.logger = Logger.new($stdout)
end
Rails.application.initialize!

class FooJob < ActiveJob::Base
  queue_as :high

  def perform(val:)
    retry_job(queue: :low) if queue_name.to_s == 'high'
  end
end

ActiveSupport::Notifications.monotonic_subscribe('perform.active_job') do |event|
  job = event.payload[:job]
  p "queue_name: #{job.queue_name}"
end

class FooJobTest < ActiveJob::TestCase
  def test_stuff
    FooJob.perform_later(val: 1)
    perform_enqueued_jobs
    perform_enqueued_jobs
  end
end
```

```
  INFO -- : Enqueued FooJob (Job ID: fa710ac3-5a45-4e85-b957-ff05876626aa) to Test(high) with arguments: {:val=>1}
  INFO -- : Performing FooJob (Job ID: fa710ac3-5a45-4e85-b957-ff05876626aa) from Test(high) enqueued at 2025-02-05T23:45:25.162898000Z with arguments: {:val=>1}
  INFO -- : Enqueued FooJob (Job ID: fa710ac3-5a45-4e85-b957-ff05876626aa) to Test(low) with arguments: {:val=>1}
  INFO -- : Retrying FooJob (Job ID: fa710ac3-5a45-4e85-b957-ff05876626aa) after 1 attempts in 0 seconds.
"queue_name: low"
  INFO -- : Performed FooJob (Job ID: fa710ac3-5a45-4e85-b957-ff05876626aa) from Test(low) in 1.44ms
  INFO -- : Performing FooJob (Job ID: fa710ac3-5a45-4e85-b957-ff05876626aa) from Test(low) enqueued at 2025-02-05T23:45:25.164454000Z with arguments: {:val=>1}
"queue_name: low"
  INFO -- : Performed FooJob (Job ID: fa710ac3-5a45-4e85-b957-ff05876626aa) from Test(low) in 0.03ms
```

### Expected behavior
<!-- Tell us what should happen -->
The first execution should print `high`. The second (retry) execution should print `low`

Logs should also print `Test(high)` correctly (`Performed FooJob...` line)

### Actual behavior
<!-- Tell us what happens instead -->
Both job executions print `low`. Logs doesn't print queue_name correctly for first run

I believe it's due to the instrumentation code passing `self` in the payload
https://github.com/rails/rails/blob/cf6ff17e9a3c6c1139040b519a341f55f0be16cf/activejob/lib/active_job/instrumentation.rb#L36

And later `retry_job` calls `enqueue` which update the job's attribute
https://github.com/rails/rails/blob/ad0105c13f61d145a659004efb928a643104e973/activejob/lib/active_job/enqueuing.rb#L113

So essentially anything we passed into `retry_job` will be reflected in the instrumentation, not just queue name

### System configuration
**Rails version**: 8.0.1

**Ruby version**: 3.2.6
rails/rails#54444.title: ActiveJob's `retry_job` causes incorrect instrumentation when passed in parameters

rails/rails#54483.body:
### Steps to reproduce
<!-- (Guidelines for creating a bug report are [available
here](https://edgeguides.rubyonrails.org/contributing_to_ruby_on_rails.html#creating-a-bug-report)) -->

<!-- Paste your executable test case created from one of the scripts found [here](https://edgeguides.rubyonrails.org/contributing_to_ruby_on_rails.html#create-an-executable-test-case) below: -->
```ruby
# frozen_string_literal: true

require "bundler/inline"

gemfile(true) do
  source "https://rubygems.org"

  gem "rails"

  gem "sqlite3"
end

require "active_record/railtie"
require "minitest/autorun"

# This connection will do for database-independent bug reports.
ENV["DATABASE_URL"] = "sqlite3::memory:"

class TestApp < Rails::Application
  config.load_defaults Rails::VERSION::STRING.to_f
  config.eager_load = false
  config.logger = Logger.new($stdout)
  config.secret_key_base = "secret_key_base"

  config.active_record.encryption.primary_key = "primary_key"
  config.active_record.encryption.deterministic_key = "deterministic_key"
  config.active_record.encryption.key_derivation_salt = "key_derivation_salt"
end
Rails.application.initialize!

ActiveRecord::Schema.define do
  create_table :posts, force: true do |t|
  end
end

class Post < ActiveRecord::Base
end

class BugTest < ActiveSupport::TestCase
  # This fails and describes my high-level problem
  def test_finds_error_with_callback_options
    post = Post.new

    # this is very similar to what a validate call might add
    error = post.errors.add(:base, :too_short, allow_nil: false, count: 42)

    assert_equal true, post.errors.added?(error.attribute, error.type, error.options)
  end

  # Succeeds because no allow_nil was passed
  def test_finds_error_without_callback_options
    post = Post.new

    error = post.errors.add(:base, :too_short, count: 42)

    assert_equal true, post.errors.added?(error.attribute, error.type, error.options)
  end

  # This is the culprit for my higher level problem
  def test_error_with_callback_options_strictly_matches_itself
    post = Post.new

    error = post.errors.add(:base, :too_short, allow_nil: false, count: 42)
    assert_equal true, error.strict_match?(error.attribute, error.type, **error.options)
  end

  # Success case for culprit, because of missing allow_nil
  def test_error_without_callback_options_strictly_matches_itself
    post = Post.new

    error = post.errors.add(:base, :too_short, count: 42)
    assert_equal true, error.strict_match?(error.attribute, error.type, **error.options)
  end
end
```

### Expected behavior
Checking for presence of an `ActiveModel::Error` using `added?(e.attribute, e.type, e.options)` should always work, because I literally pass in the interface that was used to create the error.

### Actual behavior
It doesn't find the error in some cases, because some options are treated special by `ActiveModel::Error#strict_match?`, which is implemented like

```
options == @options.except(*CALLBACKS_OPTIONS + MESSAGE_OPTIONS)
```

This means I can create an `Error` with options that I will later not be able to validate during `strict_match?`, even when comparing the error to itself.

One possible fix that comes to my mind would be:

```ruby
options.except(*CALLBACKS_OPTIONS + MESSAGE_OPTIONS) == @options.except(*CALLBACKS_OPTIONS + MESSAGE_OPTIONS)
```

Though written that way it doesn't look too nice.

### Additional notes

I used `allow_nil` for my examples, but other validation options such as `if` or `unless` are affected the same way.

### System configuration

**Rails version**: 8.0 (but also at least 7.1)

**Ruby version**: 3.3.6 (unrelated)
rails/rails#54483.title: ActiveModel::Errors#added? doesn't work when certain options were present during validation

<!--
Thanks for contributing to Rails!

Please do not make *Draft* pull requests, as they still send
notifications to everyone watching the Rails repo.

Create a pull request when it is ready for review and feedback
from the Rails team :).

If your pull request affects documentation or any non-code
changes, guidelines for those changes are [available
here](https://edgeguides.rubyonrails.org/contributing_to_ruby_on_rails.html#contributing-to-the-rails-documentation)

Feel free to discard it if you need to (e.g. when you just fix a typo). -->

### Motivation / Background

This Pull Request has been created to solve the code block overflow mentioned in this discussion #54165.

potential solution for the following discussions as well:

2. https://github.com/rails/rails/discussions/52884

The CSS regression was introduced in the PR #52686, I adjusted the CSS classes to the specific `kindle` class to avoid the overflow of the code block.

rails/rails#54591.body:
### Steps to reproduce

<!-- (Guidelines for creating a bug report are [available
here](https://edgeguides.rubyonrails.org/contributing_to_ruby_on_rails.html#creating-a-bug-report)) -->

rails/rails#54830.body:
### Steps to reproduce

```ruby
require "bundler/inline"

gemfile(true) do
  source "https://rubygems.org"

  git_source(:github) { |repo| "https://github.com/#{repo}.git" }

  gem "rails"
  gem "sqlite3"
end

require "active_record"
require "logger"
require "minitest/autorun"

# This connection will do for database-independent bug reports.
ActiveRecord::Base.establish_connection(adapter: "sqlite3", database: ":memory:")
# activate to see sql statement
ActiveRecord::Base.logger = Logger.new(STDOUT)

ActiveRecord::Schema.define do
  create_table :tables
end

class Table < ActiveRecord::Base
  after_save do
    raise_validation_error
  end
end

class BugTest < ActiveSupport::TestCase
  def test_find_or_create_by
    assert_equal false, Table.create.persisted?
    assert_equal false, Table.find_or_create_by({}).persisted?
  end
end
```

### Expected behavior

both 'create' and 'find_or_create_by' should not persist the object

### Actual behavior

'find_or_create_by' persist the object which was not the case in Rails 7.0. Looks like it's a very specific case where a RecordInvalid is raised in a after_.. callback. 'create' is now run in a subtransaction that is not rollback

### System configuration

**Rails version**: 8.0.2

**Ruby version**: 3.3.4
rails/rails#54830.title: find_or_create_by in Rails 7.1 can persist invalid object

rails/rails#54870.body:
### Steps to reproduce

1. Set ActiveStorage to proxy mode:

```ruby
Rails.application.config.active_storage.resolve_model_to_route = :rails_storage_proxy
```

2. Load a file and check the response headers,

### Expected behavior

Content-Length should be included

### Actual behavior

 Content-Length is not included

### System configuration

**Rails version**: 7.2

**Ruby version**: 3.2.2


KeyCDN requires the Content-Length header to be set ([see here](https://www.keycdn.com/support/frequently-asked-questions#why-do-my-files-not-get-cached-why-is-my-miss-ratio-high)). Without this header, it's not possible to use Rails with KeyCDN.
rails/rails#54870.title: ActiveStorage `:rails_storage_proxy` does not include Content-Length header

rails/rails#54894.body:
### Steps to reproduce

Generate a new app with Rails 7.1 and JRuby. The bundle will attempt to lock to the native platform (rather than the "universal-java" platform JRuby uses) preventing later gem installs from finding a match.

```
$ rails new demo
      create  
      create  README.md
      create  Rakefile
...
Resolving dependencies...
Fetching io-console 0.8.0 (java)
Fetching selenium-webdriver 4.31.0
Fetching rdoc 6.13.1
...
         run  bundle lock --add-platform=x86_64-linux
Writing lockfile to /Users/headius/work/jruby/demo/Gemfile.lock
Fetching gem metadata from https://rubygems.org/.........
Could not find gems matching 'activerecord-jdbcsqlite3-adapter' valid for all
resolution platforms (universal-java-21, x86_64-linux) in rubygems repository
https://rubygems.org/ or installed locally.
...
```

### Expected behavior

The application should generate and successfully resolve all dependencies.

### Actual behavior

Because the bundle is locked with a native platform, the `-java` gems for JRuby's ActiveRecord-JDBC adapters do not resolve successfully.

### System configuration

**Rails version**: 7.1.0

**Ruby version**: JRuby 10 (Ruby 3.4 compatible)
rails/rails#54894.title: Bundle lock to native platform breaks JRuby bundling

rails/rails#54920.body:
### Steps to reproduce

<!-- (Guidelines for creating a bug report are [available
here](https://edgeguides.rubyonrails.org/contributing_to_ruby_on_rails.html#creating-a-bug-report)) -->

<!-- Paste your executable test case created from one of the scripts found [here](https://edgeguides.rubyonrails.org/contributing_to_ruby_on_rails.html#create-an-executable-test-case) below: -->
```ruby
require "bundler/inline"

gemfile(true) do
  source "https://rubygems.org"
  gem "rails", github: "rails/rails", branch: "main"
  gem "sqlite3"
end

require "active_record"
require "minitest/autorun"
require "logger"

# This connection will do for database-independent bug reports
ActiveRecord::Base.establish_connection(adapter: "sqlite3", database: ":memory:")
ActiveRecord::Base.logger = Logger.new(STDOUT)

ActiveRecord::Schema.define do
  create_table :authors do |t|
    t.string :name
    t.timestamps
  end

  create_table :books do |t|
    t.string :name
    t.references :author
    t.timestamps
  end
end

class Author < ActiveRecord::Base
  has_many :books
end

class Book < ActiveRecord::Base
  belongs_to :author
end

class BugTest < Minitest::Test
  def test_find_or_create_by_with_destroyed_owner
    author = Author.create!(name: "Test Author")
    author.destroy!

    assert_raises(ActiveRecord::RecordNotSaved) do
      author.books.create_or_find_by!(name: "Test Book")
    end
  end
end
```

### Expected behavior

A `ActiveRecord::RecordNotSaved` is raised with `You cannot call create unless the parent is saved`. I'd expect `create_or_find_by` to act like `create` and raise this error.

### Actual behavior

The new record is created. 

### System configuration

**Rails version**: 
Rails main since https://github.com/rails/rails/pull/54845

cc: @Edouard-chin
rails/rails#54920.title: `create_or_find_by` creates record when parent is not persisted

rails/rails#54921.body:
I've recently been trying to reduce the amount of unnecessary `updated_at` updates when uploading using `ActiveStorage`, and found the amazing option `touch_attachment_records` introduced here: https://github.com/rails/rails/pull/49723

And it seemed to do exactly what it advertised, except only after a `reload!`.
It is because `ActiveStorage::Attachment` runs before `ActiveStorage::Engine` when `eager_load` is `true` -- and `eager_load` is very often `true`.

It is quite easy reproduce thanks to the example scripts 🙏 

### Steps to reproduce

```ruby
# frozen_string_literal: true

require "bundler/inline"

gemfile(true) do
  source "https://rubygems.org"

  gem "rails"
  # If you want to test against edge Rails replace the previous line with this:
  # gem "rails", github: "rails/rails", branch: "main"

  gem "sqlite3"
end

require "active_record/railtie"
require "active_storage/engine"
require "minitest/autorun"

ENV["DATABASE_URL"] = "sqlite3::memory:"

class TestApp < Rails::Application
  config.load_defaults Rails::VERSION::STRING.to_f

  config.root = __dir__
  config.hosts << "example.org"

  config.eager_load = true
  
  config.session_store :cookie_store, key: "cookie_store_key"
  config.secret_key_base = "secret_key_base"

  config.logger = Logger.new($stdout)
  Rails.logger = config.logger

  config.active_storage.touch_attachment_records = false
  config.active_storage.service = :local
  config.active_storage.service_configurations = {
    local: {
      root: Dir.tmpdir,
      service: "Disk"
    }
  }
end

Rails.application.initialize!

require ActiveStorage::Engine.root.join("db/migrate/20170806125915_create_active_storage_tables.rb").to_s

ActiveRecord::Schema.define do
  CreateActiveStorageTables.new.change

  create_table :users, force: true do |t|
    t.timestamps null: false
  end
end

class User < ActiveRecord::Base
  has_one_attached :profile
end

class BugTest < ActiveSupport::TestCase
  def test_touch_attachment_records
    user = User.create!(
      profile: {
        content_type: "text/plain",
        filename: "dummy.txt",
        io: ::StringIO.new("dummy"),
      }
    )
    assert_equal(ActiveStorage::Attachment.reflect_on_association(:record).options[:touch], false)
  end

  def test_updated_at
    user = User.create!
    updated_at = user.updated_at
    sleep(0.5)
    user.profile.attach(
      content_type: "text/plain",
      filename: "dummy.txt",
      io: ::StringIO.new("dummy"),
    )

    assert_equal(user.updated_at, updated_at)
  end
end
```

### Expected/Actual behavior

I expect both tests to pass when `eager_load` is either true or false.
But it passes only when `eager_load` is false.

If this is expected, are there any docs that point to a Rails way to reload specific models in `after_initialize`?

### System configuration

**Rails version**: Rails 7.2.2.1

**Ruby version**: `ruby 3.3.5 (2024-09-03 revision ef084cc8f4) [arm64-darwin24]`
rails/rails#54921.title: `touch_attachment_records` doesn't play nice with `eager_load`

rails/rails#54966.body:
### Steps to reproduce

```ruby
# frozen_string_literal: true

require "bundler/inline"

gemfile(true) do
  source "https://rubygems.org"

  gem "rails", "8.0.2"
  # If you want to test against edge Rails replace the previous line with this:
  # gem "rails", github: "rails/rails", branch: "main"

  gem "sqlite3"
end

require "active_record/railtie"
require "action_view/railtie"
require "minitest/autorun"

# This connection will do for database-independent bug reports.
ENV["DATABASE_URL"] = "sqlite3::memory:"

class TestApp < Rails::Application
  config.load_defaults Rails::VERSION::STRING.to_f
  config.eager_load = false
  config.logger = Logger.new($stdout)
  config.secret_key_base = "secret_key_base"

  config.active_record.encryption.primary_key = "primary_key"
  config.active_record.encryption.deterministic_key = "deterministic_key"
  config.active_record.encryption.key_derivation_salt = "key_derivation_salt"
end
Rails.application.initialize!

ActiveRecord::Schema.define do
  create_table :posts, force: true do |t|
  end
end

class Post < ActiveRecord::Base
end

class BugTest < ActionView::TestCase
  def test_stuff

    render inline: <<~ERB, locals: { options: [Post] }
      <%= select(nil, "class", options) %>
    ERB

    assert_equal "Post", Post.to_s
    assert_equal <<~HTML, rendered.to_s
      <select name="class" id="class"><option value="Post">Post</option></select>
    HTML
  end
end
```

### Expected behavior

Since I'm passing a unidimensional array, I would expect:
1. The helper to not detect that I'm passing group_choices
2. To render my option of Class type using `to_s`

See the test above

### Actual behavior

[`group_choices?`](https://github.com/rails/rails/blob/22a61df687a5408a37ed0b9dcf73c08b250dc9be/actionview/lib/action_view/helpers/tags/select.rb#L39-L40) ends up calling [`Post.second`](https://github.com/rails/rails/blob/22a61df687a5408a37ed0b9dcf73c08b250dc9be/activerecord/lib/active_record/querying.rb#L7), triggering a DB load, resulting in a nil object:

```
I, [2025-04-24T09:23:50.508624 #88242]  INFO -- : -------------------
I, [2025-04-24T09:23:50.508650 #88242]  INFO -- : BugTest: test_stuff
I, [2025-04-24T09:23:50.508658 #88242]  INFO -- : -------------------
D, [2025-04-24T09:23:50.516487 #88242] DEBUG -- :   Rendering inline template
D, [2025-04-24T09:23:50.523872 #88242] DEBUG -- :   ^[[1m^[[36mPost Load (0.1ms)^[[0m  ^[[1m^[[34mSELECT "posts".* FROM "posts" ORDER BY "posts"."id" ASC LIMIT ? OFFSET ?^[[0m  [["LIMIT", 1], ["OFFSET", 1]]
D, [2025-04-24T09:23:50.526037 #88242] DEBUG -- :   ^[[1m^[[36mPost Load (0.1ms)^[[0m  ^[[1m^[[34mSELECT "posts".* FROM "posts" ORDER BY "posts"."id" ASC LIMIT ?^[[0m  [["LIMIT", 1]]
D, [2025-04-24T09:23:50.527187 #88242] DEBUG -- :   ^[[1m^[[36mPost Load (0.0ms)^[[0m  ^[[1m^[[34mSELECT "posts".* FROM "posts" ORDER BY "posts"."id" DESC LIMIT ?^[[0m  [["LIMIT", 1]]
I, [2025-04-24T09:23:50.527786 #88242]  INFO -- :   Rendered inline template (Duration: 10.8ms | GC: 0.0ms)

  1) Failure:
BugTest#test_stuff [bug.rb:53]:
--- expected
+++ actual
@@ -1,2 +1,2 @@
-"<select name=\"class\" id=\"class\"><option value=\"Post\">Post</option></select>
+"<select name=\"class\" id=\"class\"><option selected=\"selected\" value=\"\"></option></select>
 "
```

If the table isn't defined, it's even worse, it crashes with `no such table: posts:`

I understand that calling the form helper with a Class may not be supported, but I don't think the form helper should be calling `.second` without checking the type first.

### System configuration

**Rails version**: 
8.0.2

**Ruby version**: 
3.3.4
rails/rails#54966.title: ActionView::Helpers::Tags::Select's group_choices detection can unknowingly end up calling the `second` method

rails/rails#55045.body:
### Steps to reproduce
It's currently happening to me with the solid_cable database, but that's because it's the only one I've set up in development other than the main one.

```yml
default: &default
  adapter: sqlite3
  pool: <%= ENV.fetch("RAILS_MAX_THREADS") { 5 } %>
  timeout: 5000

development:
  primary:
    <<: *default
    database: storage/development.sqlite3
  cable:
    <<: *default
    database: storage/development_cable.sqlite3
    migrations_paths: db/cable_migrate
```

1. Create a migration and run it with `db:migrate`.
2. Change that migration and run `db:migrate:reset`.

### Expected behavior
Primary database must be recreated by reapplying existing migrations without using the schema. The solid_cable database, since it has no migrations in its directory, must be recreated using the schema.


### Actual behavior
Primary database is working correctly, but the solid_cable database is created empty and its schema is updated to:

```ruby
# db/cable_squema.rb
ActiveRecord::Schema[8.1].define(version: 0) do
end
```

### System configuration
**Rails version**: 8.1.0.alpha 730367e
**Ruby version**: ruby 3.4.2 (2025-02-15 revision d2930f8e7a) +PRISM [arm64-darwin24]

Related issues: rails/solid_queue#540 rails/rails#52829

rails/rails#55078.body:
### Steps to reproduce

<!-- (Guidelines for creating a bug report are [available
here](https://edgeguides.rubyonrails.org/contributing_to_ruby_on_rails.html#creating-a-bug-report)) -->

<!-- Paste your executable test case created from one of the scripts found [here](https://edgeguides.rubyonrails.org/contributing_to_ruby_on_rails.html#create-an-executable-test-case) below: -->
```ruby
# frozen_string_literal: true

require "bundler/inline"

gemfile(true) do
  source "https://rubygems.org"

  gem "rails"
  gem "sqlite3"
end

require "active_record/railtie"
require "minitest/autorun"

# This connection will do for database-independent bug reports.
ENV["DATABASE_URL"] = "sqlite3::memory:"

class TestApp < Rails::Application
  config.load_defaults Rails::VERSION::STRING.to_f
  config.eager_load = false
  config.logger = Logger.new($stdout)
  config.secret_key_base = "secret_key_base"

  config.active_record.encryption.primary_key = "primary_key"
  config.active_record.encryption.deterministic_key = "deterministic_key"
  config.active_record.encryption.key_derivation_salt = "key_derivation_salt"
end
Rails.application.initialize!

ActiveRecord::Schema.define do
  create_table :users do |t|
    t.timestamps
  end

  create_table :books do |t|
    t.references :user, foreign_key: true, type: :bigint
    t.references :author, foreign_key: { to_table: :users }, type: :bigint
    t.timestamps
  end
end


ActiveSupport::TestCase.test_order = :sorted
class BugTest < ActiveSupport::TestCase
  # To check the table definition
  def test_definition
    definition = ActiveRecord::Base.connection.execute("select * from sqlite_master;").detect {|obj| obj['tbl_name'] == 'books' }['sql']

    assert_equal <<~SQL.strip, definition
      CREATE TABLE "books" ("id" integer PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT NOT NULL, "user_id" bigint, "author_id" bigint, "created_at" datetime(6) NOT NULL, "updated_at" datetime(6) NOT NULL, CONSTRAINT "fk_rails_bc582ddd02"
      FOREIGN KEY ("user_id")
        REFERENCES "users" ("id")
      , CONSTRAINT "fk_rails_53d51ce16a"
      FOREIGN KEY ("author_id")
        REFERENCES "users" ("id")
      )
    SQL
  end

  def test_remove_foreign_key
    ActiveRecord::Schema.define do
      # If you swap the following two lines, the test will pass.
      remove_foreign_key :books, :users
      remove_foreign_key :books, :users, column: :author_id
    end

    definition = ActiveRecord::Base.connection.execute("select * from sqlite_master;").detect {|obj| obj['tbl_name'] == 'books' }['sql']

    assert_equal <<~SQL.strip, definition
      CREATE TABLE "books" ("id" integer PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT NOT NULL, "user_id" bigint, "author_id" bigint, "created_at" datetime(6) NOT NULL, "updated_at" datetime(6) NOT NULL)
    SQL
  end
end
```


### Expected behavior

It removes each foreign keys.

### Actual behavior

<!-- Tell us what happens instead -->
It raises the error.
And it removes foreign key for `author_id` in `remove_foreign_key :books, :users`.

```
-- remove_foreign_key(:books, :users, {column: :author_id})
E

Finished in 0.034989s, 57.1608 runs/s, 28.5804 assertions/s.

  1) Error:
BugTest#test_remove_foreign_key:
ArgumentError: Table 'books' has no foreign key for users
```


### System configuration

**Rails version**: 
8.0.2 / main

**Ruby version**: 
3.4.1

rails/rails#55116.body:
### Steps to reproduce

<!-- (Guidelines for creating a bug report are [available
here](https://edgeguides.rubyonrails.org/contributing_to_ruby_on_rails.html#creating-a-bug-report)) -->

<!-- Paste your executable test case created from one of the scripts found [here](https://edgeguides.rubyonrails.org/contributing_to_ruby_on_rails.html#create-an-executable-test-case) below: -->
```ruby
# frozen_string_literal: true

require "bundler/inline"

gemfile(true) do
  source "https://rubygems.org"

  gem "rails", github: "rails/rails", branch: "main"
  gem "mocha"
end

require "active_job/railtie"
require "minitest/autorun"
require "mocha/minitest" 

class TestApp < Rails::Application
  config.load_defaults Rails::VERSION::STRING.to_f
  config.eager_load = false
  config.secret_key_base = "secret_key_base"
  config.active_job.queue_adapter = :test

  config.logger = Logger.new($stdout)
end
Rails.application.initialize!

class FooJob < ActiveJob::Base
  retry_on StandardError, queue: :default, attempts: 10

  before_enqueue do
    puts "before_enqueue result: #{@result}"
    puts "before_enqueue count: #{@count}"
    if @result.present?
      @result = ""
      save_progress
    end
  rescue
    @count = 0
    raise
  end

  def perform
    @result ||= ""
    @count ||= 0
    (3-@count).times do |i|
      @result += "x "
      puts "perform result: #{@result}"
      interrupt if @count == 1
      @count += 1
    end
  end

  def save_progress
    puts "saving!"
  end

  def interrupt
    puts "interrupt"
    retry_job
  end

  def deserialize(job_data)
    @count = job_data["count"]
    @result = job_data["result"]
    super
  end

  def serialize
    super.merge("count" => @count, "result" => @result)
  end
end

class FooJobTest < ActiveJob::TestCase
  def test_job_state_is_preserved_during_retry
    FooJob.any_instance.stubs(:save_progress).raises(StandardError, 'failed to save progress')

    assert_nothing_raised do
      FooJob.perform_later
      perform_enqueued_jobs
    end
  end
end
```

### Expected behavior

When job gets "interrupted" and re-enqueued at count = 1, the `before_enqueue` block will reset the count to 0 when an exception occurs.  

```
I, [2025-05-24T21:55:16.770459 #88347]  INFO -- : ----------------------------------------------------
I, [2025-05-24T21:55:16.770492 #88347]  INFO -- : FooJobTest: test_job_state_is_preserved_during_retry
I, [2025-05-24T21:55:16.770498 #88347]  INFO -- : ----------------------------------------------------
before_enqueue result: 
before_enqueue count: 
I, [2025-05-24T21:55:16.771251 #88347]  INFO -- : Enqueued FooJob (Job ID: f64ef6c8-54ab-41dd-a5fd-7d69693396ba) to Test(default)
I, [2025-05-24T21:55:16.772177 #88347]  INFO -- : Performing FooJob (Job ID: f64ef6c8-54ab-41dd-a5fd-7d69693396ba) from Test(default) enqueued at 2025-05-25T01:55:16.771216000Z
perform result: x 
perform result: x x 
interrupt
before_enqueue result: x x 
before_enqueue count: 1
E, [2025-05-24T21:55:16.772340 #88347] ERROR -- : Failed enqueuing FooJob to Test(default): StandardError (failed to save progress)
I, [2025-05-24T21:55:16.772355 #88347]  INFO -- : Retrying FooJob (Job ID: f64ef6c8-54ab-41dd-a5fd-7d69693396ba) after 1 attempts in 0 seconds.
before_enqueue result: 
before_enqueue count: 0
I, [2025-05-24T21:55:16.772495 #88347]  INFO -- : Enqueued FooJob (Job ID: f64ef6c8-54ab-41dd-a5fd-7d69693396ba) to Test(default) at 2025-05-25 01:55:20 UTC
I, [2025-05-24T21:55:16.772508 #88347]  INFO -- : Retrying FooJob (Job ID: f64ef6c8-54ab-41dd-a5fd-7d69693396ba) after 1 attempts in 3 seconds, due to a StandardError (failed to save progress).
I, [2025-05-24T21:55:16.772524 #88347]  INFO -- : Performed FooJob (Job ID: f64ef6c8-54ab-41dd-a5fd-7d69693396ba) from Test(default) in 0.36ms
```

### Actual behavior

`before_enqueue` does not reset the count to 0.

```
before_enqueue result: 
before_enqueue count: 
I, [2025-05-24T22:10:24.671247 #6354]  INFO -- : Enqueued FooJob (Job ID: 813471e0-97b4-4e32-b499-ca5434c3a036) to Test(default)
I, [2025-05-24T22:10:24.672472 #6354]  INFO -- : Performing FooJob (Job ID: 813471e0-97b4-4e32-b499-ca5434c3a036) from Test(default) enqueued at 2025-05-25T02:10:24.671218000Z
perform result: x 
perform result: x x 
interrupt
before_enqueue result: x x 
before_enqueue count: 1
E, [2025-05-24T22:10:24.672622 #6354] ERROR -- : Failed enqueuing FooJob to Test(default): StandardError (failed to save progress)
I, [2025-05-24T22:10:24.672636 #6354]  INFO -- : Retrying FooJob (Job ID: 813471e0-97b4-4e32-b499-ca5434c3a036) after 1 attempts in 0 seconds.
before_enqueue result: x x 
before_enqueue count: 1
E, [2025-05-24T22:10:24.672780 #6354] ERROR -- : Failed enqueuing FooJob to Test(default): StandardError (failed to save progress)
```

This is because now in `retry_job`, we first dup the job and enqueue the duped job. When an error is raised in `before_enqueue`, any modifications to the job in the `before_enqueue` will be lost since the `retry_job` after will dup the previous job instance and not the duped job instance.

### System configuration

Rails main, after https://github.com/rails/rails/pull/54447

Note: the interrupting feature here is simulating what the [job-iteration](https://github.com/Shopify/job-iteration) gem does, it allows the job to be interrupted and retried.

cc: @fatkodima
rails/rails#55116.title: Error in `before_enqueue` callback does not persist job state

rails/rails#55118.body:
### Steps to reproduce

```ruby
# frozen_string_literal: true

require "bundler/inline"

gemfile(true) do
  source "https://rubygems.org"

  gem "rails"
  gem "sqlite3"
end

require "active_record/railtie"
require "minitest/autorun"

# This connection will do for database-independent bug reports.
ENV["DATABASE_URL"] = "sqlite3::memory:"

class TestApp < Rails::Application
  config.load_defaults Rails::VERSION::STRING.to_f
  config.eager_load = false
  config.logger = Logger.new($stdout)
  config.secret_key_base = "secret_key_base"

  config.active_record.encryption.primary_key = "primary_key"
  config.active_record.encryption.deterministic_key = "deterministic_key"
  config.active_record.encryption.key_derivation_salt = "key_derivation_salt"

  config.time_zone = "Asia/Tokyo"
  config.active_record.default_timezone = :utc
end
Rails.application.initialize!

ActiveRecord::Schema.define do
  create_table :users, force: true do |t|
    t.time :created_at
  end
end

class User < ActiveRecord::Base
end

class BugTest < ActiveSupport::TestCase
  def test_time_attribute_changed
    user = User.create! created_at: "08:00"
    user.reload

    user.created_at
    # => 2000-01-02 08:00:00.000000000 JST +09:00

    user.created_at = "08:00"
    user.created_at
    # => 2000-01-01 08:00:00.000000000 JST +09:00

    assert_equal [], user.changed_attribute_names_to_save
    # => ["created_at"]  ← unexpected!
  end
end
```

Although the same `"08:00"` value is assigned again, Active Record considers the attribute as changed.

---

### Expected behavior

The `created_at` attribute should not be marked as changed when assigning the same time string (`"08:00"`) again.
This should hold true even when the application and database time zones differ, and internal `Time` objects have different date parts.

---

### Actual behavior

Assigning `"08:00"` again results in `:created_at` being marked as changed.

This happens because:

```ruby
user.created_at.to_s
# => "2000-01-01 08:00:00 +0900"

user.created_at.utc.to_s
# => "1999-12-31 23:00:00 UTC"
```

Due to the timezone conversion, the internal `Time` object has a different date, triggering false positives in dirty tracking.

---

### Proposal

I propose introducing a config option such as:

```ruby
config.active_record.use_fixed_date_for_time_attributes = true
```

When enabled, Active Record would normalize `time`-typed values to a fixed date (e.g., `2000-01-01`) internally.
This would eliminate unnecessary dirty tracking caused by timezone-induced date mismatches, while preserving timezone support and default behavior unless explicitly enabled.

Would the Rails team be open to such a feature?

---

### System configuration

**Rails version**: 7.x.x
**Ruby version**: 3.x.x
rails/rails#55118.title: time attributes falsely marked as changed due to date shift on timezone conversion

rails/rails#55126.body:
We encounter an issue in our Ruby environments that handle very low volumes of traffic. The first query after the worker has been idle for a long time raises 'Trilogy::SSLError: trilogy_query_recv: SSL Error: unexpected eof while reading'. After reconnecting subsequent queries work without issue.

We currently have some custom code to catch these exceptions, reconnect and try the work again. But similar code is already present in rails.

I believe this logic in rails is not used at the moment because the exception is not recognized as indicating a 'retryable connection error'.
In the Trilogy Ruby bindings there is some logic to translate Trilogy exceptions to standard active record exceptions. These translated values are then checked in the calling (rails) code to see whether a database statement can be retried if it raised the exception.

Looking at the code in [ActiveRecord::ConnectionAdapters::TrilogyAdapter.translate_exception()](https://github.com/rails/rails/blob/b7520a13adda46c0cc5f3fb4a4c1726633af2bba/activerecord/lib/active_record/connection_adapters/trilogy_adapter.rb#L178) I believe this error should also be translated to a ActiveRecord::ConnectionFailed exception (similar to a Trilogy::EOFError).
This would probably remove the need for our custom rescue/reconnect logic.

Is this analysis correct, would it make sense to also translate SSLErrors that way or am I misunderstanding the meaning/logic here?


### Expected behavior

That exception should be seen as a 'retryable connection error' (e.g. an ActiveRecord::ConnectionFailed) allowing the query to be retried

[ActiveRecord::ConnectionAdapters::TrilogyAdapter.translate_exception()](https://github.com/rails/rails/blob/b7520a13adda46c0cc5f3fb4a4c1726633af2bba/activerecord/lib/active_record/connection_adapters/trilogy_adapter.rb#L178) is aware of 'Trilogy::SSLError: trilogy_query_recv: SSL Error' and translates it to an `ActiveRecord::ConnectionFailed` exception

### Actual behavior

Error is not seen as a connection error and propagates to our application logic. 

### System configuration

**Rails version**: 8.0.2

**Ruby version**: 3.3.7
rails/rails#55126.title: Trilogy::SSLError should be translated to ActiveRecord::ConnectionFailed

rails/rails#55161.body:
### Steps to reproduce Create a file `_partial.html.erb` with the following contents alongside the minimal reproduction shown below: ```erb <%# _partial.html.erb %> <% cache "key" do %>
Hello, world!
<% end %> ``` Minimal reproduction: ``` # frozen_string_literal: true require "bundler/inline" gemfile(true) do source "https://rubygems.org" gem "rails" end require "action_controller/railtie" require "action_view/railtie" require "minitest/autorun" puts "#{Rails::VERSION::STRING.to_f}" class TestApp < Rails::Application config.load_defaults Rails::VERSION::STRING.to_f config.eager_load = false config.logger = Logger.new($stdout) config.secret_key_base = "secret_key_base" config.action_controller.perform_caching = true config.action_controller.enable_fragment_cache_logging = true config.cache_store = :null_store config.paths["app/views"] << File.expand_path(".") end Rails.application.initialize! class BugTest < ActionView::TestCase # this render works fine def test_regular_erb_render render inline: <<~ERB <% cache "key" do %>
Hello, world!
<% end %> ERB assert true end # rendering the same code, but as nested inline render works fine def test_nested_inline_render nested_partial = <<~ERB <% cache "key" do %>
Hello, world!
<% end %> ERB render inline: <<~ERB, locals: { nested_partial: nested_partial } <%= render inline: nested_partial %> ERB assert true end # rendering the same code, but as a partial on the file system fails def test_file_partial render inline: <<~ERB <%= render partial: "partial" %> ERB assert false, "Check for 'Couldn't find template for digesting: flexes/flex' in log output ↑" end end ``` ### Expected behavior Everything should render fine, cache is written, no error messages output. ### Actual behavior When running the third test `test_file_partial` the logs will show an error message: ``` Couldn't find template for digesting: flexes/flex Couldn't find template for digesting: others/other ``` If the string `render` appears anywhere in the partial, anything following will be assumed to be a template name (ie. `flex` in the above example), and Rails will attempt to digest it for the cache key. It also occurs even if another symbol separates the two, like in the Stimulus action `turbo:before-stream-render@document->leaderboard#render` the error message will read "Couldn't find template for digesting: documents/document". This is how I actually found the bug originally—removing the Stimulus action string in my code made the error go away. This only occurs inside of a `cache` block, and only if the template that contains it is nested at least one level deep in partial rendering, and that partial is on the file system (as seen above it wasn't an issue when rendering an inline partial). ### System configuration **Rails version**: 8.0 **Ruby version**: 3.4.2
rails/rails#55161.title: When rendering a partial, any mention of the string "render" inside a `cache` block results in the logged error message "Couldn't find template for digesting: strings/string"

rails/rails#55178.body:
### Steps to reproduce

Since https://github.com/rails/rails/pull/55065, the SQLite adapter uses `total_changes` to count the number of rows affected when performing a query. But [total_changes](https://www.sqlite.org/c3ref/total_changes.html) includes "changes made as part of [foreign key actions](https://www.sqlite.org/foreignkeys.html#fk_actions)", so if there is a cascading delete the number returned is wrong.

cc @jenshenny, @skipkayhil

```ruby
# frozen_string_literal: true

require "bundler/inline"

gemfile(true) do
  source "https://rubygems.org"

  gem "rails", github: "rails/rails", branch: "main"

  gem "sqlite3"
end

require "active_record/railtie"
require "minitest/autorun"

# This connection will do for database-independent bug reports.
ENV["DATABASE_URL"] = "sqlite3::memory:"

class TestApp < Rails::Application
  config.load_defaults Rails::VERSION::STRING.to_f
  config.eager_load = false
  config.logger = Logger.new($stdout)
  config.secret_key_base = "secret_key_base"

  config.active_record.encryption.primary_key = "primary_key"
  config.active_record.encryption.deterministic_key = "deterministic_key"
  config.active_record.encryption.key_derivation_salt = "key_derivation_salt"
end
Rails.application.initialize!

ActiveRecord::Schema.define do
  create_table :posts, force: true do |t|
  end

  create_table :comments, force: true do |t|
    t.references :post, null: false, foreign_key: false
  end

  add_foreign_key "comments", "posts", column: "post_id", on_delete: :cascade
end

class Post < ActiveRecord::Base
  has_many :comments
end

class Comment < ActiveRecord::Base
  belongs_to :post
end

class BugTest < ActiveSupport::TestCase
  def test_it
    post = Post.create!
    comment = post.comments.create!

    assert_equal 1, Post.delete_all
  end
end
```

### Expected behavior

`Post.delete_all` should return 1

### Actual behavior

`Post.delete_all` returns 2

### System configuration

**Rails version**: rails edge (4b7fb1d14b954d2348d2065ff955466479509656)

**Ruby version**: ruby 3.3.5 (2024-09-03 revision ef084cc8f4) [x86_64-linux]
rails/rails#55178.title: The SQLite adapter includes cascaded deletes in row count for delete_all

rails/rails#55183.body:
### Steps to reproduce
```ruby
# frozen_string_literal: true

require "bundler/inline"

gemfile(true) do
  source "https://rubygems.org"

  gem "rails"
  # If you want to test against edge Rails replace the previous line with this:
  # gem "rails", github: "rails/rails", branch: "main"

  gem "sqlite3"
end

require "active_record/railtie"
require "minitest/autorun"

# This connection will do for database-independent bug reports.
ENV["DATABASE_URL"] = "sqlite3::memory:"

class TestApp < Rails::Application
  config.load_defaults Rails::VERSION::STRING.to_f
  config.eager_load = false
  config.logger = Logger.new($stdout)
  config.secret_key_base = "secret_key_base"

  config.active_record.encryption.primary_key = "primary_key"
  config.active_record.encryption.deterministic_key = "deterministic_key"
  config.active_record.encryption.key_derivation_salt = "key_derivation_salt"
end
Rails.application.initialize!

ActiveRecord::Schema.define do
  create_table :posts, force: true do |t|
  end

  create_table :tags, force: true do |t|
  end

  create_join_table :posts, :tags, force: true do |t|
  end
end

class Post < ActiveRecord::Base
  has_and_belongs_to_many :tags
end

class Tag < ActiveRecord::Base
  has_and_belongs_to_many :posts
end

class BugTest < ActiveSupport::TestCase
  def test_habtm_strict_loading_include
    post = Post.create!
    tag = Tag.create!
    post.tags << tag

    assert Post.includes(:tags).first.tags.include?(tag)
    assert_not Post.includes(:tags).first.tags.include?(Tag.new)

    assert Post.includes(:tags).strict_loading.first.tags.include?(tag)
    assert_not Post.includes(:tags).strict_loading.first.tags.include?(Tag.new)
  end
end
```

### Expected behavior

Test passes.

### Actual behavior

The first three assertions succeed, but the last one (checking if an unpersisted model instance is a member of a pre-loaded association) raises a StrictLoadingViolationError. Sample output:
```
ActiveRecord::StrictLoadingViolationError: `Post` is marked for strict_loading. The Post::HABTM_Tags association named `:posts_tags` cannot be lazily loaded.
    /usr/local/rvm/gems/ruby-3.3.6/gems/activerecord-8.0.2/lib/active_record/core.rb:257:in `strict_loading_violation!'
    /usr/local/rvm/gems/ruby-3.3.6/gems/activerecord-8.0.2/lib/active_record/associations/association.rb:250:in `find_target'
    /usr/local/rvm/gems/ruby-3.3.6/gems/activerecord-8.0.2/lib/active_record/associations/collection_association.rb:274:in `load_target'
    /usr/local/rvm/gems/ruby-3.3.6/gems/activerecord-8.0.2/lib/active_record/associations/collection_proxy.rb:45:in `load_target'
    /usr/local/rvm/gems/ruby-3.3.6/gems/activerecord-8.0.2/lib/active_record/associations/collection_proxy.rb:1025:in `records'
    /usr/local/rvm/gems/ruby-3.3.6/gems/activerecord-8.0.2/lib/active_record/relation/delegation.rb:101:in `each'
    /usr/local/rvm/gems/ruby-3.3.6/gems/activerecord-8.0.2/lib/active_record/relation.rb:394:in `any?'
    /usr/local/rvm/gems/ruby-3.3.6/gems/activerecord-8.0.2/lib/active_record/relation.rb:394:in `any?'
    /usr/local/rvm/gems/ruby-3.3.6/gems/activerecord-8.0.2/lib/active_record/associations/collection_association.rb:510:in `include_in_memory?'
    /usr/local/rvm/gems/ruby-3.3.6/gems/activerecord-8.0.2/lib/active_record/associations/collection_association.rb:263:in `include?'
    /usr/local/rvm/gems/ruby-3.3.6/gems/activerecord-8.0.2/lib/active_record/associations/collection_proxy.rb:928:in `include?'
    habtm_strict_bug.rb:62:in `test_habtm_strict_loading_include'
```

This also occurs if the `has_and_belongs_to_many` is changed to a `has_many :through`. 

It's possible to work around this by adding additional associations to the `includes()` call, but it doesn't seem like that should be necessary.

### System configuration

**Rails version**: 8.0.2 (also occurs on `main` and goes back to 7.2 and before)

**Ruby version**: 3.3 in this case, but I don't think it matters much
rails/rails#55183.title: Strict loading error raised incorrectly when checking if HABTM association includes an unpersisted instance

rails/rails#55225.body:
Hi all,

this is actually a follow-up to #35444, which was opened many years ago but unfortunately got stale and then closed.

The password confirmation validation added by `has_secure_password` is skipped if the password string is a sequence of whitespace characters, regardless of the string provided as confirmation, which is ignored with no error messages. This happens both in the latest Rails (currently 8.0.2) and in `main`.

As far as I can tell Rails is committed to support whitespace-only passwords (#25373, #16412), and since they trigger actual changes to the password digest I think it would make sense to align the validation logic to this use case (IMHO it would just make a better default).

I've already taken a quick look at the code; I wasn't able to find any tests documenting the current behavior is intended. But I did find a possible fix, so I volunteer to open a PR should the core team approve the change.

### Steps to reproduce

<!-- (Guidelines for creating a bug report are [available
here](https://edgeguides.rubyonrails.org/contributing_to_ruby_on_rails.html#creating-a-bug-report)) -->

<!-- Paste your executable test case created from one of the scripts found [here](https://edgeguides.rubyonrails.org/contributing_to_ruby_on_rails.html#create-an-executable-test-case) below: -->
```ruby
# frozen_string_literal: true

require "bundler/inline"

gemfile(true) do
  source "https://rubygems.org"
  gem "rails", github: "rails/rails", branch: "main"
  gem "sqlite3"
  gem "bcrypt", "~> 3.1.7"
end

require "active_record/railtie"
require "minitest/autorun"

ENV["DATABASE_URL"] = "sqlite3::memory:"

class TestApp < Rails::Application
  config.load_defaults Rails::VERSION::STRING.to_f
  config.eager_load = false
  config.logger = Logger.new($stdout)
  config.secret_key_base = "secret_key_base"

  config.active_record.encryption.primary_key = "primary_key"
  config.active_record.encryption.deterministic_key = "deterministic_key"
  config.active_record.encryption.key_derivation_salt = "key_derivation_salt"
end
Rails.application.initialize!

ActiveRecord::Schema.define do
  create_table :users, force: true do |t|
    t.string :password_digest, null: false
  end
end

class User < ActiveRecord::Base
  has_secure_password
end

class BugTest < ActiveSupport::TestCase
  test "create new user with whitespace password and wrong password confirmation" do
    user = User.new(password: " ", password_confirmation: "anything")

    assert_not user.valid?(:create), "user should not be valid"
  end

  test "update existing user with whitespace password and wrong password confirmation" do
    user = User.create!(password: "password", password_confirmation: "password")

    user.password = " "
    user.password_confirmation = "anything"

    assert_not user.valid?(:update), "user should not be valid"
  end
end
```

### Expected behavior

The validation should fail if the password and the password confirmation strings are different, no matter if the password is a sequence of whitespace-only characters.

### Actual behavior

If the password is a sequence of whitespace-only characters, the validation succeeds regardless of the contents of the confirmation string.

### System configuration

**Rails version**: 8.0.2, `main@1ac4003547`

**Ruby version**: 3.4.4
rails/rails#55225.title: [`has_secure_password`] Password confirmation validation skipped if password is whitespace-only

rails/rails#55267.body:
I would like to annotate `delete_all`, but it doesn't work. Other calls like `count` do.


### Steps to reproduce

Run the following single file ruby script:

```ruby
require 'bundler/inline'

gemfile do
  source 'https://rubygems.org'
  gem 'activerecord'
  gem 'sqlite3'
  gem 'logger'
end

require 'active_record'
require 'logger'

logger = Logger.new(STDOUT)
logger.formatter = ->(_, _, _, msg) { "#{msg}\n" }
ActiveRecord::Base.logger = logger

ActiveRecord::Base.establish_connection(
  adapter: 'sqlite3',
  database: ':memory:'
)

ActiveRecord::Schema.define do
  create_table :users do |t|
    t.string :name
    t.timestamps
  end
end

class User < ActiveRecord::Base
end

puts "\nTesting annotation with count:"
puts "----------------------------"
puts User.all.annotate('foo').count

puts "\nTesting annotation with delete_all:"
puts "--------------------------------"
puts User.all.annotate('foo').delete_all
```

### Expected behavior

```
User Delete All (0.0ms)  DELETE FROM "users" /* foo */
```

### Actual behavior

```
User Delete All (0.0ms)  DELETE FROM "users"  <-- Note: no annotation!
```

### System configuration

**Rails version**: 8.0.2

**Ruby version**: 3.4.4


I've done a little digging, but I am not certain why this is...
rails/rails#55267.title: ActiveRecord annotate does not work on delete_all

rails/rails#55355.title: RedisAdapterTest#test_reconnections fails with redis v5.4.1

rails/rails#55372.body:
### Steps to reproduce

1. Set `config.active_record.schema_format = :sql` in `application.rb` since we have some stored procedure in our postgres database. Without setting this, the dumped `schema.rb` might be incorrent.
2. since Ruby on Rails 8.0.0.beta1, schema would be loaded first on fresh database. See [https://github.com/rails/rails/blob/8-0-stable/activerecord/CHANGELOG.md#rails-800beta1-september-26-2024](https://github.com/rails/rails/blob/8-0-stable/activerecord/CHANGELOG.md#rails-800beta1-september-26-2024)
3. When installing `solid_*` gems, the generated schema does not include `queue_structure.sql`, `cache_structure.sql`, `cable_structure.sql` files, the database was unable to get migrated properly.

### Expected behavior

The database schema should be created.

### Actual behavior

It leaves an empty database schema.

I don't really think generating the `sql` schema is possible, maybe generating all migrate files like how rails engine does would be a better idea comparing to a single schema file.

### System configuration

**Rails version**: 

8.0

**Ruby version**: 

3.4.4
rails/rails#55372.title: `solid_*` gems were unable to migrate the database properly if `config.active_record.schema_format = :sql` set.

rails/rails#55451.body:
In the Action View Helpers guide, on the `capture` helper section, the `@greeting` variable is declared as follows: ```html+erb <% @greeting = capture do %>

Welcome! The date and time is <%= Time.current %>

<% end %> ``` But the value is then wrongly assumed to be: ``` ruby @greeting # => "Welcome to my shiny new web page! The date and time is 2018-09-06 11:09:16 -0500" ``` ### Expected behavior The expected output should be: ``` ruby @greeting # => "Welcome! The date and time is 2018-09-06 11:09:16 -0500" ```

rails/rails#55460.body:
### Steps to reproduce

<!-- (Guidelines for creating a bug report are [available
here](https://edgeguides.rubyonrails.org/contributing_to_ruby_on_rails.html#creating-a-bug-report)) -->

<!-- Paste your executable test case created from one of the scripts found [here](https://edgeguides.rubyonrails.org/contributing_to_ruby_on_rails.html#create-an-executable-test-case) below: -->

Not exactly a bug, but more of a question about whether `Rails.logger` should be `#freeze`-friendly.

We're implementing a custom railtie that freezes `Rails.logger` to prevent unintentional modifications by gem initializers or application code. This addresses a recurring issue where we've accidentally changed the Rails logger level when configuring logging for gems that share the same logger instance.

For example, when a gem shares `Rails.logger` and we configure its logging level in an initializer like:
```ruby
# config/initializers/some_gem.rb

SomeGem.logger.level = Logger::DEBUG  # Oops! This changes Rails.logger.level too
```

This has happened a few times in our codebase, causing production log level changes that resulted in either lost log messages (when accidentally set to a higher level) or excessive logging (when set to a lower level).

The railtie works by freezing the logger after Rails completes its logger setup but before any `config/initializers` run. However, we discovered that Rails' `ActiveSupport::LoggerThreadSafeLevel` module attempts to modify the logger's singleton class when accessing `.level`, which fails when the logger is frozen (see link in code comment).

Our current workaround is to call `Rails.logger.level` before freezing to trigger the lazy initialization of the thread-local key, but this feels like an implementation detail we shouldn't have to know about. It would be ideal if Rails loggers were designed to be freeze-friendly, allowing safe freezing without requiring knowledge of internal lazy initialization patterns.

```ruby
# frozen_string_literal: true

# This railtie freezes the singleton Rails logger instance to prevent unintentional modifications
# during initialisation or runtime. This ensures that in production, we don't lose log messages due
# to a config initializer for a gem changing the logger level when sharing the same logger instance.
#
class FreezeLoggerRailtie < Rails::Railtie
  # rspec-mocks relies on proxies for method stubbing and expectations,
  # and frozen objects cannot be proxied.
  unless Rails.env.test?
    initializer :freeze_logger, after: :initialize_logger, before: :load_config_initializers do
      # We need to read the logger level before freezing the logger because the thread-local key to store
      # the level is lazily assigned to an instance variable.
      #
      # See: https://github.com/rails/rails/blob/3235827585d87661942c91bc81f64f56d710f0b2/activesupport/lib/active_support/logger_thread_safe_level.rb#L43-L45
      Rails.logger.level
      Rails.logger.freeze

      if Rails.logger.is_a?(ActiveSupport::BroadcastLogger)
        Rails.logger.broadcasts.each do |logger|
          logger.level
          logger.freeze
        end
      end
    end
  end
end

```

### Expected behavior

<!-- Tell us what should happen -->

Calling `Rails.logger.level` or `Rails.logger.info` or any common method on `Rails.logger` shouldn't raise an error when `Rails.logger.freeze` has been called beforehand.

### Actual behavior

<!-- Tell us what happens instead -->

Calling `Rails.logger.level` or `Rails.logger.info` on `Rails.logger` when `Rails.logger.freeze` has been called beforehand raises an error during boot:

```
/Users/joshuayoung/Projects/buildkite/buildkite/.bundle/ruby/3.4.0/gems/activesupport-8.0.2/lib/active_support/logger_thread_safe_level.rb:44:in 'ActiveSupport::LoggerThreadSafeLevel#local_level_key': can't modify frozen #<Class:#<ActiveSupport::Logger:0x000000014bf3d170>>: #<ActiveSupport::Logger:0x000000014bf3d170 @level=1, @progname=nil, @default_formatter=#<Logger::Formatter:0x000000014bc515d8 @datetime_format=nil>, @formatter=#<ProductionLoggerFormatter:0x000000014bd3ed88 @datetime_format=nil>, @logdev=#<Logger::LogDevice:0x0000000160516380 @shift_period_suffix="%Y%m%d", @shift_size=1048576, @shift_age=0, @filename="/Users/joshuayoung/Projects/buildkite/buildkite/log/production.log", @dev=#<File:/Users/joshuayoung/Projects/buildkite/buildkite/log/production.log>, @binmode=false, @reraise_write_errors=[], @skip_header=false, @mon_data=#<Monitor:0x000000014bc51498>, @mon_data_owner_object_id=5768>, @level_override={}> (FrozenError)
	from /Users/joshuayoung/Projects/buildkite/buildkite/.bundle/ruby/3.4.0/gems/activesupport-8.0.2/lib/active_support/logger_thread_safe_level.rb:11:in 'ActiveSupport::LoggerThreadSafeLevel#local_level'
	from /Users/joshuayoung/Projects/buildkite/buildkite/.bundle/ruby/3.4.0/gems/activesupport-8.0.2/lib/active_support/logger_thread_safe_level.rb:31:in 'ActiveSupport::LoggerThreadSafeLevel#level'
	from /Users/joshuayoung/Projects/buildkite/buildkite/.bundle/ruby/3.4.0/gems/activesupport-8.0.2/lib/active_support/broadcast_logger.rb:109:in 'Array#map'
	from /Users/joshuayoung/Projects/buildkite/buildkite/.bundle/ruby/3.4.0/gems/activesupport-8.0.2/lib/active_support/broadcast_logger.rb:109:in 'ActiveSupport::BroadcastLogger#level'
	from /Users/joshuayoung/Projects/buildkite/buildkite/.bundle/ruby/3.4.0/gems/activerecord-8.0.2/lib/active_record/railtie.rb:68:in 'block in <class:Railtie>'
	from /Users/joshuayoung/Projects/buildkite/buildkite/.bundle/ruby/3.4.0/gems/railties-8.0.2/lib/rails/railtie.rb:272:in 'block in Rails::Railtie#run_console_blocks'

The Rails documentation specifically mentions that prepared statements are not compatible with pgbouncer. But pgbouncer has had prepared statement support since 1.21.0. Just wanted to check if this documentation is still valid or if it needs to be changed given the updates to pgbouncer's prepared statement functionality.

- https://github.com/rails/rails/blob/ac18a8b9d365c9a0698d577e48c3e76ea6a764b9/guides/source/configuring.md?plain=1#L3566

rails/rails#55509.body:
https://github.com/rails/rails/issues/28153 has become a problem again due to the addition of `\restrict` in an [update to all supported versions of PostgreSQL, released a few days ago](https://www.postgresql.org/about/news/postgresql-176-1610-1514-1419-1322-and-18-beta-3-released-3118/):

```diff
diff --git a/db/structure.sql b/db/structure.sql
index 3b27281..d1465ca 100644
--- a/db/structure.sql
+++ b/db/structure.sql
@@ -1,3 +1,8 @@
+\restrict pbgv1pF8SxQK6cuT7hwDi21uDYr8wpxKJ3wlLa9Zk5EIO1xBiu84SJQU8fL22PT
+
+-- Dumped from database version 15.4 (Debian 15.4-1.pgdg110+1)
+-- Dumped by pg_dump version 15.14 (Ubuntu 15.14-1.pgdg22.04+1)
+
 SET statement_timeout = 0;
 SET lock_timeout = 0;
 SET idle_in_transaction_session_timeout = 0;
@@ -18747,6 +18752,8 @@ ALTER TABLE ONLY public.user_restrictions
 -- PostgreSQL database dump complete
 --
 
+\unrestrict pbgv1pF8SxQK6cuT7hwDi21uDYr8wpxKJ3wlLa9Zk5EIO1xBiu84SJQU8fL22PT
+
 SET search_path TO "$user", public;
 
 INSERT INTO "schema_migrations" (version) VALUES
```

The `\restrict` line is mentioned as being added in the [changelog of 15.14](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/release/15.14/).

The addition of this line prevents the fix added in https://github.com/rails/rails/pull/28154 from working, as that removes comment/blank lines until the first non-comment line, but the `\restrict` line is now the first line, so nothing gets removed. And there is also `\unrestrict` added further down.

Adding the `\restrict` command was a security fix, but I don't think it's applicable to this use case - the schema dump is trusted - so is fine to remove.

The string within the `\restrict` command looks to be random, so even if the lines were kept, they'd produce an inconsistency. I think I saw there was a way to use a consistent key, but I think it's preferable to remove it to allow interoperability of different versions of PostgreSQL.

As a workaround, this is what I have added to our app:

```ruby
  # Prevent output added in pg_dump 15.14 from causing an unnecessary diff in db/structure.sql
  Rake::Task["db:schema:dump"].enhance do
    schema = File.read(Rails.root.join('db/structure.sql'))
    schema.gsub!(/^-- Dumped from database version.*$\n/, '')
    schema.gsub!(/^-- Dumped by pg_dump version.*$\n\n/, '')
    schema.gsub!(/^\\restrict .*$\n\n/, '')
    schema.gsub!(/^\\unrestrict .*$\n\n/, '')
    File.write(Rails.root.join('db/structure.sql'), schema)
  end
```

I've developed a fix in Rails now; PR incoming momentarily
rails/rails#55509.title: structure.sql is inconsistent on latest PostgreSQL versions, e.g. 15.14

rails/rails#55513.body:
### Steps to reproduce

In one terminal tab, with parallel tests enabled:

```console
$ rails test
```

In another tab, find one of the worker processes and kill it:

```console
$ ps aux | grep Rails
tavianator       60418  91.5  1.0 414614432 497440 s007  S+   10:29AM   0:01.42 Rails test worker 1 -
tavianator       60419  75.1  0.9 414614464 462272 s007  R+   10:29AM   0:01.34 Rails test worker 2 -
tavianator         845   0.0  0.0 414575232  13712   ??  S    Thu11AM   0:01.44 Rails test worker 0 -
...
$ kill -9 60418
```

### Expected behavior

The tests should fail.

### Actual behavior

The tests will hang forever:

```console
$ rails test
Running 3763 tests in parallel using 12 processes
Started with run options --seed 1248

  3762/3763: [============================================ ] 99% Time: 00:00:24,  ETA: 00:00:00
```

### System configuration

**Rails version**: 

```console
$ rails --version
Rails 8.0.2
```

**Ruby version**: 

```console
$ ruby --version
ruby 3.4.4 (2025-05-14 revision a38531fd3f) +PRISM [arm64-darwin23]
```

### More details

I noticed this due to tests timing out in CI.  It turned out that the workers were being OOM killed, but there was no indication in the logs.  All we saw was the tests hanging after nearly completing.

The hang happens in https://github.com/rails/rails/blob/430cff888c248eb45258c74953226edd3b4bae50/activesupport/lib/active_support/testing/parallelization/server.rb#L67-L70

`active_workers?` is waiting for a worker to call `stop_worker`: https://github.com/rails/rails/blob/430cff888c248eb45258c74953226edd3b4bae50/activesupport/lib/active_support/testing/parallelization/server.rb#L47-L53

But the worker will never call `stop_worker` if it's killed in the middle of running tests: https://github.com/rails/rails/blob/430cff888c248eb45258c74953226edd3b4bae50/activesupport/lib/active_support/testing/parallelization/worker.rb#L21-L32

I suspect the fix will involve refactoring `Parallelization#shutdown` to call `waitpid` earlier and check for a successful exit: https://github.com/rails/rails/blob/430cff888c248eb45258c74953226edd3b4bae50/activesupport/lib/active_support/testing/parallelization.rb#L62-L65
rails/rails#55513.title: Parallel tests hang if a worker dies abruptly

rails/rails#55518.body:
According to the [Rails Guides](https://guides.rubyonrails.org/configuring.html#config-action-controller-logger):

> Accepts a logger conforming to the interface of Log4r or the default Ruby Logger class, which is then used to log information from Action Controller. Set to nil to disable logging.

However, setting `config.action_controller.logger = nil` does not actually disable Action Controller logs. They still appear in the application logs.

### Steps to reproduce

1. In a new Rails app, configure the logger to be nil:

```ruby
# config/application.rb
module Demo
  class Application < Rails::Application
    # ...
    config.action_controller.logger = nil
  end
end
```

2. Boot the app and hit any controller endpoint.

3. Observe that Action Controller request logs are still emitted, such as: 

```
Processing by PostsController#index as HTML
Parameters: {"foo"=>"1", "bar"=>"2"}
Completed 200 OK in 11ms (Views: 0.3ms | ActiveRecord: 0.0ms | GC: 0.3ms)
```

Reproduction app available here: [GitHub repo](https://github.com/requiemformemories/disable_actioncontroller_logger_example)

### Expected behavior

- Setting `config.action_controller.logger = nil` should keep `ActionController::Base.logger` as nil.
- As a result, no Action Controller request logs (e.g., `Processing by...`, `Parameters:...`, `Completed...`) should be written.

### Actual behavior
- Even with `config.action_controller.logger = nil`, Action Controller logs are still written.

### Notes / investigation
Looking at the implementation in [actionpack/lib/action_controller/railtie.rb](https://github.com/rails/rails/blob/main/actionpack/lib/action_controller/railtie.rb):

```ruby
initializer "action_controller.set_configs" do |app|
  paths   = app.config.paths
  options = app.config.action_controller

  options.logger      ||= Rails.logger
  options.cache_store ||= Rails.cache
  # ...
```

The `||=` operator means if `options.logger` is nil, it falls back to `Rails.logger`.
This behavior conflicts with the documentation, which states that setting it to nil should disable logging.

#### Workarounds found so far:

(1) Explicitly clear the logger after initialization:
```ruby
Rails.application.config.after_initialize do
  ActionController::Base.logger = nil
end
```

(2) Point the logger to IO::NULL:
```ruby
config.action_controller.logger = ActiveSupport::Logger.new(IO::NULL)
```

### Proposal

If the intended behavior is that `nil` falls back to `Rails.logger`, then the Rails Guides should be updated to reflect this, instead of saying "Set to nil to disable logging."

### System configuration

**Rails version**: 8.0.1 
**Ruby version**: 3.3.4
rails/rails#55518.title: `config.action_controller.logger` = nil does not disable Action Controller logging
