rails/rails#55607.body:
### Steps to reproduce

In API mode, RoutingError occurs when simultaneous access occurs immediately after startup.
This only occurs the first time I access the app after launching it, and does not occur thereafter.
Possibly the same problem as #54306.

Sorry, I can't provide a simple test code like guide, because simultaneous access is a factor.
Since this is a timing-dependent issue, it may or may not occur depending on the environment, but the steps to reproduce it are as follows.

Rails side:
```console
$ rails new api --api
$ cd api
$ bundle install
$ rails g controller foo xxx yyy
$ rails s
```

front side:
```html
<html>
  <body>
    <script>
      Promise.all([
        fetch('http://localhost:3000/foo/xxx'),
        fetch('http://localhost:3000/foo/yyy')
      ])
    </script>
  </body>
</html>
```

... and just open HTML file with browser.

### Expected behavior

No error on rails side.
(CORS error is expected, that's not my point)

### Actual behavior

```
Started GET "/foo/xxx" for 127.0.0.1 at 2025-09-02 20:36:45 +0900
Started GET "/foo/yyy" for 127.0.0.1 at 2025-09-02 20:36:45 +0900
  ActiveRecord::SchemaMigration Load (0.1ms)  SELECT "schema_migrations"."version" FROM "schema_migrations" ORDER BY "schema_migrations"."version" ASC /*application='Api'*/
  
ActionController::RoutingError (No route matches [GET] "/foo/yyy"):
  
Processing by FooController#xxx as */*
Completed 204 No Content in 4ms (ActiveRecord: 0.0ms (0 queries, 0 cached) | GC: 0.0ms)
```

### System configuration

**Rails version**: 
8.0.2.1

**Ruby version**: 
3.4.5
rails/rails#55607.title: RoutingError occurs in API mode by simultaneous accesses after startup

rails/rails#55617.body:
The new template for config/environments/production.rb sets `public_file_server.headers["cache-control"]` to a proc, but I don't see anything that actually calls that proc.
Instead Rack just dumps it to a string in the output headers, like `#<Proc:0x0000000133195300 /Users/jon/Developer/rails-static-headers/config/environments/development.rb:19 (lambda)>`

### Steps to reproduce

Create a new Rails app, run it with RAILS_ENV=production, or copy the `config.public_file_server.headers` setting from production.rb to development.rb

Fetch a static file & inspect the headers:

```shellsession
$ curl -I localhost:3000/robots.txt
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
last-modified: Fri, 05 Sep 2025 11:24:59 GMT
content-type: text/plain
cache-control: #<Proc:0x0000000133195300 /Users/jon/Developer/rails-static-headers/config/environments/development.rb:19 (lambda)>
content-length: 99
```

### Expected behavior

The response headers should include something like `cache-control: public, max-age=12345`

### Actual behavior

The response headers output a proc that's just been dumped to a string.

### System configuration

**Rails version**: 8.1.0.beta1 (5a0ba202b9c855534a88057c04d741b62fc391bb)

**Ruby version**: 
3.4.5



I've created a reproduction repo at https://github.com/jdelStrother/rails-static-headers with `rails new --main --api`.

---

cc @thisismydesign
rails/rails#55617.title: public_file_server.headers can't contain procs

rails/rails#55619.body:
Follow-up: #55209, #55336

Hello,

Changes in #55336 introduced some test failures here: https://github.com/DavyJonesLocker/client_side_validations/issues/987

Specs check for the presence of the `autocomplete` attribute like this:

```rb
def autocomplete_attribute
  @autocomplete_attribute ||=
    if hidden_field_tag(:test).include?('autocomplete')
      'autocomplete="off"'
    else
      ''
    end
end
```

With Rails edge, `hidden_field_tag` correctly omits the `autocomplete` attribute. However, hidden fields generated by the following helpers still include it:
- `check_box`
- `select` (with `multiple`)
- `file_field` (with `multiple` and `include_hidden`)

These helpers use `tag` directly instead of `hidden_field_tag` and therefore don’t benefit from the recent change.

You can find the relevant code by searching for `/tag\("input".+autocomplete/`:
- https://github.com/rails/rails/blob/5a0ba202b9c855534a88057c04d741b62fc391bb/actionview/lib/action_view/helpers/tags/check_box.rb#L60
- https://github.com/rails/rails/blob/5a0ba202b9c855534a88057c04d741b62fc391bb/actionview/lib/action_view/helpers/tags/file_field.rb#L21
- https://github.com/rails/rails/blob/5a0ba202b9c855534a88057c04d741b62fc391bb/actionview/lib/action_view/helpers/tags/select_renderer.rb#L25

At the best of my knowledge, they are the only affected helpers

Thank you!

_Originally posted by @tagliala in https://github.com/rails/rails/issues/55336#issuecomment-3257716309_
rails/rails#55619.title: Rails edge: `autocomplete="off"` on hidden input is not being removed under some circumstances

rails/rails#55620.body:
### Steps to reproduce

Create new 8.1.0.beta1 project
```ruby
Loading development environment (Rails 8.1.0.beta1)
foo(dev):001> ActiveJob::Continuable
(foo):1:in '<main>': uninitialized constant ActiveJob::Continuable (NameError)
Did you mean?  ConditionVariable
foo(dev):002> ActiveJob::Continuation
=> ActiveJob::Continuation
foo(dev):003> ActiveJob::Continuable
=> ActiveJob::Continuable
foo(dev):004>
```

### Expected behavior

`ActiveJob::Continuable` should be available in development.

### Actual behavior

`uninitialized constant ActiveJob::Continuable (NameError)`

If works after requiring `active_job/continuation` or loading `ActiveJob::Continuable` first.

I think it's related to [moving it](https://github.com/rails/rails/commit/f4ec7e88989ab205a967a8647253851a23ebd712) to `eager_load` block.

### System configuration

**Rails version**: 8.1.0.beta1

**Ruby version**: 3.4.5
rails/rails#55620.title: Rails 8.1.0.beta1 uninitialized constant ActiveJob::Continuable (NameError) in development

rails/rails#55644.body: ### Steps to reproduce - use any ruby manager, other than rbenv and rvm, i.e mise - use a `pre` version like 3.5.0-preview1 to create a new rails app ### Expected behavior - `.ruby_version` should have `ruby-3.5.0-preview1` as value ### Actual behavior - `.ruby_version` have `ruby-3.5.0` as value instead ### System configuration **Rails version**: 8.1.0.beta1 **Ruby version**: 3.5.0-preview1 This mainly happens due to the implementation of the ruby-version template, it tries to use rvm and rbenv env variables which has the full ruby version name, however, if it tries the fallback, it'll just return `3.5.0` causing ruby shell load to fail https://github.com/rails/rails/blob/c22750a40f8801aa365f481cd15632e9deae1f6b/railties/lib/rails/generators/rails/app/templates/ruby-version.tt#L1 I can try to fix this but I'm not sure how to proceed. For instance, `Gem.ruby_version()` returns the correct version but in a Gem version format, and it does some string manipulation to get the `.preview1` part in the rubygems repo; should I create a similar function to return the correct ruby version and skip `RBENV` and `RVM` env vars all together, just use `RUBY_VERSION` and `RUBY_DESCRIPTION` and regex. https://github.com/rubygems/rubygems/blob/94f6f8332c58c9d7fb5245260e52238993a971a4/lib/rubygems.rb#L961 this breaks `.ruby-version` `Dockerfile` and ci templates when using a pre version of ruby
rails/rails#55644.title: .ruby-version file will be wrong when using a pre version with ruby managers other than rbenv or rvm (i.e mise)

rails/rails#55647.body:
### Steps to reproduce

Hi, I wanted to report a possible performance regression with `in_batches` and 8.1.0 beta1, which generates a different query than earlier versions.

The performance issue seems to occur when Postgres statistics are out-of-date (it's not clear to me how out-of-date they need to be, but this happens in the test suite for one of my projects).

```ruby
# frozen_string_literal: true

require "bundler/inline"

gemfile(true) do
  source "https://rubygems.org"

  gem "rails", "8.1.0.beta1"
  gem "pg"
end

require "active_record/railtie"
require "minitest/autorun"

ENV["DATABASE_URL"] = "postgres://localhost/repro"

class TestApp < Rails::Application
  config.load_defaults Rails::VERSION::STRING.to_f
  config.eager_load = false
  config.logger = Logger.new($stdout)
  config.secret_key_base = "secret_key_base"
end
Rails.application.initialize!

ActiveRecord::Schema.define do
  create_table :posts, force: true do |t|
    t.text :title
    t.text :content
  end
end

class Post < ActiveRecord::Base
end

class BugTest < ActiveSupport::TestCase
  def test_works
    3.times do
      Post.create!(content: "Test")
      Post.delete_all
    end

    # generate statistics (will soon be outdated)
    Post.connection.execute("ANALYZE posts")
 
    Post.create!(content: "Test")
    Post.insert_all!(10000.times.map { {title: "Test"} })
    Post.in_batches(of: 10000) do |relation|
      relation.where(content: nil).update_all(content: "Test")
    end
  end
end
```

### Expected behavior

A fast query, like earlier versions of Active Record (or the ability to opt-in to this type of query)

```text
Post Update All (15.8ms)  UPDATE "posts" SET "content" = $1 WHERE "posts"."id" <= $2 AND "posts"."content" IS NULL  [["content", "Test"], ["id", 10003]]
```

Explain output (separate run)

```text
                                                         QUERY PLAN                                                         
----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Update on posts  (cost=0.25..8.27 rows=0 width=0) (actual time=30.955..30.955 rows=0 loops=1)
   ->  Index Scan using posts_pkey on posts  (cost=0.25..8.27 rows=1 width=38) (actual time=0.081..2.227 rows=9999 loops=1)
         Index Cond: (id <= 10003)
         Filter: (content IS NULL)
         Rows Removed by Filter: 1
 Planning Time: 0.382 ms
 Execution Time: 31.000 ms
```

### Actual behavior

A slow query

```text
Post Update All (9475.9ms)  UPDATE "posts" SET "content" = $1 WHERE ("posts"."id") IN (SELECT "posts"."id" FROM "posts" WHERE "posts"."id" <= $2 AND "posts"."content" IS NULL ORDER BY "posts"."id" ASC)  [["content", "Test"], ["id", 10003]]
```

Note that `use_ranges: true` does not change the query

Explain output (separate run)

```text
                                                                    QUERY PLAN                                                                     
---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Update on posts  (cost=0.50..16.55 rows=0 width=0) (actual time=11202.341..11202.342 rows=0 loops=1)
   ->  Nested Loop Semi Join  (cost=0.50..16.55 rows=1 width=70) (actual time=5.318..11186.322 rows=9999 loops=1)
         Join Filter: (posts.id = "ANY_subquery".id)
         Rows Removed by Join Filter: 50004999
         ->  Index Scan using posts_pkey on posts  (cost=0.25..8.26 rows=1 width=14) (actual time=0.123..1.077 rows=10001 loops=1)
         ->  Subquery Scan on "ANY_subquery"  (cost=0.25..8.28 rows=1 width=40) (actual time=0.003..1.024 rows=5001 loops=10001)
               ->  Index Scan using posts_pkey on posts posts_1  (cost=0.25..8.27 rows=1 width=8) (actual time=0.003..0.765 rows=5001 loops=10001)
                     Index Cond: (id <= 10003)
                     Filter: (content IS NULL)
                     Rows Removed by Filter: 1
 Planning Time: 0.656 ms
 Execution Time: 11202.424 ms
```

### System configuration

**Rails version**: 8.1.0 beta1

**Ruby version**: 3.4.5
rails/rails#55647.title: Performance regression with `in_batches` update query with 8.1.0 beta1

rails/rails#55648.body:
During investigation of performance issues in gitlab i discover a lot of expensive schema queries for primary keys. They come from [schema_statements.rb](https://github.com/rails/rails/blob/main/activerecord/lib/active_record/connection_adapters/postgresql/schema_statements.rb#L435).[schema_statements.rb](https://github.com/rails/rails/blob/main/activerecord/lib/active_record/connection_adapters/postgresql/schema_statements.rb#L435). This is not the recommended way to query this information. https://wiki.postgresql.org/wiki/Retrieve_primary_key_columns The recommended is way faster an use less memory. Is there any special case this statement has to be so heavy? PostgreSQL 17.6 ### Plan: query primary key ruby flavor ```pgsql -- query primary key SELECT a.attname FROM ( SELECT indrelid, indkey, generate_subscripts(indkey, 1) idx FROM pg_index WHERE indrelid = '"user_callouts"'::regclass AND indisprimary ) i JOIN pg_attribute a ON a.attrelid = i.indrelid AND a.attnum = i.indkey[i.idx] ORDER BY i.idx ```
\# Node Timings Rows Loops
Exclusive Inclusive Rows X Actual Plan
1. Sort (cost=3249.07..3249.83 rows=303 width=68) (actual=23.89..23.892 rows=1 loops=1) 0.017 ms 23.892 ms ↑ 303 1 303 1
2. Hash Inner Join (cost=2922.43..3236.58 rows=303 width=68) (actual=23.841..23.875 rows=1 loops=1) * **Hash Cond**: ((pg_index.indrelid = a.attrelid) AND (pg_index.indkey\[(generate_subscripts(pg_index.indkey, 1))\] = a.attnum)) 0.485 ms 23.875 ms ↑ 303 1 303 1
3. ProjectSet (cost=0.29..146.68 rows=11000 width=35) (actual=0.01..0.043 rows=1 loops=1) 0.006 ms 0.043 ms ↑ 11000 1 11000 1
4. Index Scan using pg_index_indrelid_index on pg_catalog.pg_index as pg_index (cost=0.29..91.6 rows=11 width=31) (actual=0.008..0.037 rows=1 loops=1) * **Filter**: pg_index.indisprimary * **Index Cond**: (pg_index.indrelid = '23333'::oid) * **Rows Removed by Filter**: 29 0.037 ms 0.037 ms ↑ 11 1 11 1
5. Hash (cost=1934.46..1934.46 rows=65846 width=70) (actual=23.346..23.347 rows=65880 loops=1) * **Buckets**: 131072 **Batches**: 1 **Memory Usage**: 7587 kB 12.017 ms 23.347 ms ↓ 1.01 65880 65846 1
6. Seq Scan on pg_catalog.pg_attribute as a (cost=0..1934.46 rows=65846 width=70) (actual=0.006..11.331 rows=65880 loops=1) 11.331 ms 11.331 ms ↓ 1.01 65880 65846 1
### Plan: query primary key recommended
\# Node Timings Rows Loops
Exclusive Inclusive Rows X Actual Plan
1. Nested Loop Inner Join (cost=0.58..267.86 rows=19 width=96) (actual=0.051..0.088 rows=1 loops=1) * **Join Filter**: (a.attnum = ANY ((i.indkey)::smallint\[\])) 0.059 ms 0.088 ms ↑ 19 1 19 1
2. Index Scan using pg_attribute_relid_attnum_index on pg_catalog.pg_attribute as a (cost=0.29..159.95 rows=59 width=78) (actual=0.01..0.028 rows=65 loops=1) * **Index Cond**: (a.attrelid = '23333'::oid) 0.028 ms 0.028 ms ↓ 1.11 65 59 1
3. Materialize (cost=0.29..91.66 rows=11 width=31) (actual=0..0.001 rows=1 loops=65) 0.001 ms 0.001 ms ↑ 11 1 11 65
4. Index Scan using pg_index_indrelid_index on pg_catalog.pg_index as i (cost=0.29..91.6 rows=11 width=31) (actual=0.009..0.03 rows=1 loops=1) * **Filter**: i.indisprimary * **Index Cond**: (i.indrelid = '23333'::oid) * **Rows Removed by Filter**: 29 0.001 ms 0.001 ms ↑ 0.17 1 11 1
rails/rails#55648.title: Unoptimized schema query for primary key colums in PostgreSQL adapter

rails/rails#55653.body:
Currently it appears that rail deallocates prepared statements via statement instead[0]. This is not efficient and does not work well with pgbouncer. See the Deallocation functionality in psycopg [2] as an example of protocol prepared statement Deallocation.

If I understand correctly it appears that rails uses the ruby-pg library [3] to execute protocol level prepare [4, 5]. Similarly I believe it should be a matter of invoking the `close_prepared` function in ruby-pg.

I have not tested the below code but I believe the solution could be something like this.

```diff
diff --git a/activerecord/lib/active_record/connection_adapters/postgresql_adapter.rb b/activerecord/lib/active_record/connection_adapters/postgresql_adapter.rb
index 18de389..9001f5a 100644
--- a/activerecord/lib/active_record/connection_adapters/postgresql_adapter.rb
+++ b/activerecord/lib/active_record/connection_adapters/postgresql_adapter.rb
@@ -310,7 +310,9 @@ def dealloc(key)
             # don't need the complication of with_raw_connection because
             # a reconnect would invalidate the entire statement pool.)
             if conn = @connection.instance_variable_get(:@raw_connection)
-              conn.query "DEALLOCATE #{key}" if conn.status == PG::CONNECTION_OK
+              if conn.status == PG::CONNECTION_OK
+                conn.close_prepared "#{key}"
+              end
             end
           rescue PG::Error
           end

```

[1] https://www.postgresql.org/docs/current/protocol-message-formats.html#PROTOCOL-MESSAGE-FORMATS-CLOSE
[2] https://github.com/psycopg/psycopg/blob/94583702ada92187c8b1bb4da8de0d30a879c8bd/psycopg/psycopg/_connection_base.py#L489

related to https://github.com/rails/rails/pull/55448/ ### Steps to reproduce ```ruby # frozen_string_literal: true require "bundler/inline" gemfile(true) do source "https://rubygems.org" # gem "rails", github: 'rails/rails' # If you want to test against edge Rails replace the previous line with this: gem "rails", github: "rails/rails", branch: "main" gem "net-smtp", github: "ruby/net-smtp", ref: "d496a829f9b99adb44ecc1768c4d005e5f7b779e", require: false end require "action_mailer/railtie" require "minitest/autorun" class TestApp < Rails::Application config.load_defaults Rails::VERSION::STRING.to_f config.root = __dir__ config.eager_load = false config.hosts << "example.org" config.secret_key_base = "secret_key_base" config.logger = Logger.new($stdout) end Rails.application.initialize! $should_not_be_run = false class TestMailer < ActionMailer::Base attr_accessor :should_not_be_run def hello_world @message = "Hello, world" $should_not_be_run = true mail from: "test@example.com", to: "user@example.com" do |format| format.html { render inline: "

rails/rails#55689.body:
### Steps to reproduce

At GitLab, we are upgrading to Rails 7.2 and noticed that all our requests made from system specs were no longer using the query cache.

I dug into the code and found the query cache refactor in https://github.com/rails/rails/commit/85742ce529b028812066cd6070e7de2ff3d44bc8 and other subsequent changes related to that.

Based on that, the pool now owns the caches and it keeps a set of cache stores per thread. Now the problem happens when there is a pinned connection.

Here's the sequence of events:

1. RSpec thread checks out a connection and sets `connection.query_cache` to a new cache store based on the current thread. This connection is then pinned. The cache store for this connection and pool is disabled.
2. RSpec thread makes a web request to Puma
3. Puma processes the request and the Rails executor hook enables the query cache on a new cache store based on the Puma thread.
4. Puma thread checks out a connection and gets the same pinned connection. `checkout_and_verify` is not called in this code path so `connection.query_cache` still points to the cache store owned by the RSpec thread.
 
   This cache store isn't the one enabled by the executor hook so this request does not use the query cache. 


### System configuration

**Rails version**: 7.2

**Ruby version**: 3.3.9
rails/rails#55689.title: Query cache does not work in system tests in Rails 7.2+

rails/rails#55708.body:
My team discovered this issue troubleshooting a flaky test that depended on class state that was (re)created at load time. We found that when `Time.now` is stubbed with time travel test helpers, the checker treats all files as updated and reloads even when files haven't been changed. Our temporary fix was to enable `cache_classes` in the Rails config for tests (which does make sense in general), but this led me to a possible change to the `FileUpdateChecker` to avoid those unnecessary file reloads caused by time travel in tests.

### Steps to reproduce

Run `ruby file_update_checker_time_travel.rb --seed 23705` with the following structure and observe the constant is reloaded:

```
  1) Failure:
BugTest#test_without_time_travel [file_update_checker_time_travel.rb:58]:
--- expected
+++ actual
@@ -1,3 +1,3 @@
 # encoding: ASCII-8BIT
 #    valid: true
-"243"
+"437"
```

This is the smallest setup I could get to show that a test, when executed following one that uses time travel helpers, will experience unnecessary reloads.

```
.
├── app
│   └── models
│       └── user.rb
├── config
│   └── routes.rb
└── file_update_checker_time_travel.rb
```

```ruby
# app/models/user.rb
class User
  puts "Loading User..."
  cattr_accessor :random_value do
    rand(1000)
  end
end
```

```ruby
# config/routes.rb
Rails.application.routes.draw do
  get "/", to: "test#index"
end
```

```ruby
# file_update_checker_time_travel.rb
# frozen_string_literal: true

require "bundler/inline"

gemfile(true) do
  source "https://rubygems.org"

  gem "rails"
end

require "action_controller/railtie"
require "minitest/autorun"
require "rack/test"

class TestApp < Rails::Application
  config.load_defaults Rails::VERSION::STRING.to_f
  config.root = __dir__
  config.eager_load = false
  config.hosts << "example.org"
  config.secret_key_base = "secret_key_base"
  config.logger = Logger.new($stdout)

  config.enable_reloading = true
end

Rails.application.initialize!

# moved routes to config/routes.rb - otherwise the RoutesReloader runs before tests start and
# @loaded is already true => the bug isn't reproduced
# Rails.application.routes.draw do
#   get "/", to: "test#index"
# end

class TestController < ActionController::Base
  include Rails.application.routes.url_helpers

  def index
    render plain: User.random_value
  end
end

class BugTest < ActionDispatch::IntegrationTest
  include Rack::Test::Methods

  $initial_random_value = nil
  def test_with_time_travel # ensure with a --seed value that this runs first
    travel_to Time.utc(2023) do
      get "/"
      assert last_response.ok?
      $initial_random_value = last_response.body
      puts last_response.body
    end
  end

  def test_without_time_travel # ensure this runs last
    get "/"
    assert last_response.ok?
    assert_equal last_response.body, $initial_random_value
  end

  private

  def app
    Rails.application
  end
end

# extra debugging for FileUpdateChecker
module ActiveSupport
  class FileUpdateChecker
    alias_method :original_initialize, :initialize
    alias_method :original_updated?, :updated?
    alias_method :original_execute, :execute
    def initialize(files, dirs = {}, &block)
      original_initialize(files, dirs = {}, &block)
      if caller.any? { |line| line.include?("routes_reloader") }
        puts "[initializing FileUpdateChecker] #{self.object_id} for #{files.inspect}, #{dirs.inspect}, @last_update_at: #{@last_update_at.inspect}"
      end
    end

    def updated?
      original_updated?.tap do
        if caller.any? { |line| line.include?("routes_reloader") }
          puts "[updated? #{self.object_id}] @last_update_at: #{@last_update_at.inspect}"
        end
      end
    end

    def execute
      original_execute.tap do
        if caller.any? { |line| line.include?("routes_reloader") }
          puts "[execute #{self.object_id}] set @last_update_at to #{@last_update_at.inspect}"
        end
      end
    end
  end
end

```

### Expected behavior

Unchanged files are not reloaded.

### Actual behavior

`FileUpdateChecker` sets `@last_update_at` to `Time.at(0)` and treats all files as changed.

### Proposed fixes (pull request pending)

- replace `Time.now` with system time in `FileUpdateChecker`: [Use process time instead of Time.now in FileUpdateChecker](https://github.com/rails/rails/commit/b623a64045168dd6f441cb167f9911bf51714a62)
- kind of a bonus, but using the `EventedFileUpdateChecker` for reloading routes also fixed our issue: [Respect the file_watcher config in the routes reloader](https://github.com/rails/rails/commit/8a3d601a423865f88dd08acf8679f8136185a8da)

### Additional notes

New Rails apps disable reloading in the `test` environment by default, so they won't be affected by this.

```
# While tests run files are not watched, reloading is not necessary.
config.enable_reloading = false
```

### System configuration

**Rails version**: 8.0.2.1

**Ruby version**: 3.4.5
rails/rails#55708.title: ActiveSupport::FileUpdateChecker triggers unnecessary reloads in tests using time travel

rails/rails#55728.body:
I observe a ~5% increase in response time since migrating to Rails 7.2.

This regression stems from the changes introduced with `ActiveRecord::Base.with_connection` and `ActiveRecord::Base.lease_connection`.

Indeed, as there's no permanent AR connection anymore, my app now leases then releases it's connection, at least once for **every ActiveRecord query**.

Traced  those `with_connection` calls from `ActiveRecord::Core::ClassMethods#cached_find_by`, `ActiveRecord::Associations::AliasTracker.create`, `AssociationReflection#association_scope_cache`, `ActiveRecord::Associations::Association#find_target`...

This also triggers many, many calls to `checkin` callbacks per request, which is quite wasteful.

I get that the change can be beneficial on busy heavily multi-threaded workers, but I don't see the benefit on single-threaded web workers (`unicorn`) or when the connection pool is large enough for all threads (and when the database able to deal with all those concurrent connections).

Adding a call to `ActiveRecord::Base.lease_connection` as the first `before_action` in the controllers that requires a connection to the DB restores the performance back to Rails 7.1 level.

Is this new default behavior expected?
Should my explicit `lease_connection` hack in my controllers be needed (and does it have a drawback I didn't get?) or did I miss something that would pin the connection at first use?

Thanks!

cc @byroot 

### System configuration

**Rails version**: 7.2+

**Ruby version**: 3.4
rails/rails#55728.title: 7.1 -> 7.2 perf regression due to non permanent ActiveRecord connections

rails/rails#55758.body:
This is a really niche issue, but I was able to reproduce it in a sample app.

### Steps to reproduce

<!-- (Guidelines for creating a bug report are [available
here](https://edgeguides.rubyonrails.org/contributing_to_ruby_on_rails.html#creating-a-bug-report)) -->

<!-- Paste your executable test case created from one of the scripts found [here](https://edgeguides.rubyonrails.org/contributing_to_ruby_on_rails.html#create-an-executable-test-case) below: -->

I've generated a [minimal sample app](https://github.com/a-maas/testapp) that reproduces this issue. The app has:

1. Multiple [postgresql databases](https://github.com/a-maas/testapp/blob/main/docker-compose.yml), [one primary, and one replica](https://github.com/a-maas/testapp/blob/main/config/database.yml#L16-L39).
2. Standard minitest, with the tests running in parallel so that there are `n` number of test databases for each parallel worker, i.e. `test_db-0` & `test_db-1`
3. The IntegrationTest class should use `use_transactional_tests=false`. This issue does not happen when `use_transactional_tests=true`, but assume that it's not possible to use `use_transactional_tests=true` in this case.

To reproduce with the sample app:

1. Pull down the sample app (requires docker for postgresql)
2. docker compose to get the databases working `docker compose up -d`
3. `./bin/setup`
4. Run the tests in parallel `PARALLEL_WORKERS=5 ./bin/rails test`
5. Observe that all the tests fail with 404

### Expected behavior

<!-- Tell us what should happen -->

When running the tests in parallel, the IntegrationTest should use the same database for the controller request that it does for test setup.

i.e.
[Example test file](https://github.com/a-maas/testapp/blob/main/test/controllers/tests_controller_test.rb)
```
class TestsControllerTest < ActionDispatch::IntegrationTest
  self.use_transactional_tests = false
  test "should get show" do
    test = Test.create(name: "test") # Should use `test_db-{n}`
    get test_url(test)  # Should use `test_db-{n}`
    assert_response :success
  end
end
```

### Actual behavior

<!-- Tell us what happens instead -->

When running the tests in parallel, the IntegrationTest uses the numbered parallel databases, `test_db-{n}`, for test setup, but uses non-number test database, i.e. `test_db` for the controller request, leading to not found errors.

```
class TestsControllerTest < ActionDispatch::IntegrationTest
  self.use_transactional_tests = false
  test "should get show" do
    test = Test.create(name: "test") # Uses `test_db-{n}`
    get test_url(test)  # Uses `test_db`
    assert_response :success # 404
  end
end
```

### System configuration

**Rails version**: 8.0.3

**Ruby version**: 3.4.3
rails/rails#55758.title: Parallel ActionDispatch::IntegrationTest tests, when using multiple databases and use_transactional_tests=false do not use the correct database

rails/rails#55761.body:
### Steps to reproduce

<!-- (Guidelines for creating a bug report are [available
here](https://edgeguides.rubyonrails.org/contributing_to_ruby_on_rails.html#creating-a-bug-report)) -->

<!-- Paste your executable test case created from one of the scripts found [here](https://edgeguides.rubyonrails.org/contributing_to_ruby_on_rails.html#create-an-executable-test-case) below: -->
```ruby
# frozen_string_literal: true

require "bundler/inline"

gemfile(true) do
  source "https://rubygems.org"

  gem "rails"
  # If you want to test against edge Rails replace the previous line with this:
  # gem "rails", github: "rails/rails", branch: "main"

  gem "sqlite3"
end

require "active_record/railtie"
require "minitest/autorun"

# This connection will do for database-independent bug reports.
ENV["DATABASE_URL"] = "sqlite3::memory:"

class TestApp < Rails::Application
  config.load_defaults Rails::VERSION::STRING.to_f
  config.eager_load = false
  config.logger = Logger.new($stdout)
  config.secret_key_base = "secret_key_base"

  config.active_record.encryption.primary_key = "primary_key"
  config.active_record.encryption.deterministic_key = "deterministic_key"
  config.active_record.encryption.key_derivation_salt = "key_derivation_salt"
end
Rails.application.initialize!

ActiveRecord::Schema.define do
  create_table :posts, force: true do |t|
    t.string :title
    t.boolean :local
    t.boolean :global
  end
end

class Post < ActiveRecord::Base
  validates :title, presence: { if: :local? }, if: :global?
end

class BugTest < ActiveSupport::TestCase
  def test_association_stuff
    assert_not Post.new(local: true, global: true).valid?

    assert Post.new(local: false, global: true).valid?
    assert Post.new(local: false, global: false).valid?

    # Presence's :if (local) replaces validates's :if (global), so whether global? is truthy or falsey is irrelevant
    assert Post.new(local: true, global: false).valid?
  end
end
```

### Expected behavior

I expected the validation to only be enforced when both `local` and `global` were truthy.

### Actual behavior

The validation is enforced when `local` is truthy, regardless of the value of `global`.

### System configuration

**Rails version**: main

**Ruby version**: 3.4.5

### More Info

That's an over-simplified reproduction.

More specifically, I have a number of validations I want to run only when a certain condition passes, then within those validations, a subset have an additional criteria.

I tried something like:
```ruby
with_options(if: :global?) do |o|
  o.validates :title, presence: true, if: :local?
  o.validates :author, presence: true
  # and so on
end
```

hoping it'd become something like (not literally expecting Rails to define a method combining the conditions):
```ruby
validates :title, presence: true, if: :local_and_global?
validates :author, presence: true, if: :global?
```

But `with_options` documents that the inner options take precedence over the options given to `with_options`, so that wouldn't work the way I wanted, as that becomes:
```ruby
validates :title, presence: true, if: :local?
validates :author, presence: true, if: :global?
```

Then I considered moving the condition inside the specific validator (`presence`), rather than at the `validates` level, along the lines of:
```ruby
with_options(if: :global?) do |o|
  o.validates :title, presence: { if: :local? }
  o.validates :author, presence: true
  # and so on
end
```
hoping it would effectively combine the `:if`s, checking both, yielding something equivalent to:
```ruby
validates :title, presence: { if: :local? }, if: :global?
validates :author, presence: true, if: :global?
```

It seems the options given to individual validators take precedence over the options given at the `validates` level, so that really just evaluates to:
```ruby
validates_presence_of :title, if: :local?
```

Best I can tell, having a validator-specific `:if` and `validates`-level `:if` is not explicitly [documented](https://api.rubyonrails.org/classes/ActiveModel/Validations/ClassMethods.html#method-i-validates), nor is it explicitly [tested](https://github.com/rails/rails/blob/a821b31a40a03404412a1aafeb9c3190f138ac0b/activemodel/test/cases/validations/validates_test.rb), leading me to believe this combination is simply undefined as to what the expected behavior should be.

I could see three directions to take this in:
1. document that when providing the same option at both levels, the inner one wins
2. check both conditions to enforce validation (though this could be breaking for applications reliant on the current behavior). Perhaps this could be accomplished by adding array support to `:if` where all conditions need to pass to enforce the validation, then Rails could combine both levels of `:if`s into an array automatically (and the end-user can use an array where needed, separately from this issue).
3. disallow/error when the same option is provided at both levels, as the intended effects could be ambiguous (replace or combine?)

There's likely a broader question here in that whatever is decided for `:if` would likely apply to `:unless`, but would it also apply to the other options (ie. `:on`, `:except_on`, `:allow_nil`, `:allow_blank`, or `:strict`)?
rails/rails#55761.title: `validates` per-validator options replace top-level options

rails/rails#55771.body:
### Steps to reproduce

Have a model relationship with a `foo --* bar --* baz` like structure where nested attributes are accepted for the deeper models. Delete a distant model `baz` via nested attributes when `bar`'s validations require it not be deleted.

In this case, the instance of `bar` will be invalid, and therefore `foo` will be invalid, but the deletion will occur anyway in Rails 8.0.2 and up. This leads to an invalid model structure when it was valid before.

The deletion does not occur in Rails 8.0.1 and validity is preserved.

A reproduction script follows, where the models are named `Parent`, `Child`, and `Grandchild` for simplicity, but I do not believe the tree-like structure is necessary.

```ruby
# frozen_string_literal: true

require "bundler/inline"

gemfile(true) do
  source "https://rubygems.org"

  # The tests pass in 8.0.1
  # gem "rails", "8.0.1"

  # The tests fail in 8.0.2
  gem "rails", "8.0.2"

  # and in 8.0.3
  # gem "rails", "8.0.3"

  gem "sqlite3"
end

require "active_record/railtie"
require "minitest/autorun"

# This connection will do for database-independent bug reports.
ENV["DATABASE_URL"] = "sqlite3::memory:"

class TestApp < Rails::Application
  config.load_defaults Rails::VERSION::STRING.to_f
  config.eager_load = false
  config.logger = Logger.new($stdout)
  config.secret_key_base = "secret_key_base"

  config.active_record.encryption.primary_key = "primary_key"
  config.active_record.encryption.deterministic_key = "deterministic_key"
  config.active_record.encryption.key_derivation_salt = "key_derivation_salt"
end
Rails.application.initialize!

ActiveRecord::Schema.define do
  create_table :parents, force: true do |t|
  end

  create_table :children, force: true do |t|
    t.integer :parent_id
  end

  create_table :grandchildren, force: true do |t|
    t.integer :child_id
  end
end

class Parent < ActiveRecord::Base
  has_many :children
  accepts_nested_attributes_for :children, allow_destroy: true
end

class Child < ActiveRecord::Base
  belongs_to :parent
  has_many :grandchildren
  accepts_nested_attributes_for :grandchildren, allow_destroy: true

  validate :has_at_least_two_grandchildren

  # This is a workaround for 8.0.2:
  # def changed?
  #   grandchildren.any? { |gc| gc.marked_for_destruction? } || super
  # end

  private

  def has_at_least_two_grandchildren
    current_grandchildren = grandchildren.select { |gc| !gc.marked_for_destruction? }
    errors.add(:grandchildren, "must have at least two grandchildren") if current_grandchildren.size < 2
  end
end

class Grandchild < ActiveRecord::Base
  belongs_to :child
end

class BugTest < ActiveSupport::TestCase
  setup do
    @parent = Parent.create!
    @child = @parent.children.new
    @grandchild1 = @child.grandchildren.new
    @grandchild2 = @child.grandchildren.new
    @child.save!

    @deletion_params = {
      "children_attributes" => {
        "0" => {
          "id" => @child.id,
          "grandchildren_attributes" => {
            "0" => {
              "id" => @grandchild1.id,
              "_destroy" => '1',
            },
          }
        }
      }
    }
  end

  test "starts out in a valid state" do
    assert_predicate @parent, :valid?
    assert_predicate @child, :valid?
    assert_predicate @grandchild1, :valid?
    assert_predicate @grandchild2, :valid?
  end

  test "cannot update a parent with invalid child via deleting a grandchild" do
    refute @parent.update(@deletion_params), "Parent should not update successfully"
  end

  test "failed update should not delete grandchild" do
    @parent.update(@deletion_params)
    assert_nothing_raised do
      @grandchild1.reload
    end
  end

  test "child should be invalid after failed update" do
    @parent.update(@deletion_params)
    refute_predicate @child, :valid?
    assert_includes @child.errors[:grandchildren], "must have at least two grandchildren"
  end

  test "parent should be invalid when child is invalid after failed update" do
    @parent.update(@deletion_params)
    refute_predicate @parent, :valid?
    assert_includes @parent.errors[:"children.grandchildren"], "must have at least two grandchildren"
  end
end
```

Note that you can change the version to 8.0.1 and see that the tests pass. There is also a workaround commented out that can be uncommented to have the tests pass in 8.0.2 and above. 

It also fails in 8.0.3, but 8.0.2 is used a reference since that is the first failing version.

### Expected behavior

All tests should pass as they do in Rails 8.0.1.

In this case, the `Parent` instance should not be saved, because its existing `Child` instance is now invalid. The child is properly validated and expresses that it is invalid through adding an error.

### Actual behavior

The `Parent` is saved, its `Child` instance is saved, and the `Grandchild` instance is deleted. Now the `Child` is invalid and the `Parent` is invalid, despite all records starting as valid.

### Notes

This behavior was changed in

- https://github.com/rails/rails/pull/53951 (cc @byroot)
- https://github.com/rails/rails/pull/54273 (cc @Edouard-chin)

Notably it is caused by this in `activerecord/lib/active_record/autosave_association.rb`:

```ruby
if record.changed? || record.new_record? || context
  associated_errors = record.errors.objects
else
  # If there are existing invalid records in the DB, we should still be able to reference them.
  # Unless a record (no matter where in the association chain) is invalid and is being changed.
  associated_errors = record.errors.objects.select { |error| error.is_a?(Associations::NestedError) }
end
```

The issue is there are _not_ existing invalid records in the database. Unfortunately, the change is occurring via an association with nested attributes and this is not reported via dirty tracking.

As a workaround, we can modify our own code like so:

```ruby
def changed?
  grandchildren.any? { |gc| gc.marked_for_destruction? } || super
end
```

But this is surprising behavior for a patch release since it worked through 8.0.1.

### System configuration

**Rails version**: 8.0.2+

**Ruby version**: 3.4.1
rails/rails#55771.title: Rails 8.0.2+ saves invalid records when required distantly associated records are marked for deletion (is correct on 8.0.1)

### Steps to reproduce

```ruby
# frozen_string_literal: true

require "bundler/inline"

gemfile(true) do
  source "https://rubygems.org"

  gem "rails", "8.0.3"
  gem "securerandom", "0.4.1"

  # gem "rails", github: "rails/rails", branch: "main"
end

require "active_support"
require "active_support/core_ext/object/blank"
require "minitest/autorun"

class TestClass
  def eigenclass
    @eigenclass = class << self; self end
  end
end

class BugTest < ActiveSupport::TestCase
  def test_stuff
    instance = TestClass.new
    eigenclass = instance.eigenclass

    eigenclass.class_attribute :foo

    eigenclass.foo = 'bar'
    assert_equal 'bar', eigenclass.foo
    assert_equal 'bar', instance.foo

    instance.foo = 'foo'
    assert_equal 'bar', eigenclass.foo
    assert_equal 'bar', instance.foo # This is the output of Rails 7.2.2.2 but is this even expected?
  end
end
```

### Expected behavior

It should not throw an exception when trying to access `instance.foo`.

### Actual behavior

Accessing `instance.foo` results in:
```
NameError: undefined local variable or method `__class_attr_foo' for #<TestClass:0x00007efcca67c5f0>
    test.rb:29:in `foo'
    test.rb:33:in `test_stuff'
```

### Steps to reproduce

rails new lol --minimal --skip-active-record
cd lol

end

### Expected behavior

If I have a file `config/environments/staging.rb` and I accidentally try to run `RAILS_ENV=stage rails server` (note it is `stage` but the file is `staging`). I expect I will get an error.

### Actual behavior

It boots with no problem and no clear warning:

```
$ RAILS_ENV=stage rails server
=> Booting Puma
=> Rails 8.0.3 application starting in stage
=> Run `bin/rails server --help` for more startup options
config.eager_load is set to nil. Please update your config/environments/*.rb files accordingly:

  * development - set it to false
  * test - set it to false (unless you use a tool that preloads your test environment)
  * production - set it to true

Puma starting in single mode...
* Puma version: 7.0.4 ("Romantic Warrior")

*  Environment: stage
*          PID: 86318
* Listening on http://0.0.0.0:3000
Use Ctrl-C to stop
```

## Suggestion

> WARNING: You are using `RAILS_ENV=stage`, but no `config/environments/stage.rb` was found. Known environments:
>
> - test.rb
> - production.rb
> - development.rb
>
> If this is intentional, you can ignore this warning by setting `RAILS_IGNORE_MISSING_ENV=stage`

Related: We could also have `Rails.env.<environment>?`, also check for the existence of a specific file for proof that it's a valid environment. Otherwise, `Rails.env.staaging?` has the same problem (silent typo failure, hard to find).

rails/rails#55785.body:
In Rails 8.1, the select method generates duplicate columns.
This causes an error when used as a subquery with `distinct`.

This behavior change is related to https://github.com/rails/rails/pull/53723.

### Steps to reproduce

```ruby
# frozen_string_literal: true

require "bundler/inline"

gemfile(true) do
  source "https://rubygems.org"

  if ENV["RAILS_EDGE"]
    gem "rails", git: "https://github.com/rails/rails"
  else
    gem "rails", "~> 8.0"
  end
  gem "sqlite3"
end

require "active_record/railtie"
require "minitest/autorun"

# This connection will do for database-independent bug reports.
ENV["DATABASE_URL"] = "sqlite3::memory:"

class TestApp < Rails::Application
  config.load_defaults Rails::VERSION::STRING.to_f
  config.eager_load = false
  config.logger = Logger.new($stdout)
  config.secret_key_base = "secret_key_base"

  config.active_record.encryption.primary_key = "primary_key"
  config.active_record.encryption.deterministic_key = "deterministic_key"
  config.active_record.encryption.key_derivation_salt = "key_derivation_salt"
end
Rails.application.initialize!
ActiveRecord::Tasks::DatabaseTasks.create_all

ActiveRecord::Schema.define do
  create_table :posts, force: true do |t|
    t.string :title
  end
end

class Post < ActiveRecord::Base
end

class BugTest < ActiveSupport::TestCase
  def test_selecting_duplicate_columns
    Post.create!(title: 'foo')
    rel = Post.where(id: Post.distinct.select(:id).select(:id))

    assert_equal rel.take!.title, 'foo'
    # * 8.0
    # no errors
    #
    # * 8.1
    # ActiveRecord::StatementInvalid: SQLite3::SQLException: sub-select returns 2 columns - expected 1:
    # SELECT "posts".* FROM "posts" WHERE "posts"."id" IN (SELECT DISTINCT "posts"."id", "posts"."id" FROM "posts") LIMIT ?
  end
end
```

### Expected behavior

no errors.

### Actual behavior

ActiveRecord::StatementInvalid occurs.

### System configuration

**Rails version**: 8.1.0.beta1

**Ruby version**: 3.4.6
rails/rails#55785.title: 8.1.0.beta1: ActiveRecord generates duplicate columns, causing an exception

rails/rails#55803.body:
Running test suite for ActiveSupport against Ruby 3.4 on Fedora Rawhide, I observe following error:

~~~
  1) Error:
XmlMiniTest::ParsingTest#test_decimal:
ArgumentError: can't omit precision for a Float.
    lib/active_support/xml_mini.rb:78:in 'Kernel#BigDecimal'
    lib/active_support/xml_mini.rb:78:in 'block in <module:XmlMini>'
    test/xml_mini_test.rb:313:in 'XmlMiniTest::ParsingTest#test_decimal'
~~~

I believe this has been introduced by https://github.com/rails/rails/commit/c9a82e214046ec0daa06dc0b1718e948ccdc7c9a and is due to Ruby 3.4 shipping with older BigDecimal:

~~~
$ gem list | grep decimal
bigdecimal (3.1.8)
~~~

If Rails needs more recent BigDecimal, the dependency should be likely updated appropriately. Dunno.

rails/rails#55807.body:
### Steps to reproduce

As of Rails 8.0.3:
```ruby
Rails::VERSION::STRING
# => "8.0.3"

enum = Enumerator.new(1) do |yielder|
  yielder.yield(1, "one")
end

enum.first
# => [1, "one"]

enum.rewind

enum.sole
# => 1
```

### Expected behavior

`Enumerable#sole` should return the full tuple, and the same value as `Enumerable#first`. This was the case prior to Rails 8.0.3:
```ruby
Rails::VERSION::STRING
# => "8.0.2.1"

enum = Enumerator.new(1) do |yielder|
  yielder.yield(1, "one")
end

enum.first
# => [1, "one"]

enum.rewind

enum.sole
# => [1, "one"]
```

### Actual behavior

#54341 changed the behavior of `Enumerable#sole`, causing it to only return the first element of tuples instead of the full tuple.

### System configuration

**Rails version**: 8.0.3

**Ruby version**: 3.4.4
rails/rails#55807.title: `Enumerable#sole` returns only the first element of tuples

rails/rails#55823.body:
### Motivation / Background

When upgrading to Rails 8.1.0.beta1, older migrations were failing.

### Detail

There are several different ways to remove foreign keys in migrations:

```ruby
def up
  change_table(:sub_testings) do |t|
    t.remove_foreign_key :testings, column: "testing_id"
  end
end
```

also

```ruby
def up
  remove_foreign_key :sub_testings, column: "testing_id"
end
```

in the last syntax the to_table variable is not defined. The error we were seeing is that this was getting passed into rails as a nil and then transformed into an empty string, and then failing when the migration ran:

```
Caused by:
ArgumentError: Table '<table_name>' has no foreign key for  (ArgumentError)

            raise(ArgumentError, "Table '#{from_table}' has no foreign key for #{to_table || options}")
                  ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
```

### Additional information

#proper_table_name has been around for a long time. I can't think of any reason why we'd ever want to return an empty string instead of a nil if we don't pass in a name, but there may be a use case. None of the tests failed when I made this change. 

I added a test with a migration for `remove_foreign_key` versioned to Rails 7.0 because that's where I saw this migration originally fail. However, if I version that migration to 8.0, it fails as well.

### Checklist

Before submitting the PR make sure the following are checked:

* [x] This Pull Request is related to one change. Unrelated changes should be opened in separate PRs.
* [x] Commit message has a detailed description of what changed and why. If this PR fixes a related issue include it in the commit message. Ex: `[Fix #issue-number]`
* [x] Tests are added or updated if you fix a bug or add a feature.
* [ ] CHANGELOG files are updated for the changed libraries if there is a behavior change or additional feature. Minor bug fixes and documentation changes should not be included.
rails/rails#55823.title: Backwards compatibility in migrations with remove_foreign_key syntax

rails/rails#55834.body:
### Background

While working on https://github.com/Shopify/maintenance_tasks/pull/1276, I discovered that SQLite's migration behavior differs from other database adapters. The `change_column` method should behave consistently across all adapters.

### Steps to reproduce

<!-- (Guidelines for creating a bug report are [available
here](https://edgeguides.rubyonrails.org/contributing_to_ruby_on_rails.html#creating-a-bug-report)) -->

<!-- Paste your executable test case created from one of the scripts found [here](https://edgeguides.rubyonrails.org/contributing_to_ruby_on_rails.html#create-an-executable-test-case) below: -->
```ruby
require "bundler/inline"

gemfile(true) do
  source "https://rubygems.org"
  gem "logger"
  # gem "rails", path: "."
  gem "rails", github: "rails/rails", branch: "main"
  gem "sqlite3"
end

require "active_record"
require "minitest/autorun"
require "logger"

ActiveRecord::Base.establish_connection(adapter: "sqlite3", database: ":memory:")
ActiveRecord::Base.logger = Logger.new(STDOUT)

ActiveRecord::Schema.define do
  create_table :posts, force: true do |t|
    t.integer :user_id, null: false, default: 0
  end
end

class Post < ActiveRecord::Base
end

class BugTest < Minitest::Test
  def test_change_column_preserves_constraints
    connection = ActiveRecord::Base.connection

    # Change column type
    connection.change_column :posts, :user_id, :bigint

    column = connection.columns(:posts).find { |c| c.name == "user_id" }

    # These assertions fail from Rails 7.x (commit: 8db97a70460a42b12b61a3fe4cd57f26d3f95a3d)
    assert_equal false, column.null, "NOT NULL constraint should be preserved"
    assert_equal 0, column.default, "DEFAULT value should be preserved"
  end
end
```

### Expected behavior

The change_column method should preserve existing column options (NOT NULL, DEFAULT, limit, etc.) when they are not explicitly specified in the method call.


### Actual behavior

On SQLite, change_column drops all existing column options that are not explicitly specified, including NOT NULL constraints and DEFAULT values.

### System configuration

**Rails version**:  Rails version: 7.0+

**Ruby version**:  Ruby version: 3.x

### Root cause

This issue was introduced in commit 8db97a70460 which fixed precision settings for datetime columns. The fix changed change_column to not use existing column options, which had the side effect of dropping constraints.

### Proposed fix

Preserve existing column options and merge using with_defaults().

```diff
diff --git a/activerecord/lib/active_record/connection_adapters/sqlite3/schema_definitions.rb b/activerecord/lib/active_record/connection_adapters/sqlite3/schema_definitions.rb
index 59852cfc56d..00b8d884109 100644
--- a/activerecord/lib/active_record/connection_adapters/sqlite3/schema_definitions.rb
+++ b/activerecord/lib/active_record/connection_adapters/sqlite3/schema_definitions.rb
@@ -1,19 +1,21 @@
 # frozen_string_literal: true
 
 module ActiveRecord
   module ConnectionAdapters
     module SQLite3
       # = Active Record SQLite3 Adapter \Table Definition
       class TableDefinition < ActiveRecord::ConnectionAdapters::TableDefinition
         def change_column(column_name, type, **options)
           name = column_name.to_s
+          existing_column = @columns_hash[name]
+          options = options.with_defaults(existing_column.options) if existing_column
           @columns_hash[name] = nil
           column(name, type, **options)
         end
 
         def references(*args, **options)
           super(*args, type: :integer, **options)
         end
         alias :belongs_to :references
 
         def new_column_definition(name, type, **options) # :nodoc:
```
rails/rails#55834.title: change_column drops existing column options (NOT NULL, DEFAULT, etc.) on SQLite

rails/rails#55853.body:
### Steps to reproduce 1. Prepare `Gemfile` as follows: ```Gemfile.rb gem "activesupport" gem "json", group: :development ``` 2. Utilize dependabot version updates for `bundler`. ### Expected behavior When the prefix development is enabled, by default, https://docs.github.com/en/code-security/dependabot/working-with-dependabot/dependabot-options-reference#prefix-development, the title of dependabot's version update PRs should be `chore(deps): Bump json from 2.14.1 to 2.15.0`, which helps developers pay attentions to potential non-development impact. ### Actual behavior The title of dependabot's version update PRs is now like `chore(deps-dev): Bump json from 2.14.1 to 2.15.0`, which can cause misunderstanding about the scope of the potential impact. ### System configuration **Rails version**: n/a (8.0.x, 8.1.0.beta etc.) **Ruby version**: 3.2, 3.3, 3.4, 3.5.preview ## Why _Originally posted in https://github.com/rails/rails/pull/55847#issuecomment-3376931280_ I am not on listing every stdgem at all. `json` gem has been unique in terms of the gems installed via `rails new` (see [Gemfile.tt](https://github.com/rails/rails/blob/v8.1.0.beta1/railties/lib/rails/generators/rails/app/templates/Gemfile.tt)). Per [@Earlopain's list](https://github.com/rails/rails/pull/55847#issuecomment-3375605643), we will have four gems as follows: ``` # grep -E '^ (cgi|date|digest|erb|fileutils|ipaddr|net-http|open-uri|pp|psych|shellwords|tempfile|time|tmpdir|weakref|zlib) ' < Gemfile.lock date (3.4.1) erb (5.0.3) pp (0.6.3) psych (5.2.6) ``` `json` gem is the only gem on that gems in `group: :development` depend: ``` ## date rails -> actiontext -> action_text-trix -> railties -> irb -> rdoc -> psych -> date importmap-rails -> railties -> irb -> rdoc -> psych -> date rails -> railties -> irb -> rdoc -> psych -> date solid_cable -> railties -> irb -> rdoc -> psych -> date solid_cache -> railties -> irb -> rdoc -> psych -> date solid_queue -> railties -> irb -> rdoc -> psych -> date stimulus-rails -> railties -> irb -> rdoc -> psych -> date turbo-rails -> railties -> irb -> rdoc -> psych -> date web-console -> railties -> irb -> rdoc -> psych -> date debug -> irb -> rdoc -> psych -> date rails -> actionmailbox -> mail -> net-imap -> date rails -> actionmailer -> mail -> net-imap -> date ## erb rails -> actiontext -> action_text-trix -> railties -> irb -> rdoc -> erb importmap-rails -> railties -> irb -> rdoc -> erb rails -> railties -> irb -> rdoc -> erb solid_cable -> railties -> irb -> rdoc -> erb solid_cache -> railties -> irb -> rdoc -> erb solid_queue -> railties -> irb -> rdoc -> erb stimulus-rails -> railties -> irb -> rdoc -> erb turbo-rails -> railties -> irb -> rdoc -> erb web-console -> railties -> irb -> rdoc -> erb debug -> irb -> rdoc -> erb ## pp rails -> actiontext -> action_text-trix -> railties -> irb -> pp importmap-rails -> railties -> irb -> pp rails -> railties -> irb -> pp solid_cable -> railties -> irb -> pp solid_cache -> railties -> irb -> pp solid_queue -> railties -> irb -> pp stimulus-rails -> railties -> irb -> pp turbo-rails -> railties -> irb -> pp web-console -> railties -> irb -> pp debug -> irb -> pp ## psych rails -> actiontext -> action_text-trix -> railties -> irb -> rdoc -> psych importmap-rails -> railties -> irb -> rdoc -> psych rails -> railties -> irb -> rdoc -> psych solid_cable -> railties -> irb -> rdoc -> psych solid_cache -> railties -> irb -> rdoc -> psych solid_queue -> railties -> irb -> rdoc -> psych stimulus-rails -> railties -> irb -> rdoc -> psych turbo-rails -> railties -> irb -> rdoc -> psych web-console -> railties -> irb -> rdoc -> psych debug -> irb -> rdoc -> psych ``` ``` ## json rubocop-rails-omakase -> rubocop-performance -> rubocop -> json rubocop-rails-omakase -> rubocop-rails -> rubocop -> json rubocop-rails-omakase -> rubocop -> json selenium-webdriver -> json ``` This is why I think we should only add the json gem as a dependency to `activesupport.gemspec`. rails/rails#55856.body:
The s3_service exposes it's respective client and bucket attributes publicly. I think this makes sense in the context of active storage, as it allows users to rely on the same adapter & configs that the framework is using for Activestorage without having to duplicate your own client and ensure it matches the original, which can be modified at runtime, in order to perform operations outside of the scope of ActiveStorage.

This commit serves to make the same true of the gcs_service, which currently defined it's respective client & bucket as private method definitions.

I did a bit of git snooping and didn't see any mention of this being an intentional decision when first released.

* [X] This Pull Request is related to one change. Unrelated changes should be opened in separate PRs.
rails/rails#55856.title: Refactor gcs_service public interface to bring more in line with s3_service

rails/rails#55866.body:
[A mere add_foreign_key can wipe out your whole Rails+SQLite production table](https://kyrylo.org/software/2025/09/27/a-mere-add-foreign-key-can-wipe-out-your-whole-rails-sqlite-production-table.html)

We likely should be disabling and re-enabling the `foreign_keys` PRAGMA before/after the transaction to change the table per the [sqlite docs](https://www.sqlite.org/lang_altertable.html#otheralter).

Edit: actually it looks like we already do that since https://github.com/rails/rails/commit/45881b0a64b4039e3b0d27e2c4d959dc2d744b9d

Edit2: Ah, this is complex. We currently do

```
transaction do
  disable_referential_integrity do
```

but it should be

```
disable_pragma_foreign_keys do
  transaction do
    enable_defer_foreign_keys do
```

because `PRAGMA foreign_keys`

> is a no-op within a transaction; foreign key constraint enforcement may only be enabled or disabled when there is no pending [BEGIN](https://www.sqlite.org/lang_transaction.html) or [SAVEPOINT](https://www.sqlite.org/lang_savepoint.html).

per [doc](https://www.sqlite.org/pragma.html#pragma_foreign_keys), and "`PRAGMA foreign_keys` before `TRANSACTION`" is the order recommended in the first sqlite doc link above. However, `PRAGMA defer_foreign_keys` is [set _per transaction_](https://www.sqlite.org/pragma.html#pragma_defer_foreign_keys) so it has to be inside the transaction block.
rails/rails#55866.title: Migrations that alter SQLite table structure can cause data loss

rails/rails#55900.body:
### Motivation / Background

This Pull Request has been created because framework log subscribers currently consume notification events. Since the introduction of structured event subscribers in https://github.com/rails/rails/pull/55690, this doesn't necessarily need to happen anymore. We can instead use the structured event subscribers to produce logs instead for environments like development.

### Detail

This Pull Request adds a base class for framework structured event log subscribers, and changes current log subscribers (private API since https://github.com/rails/rails/pull/55755) to consume structured events instead of notification events.

### Checklist

Before submitting the PR make sure the following are checked:

* [x] This Pull Request is related to one change. Unrelated changes should be opened in separate PRs.
* [x] Commit message has a detailed description of what changed and why. If this PR fixes a related issue include it in the commit message. Ex: `[Fix #issue-number]`
* [x] Tests are added or updated if you fix a bug or add a feature.
* [x] CHANGELOG files are updated for the changed libraries if there is a behavior change or additional feature. Minor bug fixes and documentation changes should not be included.
rails/rails#55900.title: Make framework log subscribers to consume structured events

rails/rails#55908.body:
### Steps to reproduce

```ruby
# frozen_string_literal: true

require "bundler/inline"

gemfile(true) do
  source "https://rubygems.org"

  gem "rails"
  # If you want to test against edge Rails replace the previous line with this:
  # gem "rails", github: "rails/rails", branch: "main"

  gem "sqlite3"
end

require "active_record/railtie"
require "minitest/autorun"

# This connection will do for database-independent bug reports.
ENV["DATABASE_URL"] = "sqlite3::memory:"

class TestApp < Rails::Application
  config.load_defaults Rails::VERSION::STRING.to_f
  config.eager_load = false
  config.logger = Logger.new($stdout)
  config.secret_key_base = "secret_key_base"

  config.active_record.encryption.primary_key = "primary_key"
  config.active_record.encryption.deterministic_key = "deterministic_key"
  config.active_record.encryption.key_derivation_salt = "key_derivation_salt"
end
Rails.application.initialize!

ActiveRecord::Schema.define do
  create_table :posts, force: true do |t|
    t.string :state
  end
end

class Post < ActiveRecord::Base
  enum :state, %w[pending published]
end

class BugTest < ActiveSupport::TestCase
  def test_enum_null_values
    pending_post = Post.create!(state: "pending")
    published_post = Post.create!(state: "published")
    null_state_post = Post.create!(state: nil)

    assert_equal [published_post], Post.published.sort_by(&:id)
    assert_equal [pending_post, null_state_post], Post.not_published.sort_by(&:id)
  end
end
```

### Expected behavior

I'd expect `.not_published` to include all posts that are not published, including the one with no state.

### Actual behavior

Due to how SQL works, null value is treated separately and only records where the value is present and not equal to the given value are returned.

### System configuration

**Rails version**: 8.0.3

**Ruby version**: 3.4.6
rails/rails#55908.title: Records with null enum values are omitted in `.not_` enum scopes

rails/rails#55915.body:
`shallow` resources option is not respected when `module` option is given on `main` and `8-1-stable`. `git bisect` is showing 3b4255e1800d0b53c96db5aeb88aaa66163f74d5 (#53689) as a source of this issue.

### Steps to reproduce

<!-- (Guidelines for creating a bug report are [available
here](https://edgeguides.rubyonrails.org/contributing_to_ruby_on_rails.html#creating-a-bug-report)) -->

<!-- Paste your executable test case created from one of the scripts found [here](https://edgeguides.rubyonrails.org/contributing_to_ruby_on_rails.html#create-an-executable-test-case) below: -->
```ruby
# frozen_string_literal: true

require "bundler/inline"

gemfile(true) do
  source "https://rubygems.org"

  # gem "rails"
  # If you want to test against edge Rails replace the previous line with this:
  gem "rails", github: "rails/rails", branch: "8-1-stable"
end

require "action_controller/railtie"
require "minitest/autorun"
require "rack/test"

class TestApp < Rails::Application
  config.load_defaults Rails::VERSION::STRING.to_f
  config.root = __dir__
  config.eager_load = false
  config.hosts << "example.org"
  config.secret_key_base = "secret_key_base"

  config.logger = Logger.new($stdout)
end
Rails.application.initialize!

Rails.application.routes.draw do
  resources :evaluators do
    resources :metrics, module: :evaluators, shallow: true
  end
end

class BugTest <  ActionDispatch::IntegrationTest
  include Rack::Test::Methods

  def test_shallow_route
    assert_equal url_for(controller: "evaluators/metrics", action: :update, id: 1, only_path: true), "/metrics/1"
  end

  private
    def app
      Rails.application
    end
end
```

### Expected behavior

Following routes should be generated:

```
   evaluator_metrics GET    /evaluators/:evaluator_id/metrics(.:format)     evaluators/metrics#index
                     POST   /evaluators/:evaluator_id/metrics(.:format)     evaluators/metrics#create
new_evaluator_metric GET    /evaluators/:evaluator_id/metrics/new(.:format) evaluators/metrics#new
         edit_metric GET    /metrics/:id/edit(.:format)                     evaluators/metrics#edit
              metric GET    /metrics/:id(.:format)                          evaluators/metrics#show
                     PATCH  /metrics/:id(.:format)                          evaluators/metrics#update
                     PUT    /metrics/:id(.:format)                          evaluators/metrics#update
                     DELETE /metrics/:id(.:format)                          evaluators/metrics#destroy
```

### Actual behavior

<!-- Tell us what happens instead -->
```
    evaluator_metrics GET    /evaluators/:evaluator_id/metrics(.:format)          evaluators/metrics#index
                      POST   /evaluators/:evaluator_id/metrics(.:format)          evaluators/metrics#create
 new_evaluator_metric GET    /evaluators/:evaluator_id/metrics/new(.:format)      evaluators/metrics#new
edit_evaluator_metric GET    /evaluators/:evaluator_id/metrics/:id/edit(.:format) evaluators/metrics#edit
     evaluator_metric GET    /evaluators/:evaluator_id/metrics/:id(.:format)      evaluators/metrics#show
                      PATCH  /evaluators/:evaluator_id/metrics/:id(.:format)      evaluators/metrics#update
                      PUT    /evaluators/:evaluator_id/metrics/:id(.:format)      evaluators/metrics#update
                      DELETE /evaluators/:evaluator_id/metrics/:id(.:format)      evaluators/metrics#destroy

```

```
url_for(controller: "evaluators/metrics", action: :update, id: 1, only_path: true) =>
ActionController::UrlGenerationError: No route matches {action: "update", controller: "evaluators/metrics", id: 1}
```

### System configuration

**Rails version**:  `main` and `8-1-stable`

**Ruby version**:  `3.4.6`

rails/rails#55917.body:
According to the documentation, `ActiveRecord::QueryMethods#with` accepts SQL literals using Arel.

However, only instances of `Arel::Nodes::SqlLiteral` are accepted. Instances of `Arel::Nodes::BoundSqlLiteral` are not supported. When bind value parameters are supplied, an `ArgumentError` exception is raised.

### Steps to reproduce

* Create a SQL literal with bind value parameters using `Arel.sql`
* Pass the literal to `#with`.
* Execute the query.

<!-- (Guidelines for creating a bug report are [available
here](https://edgeguides.rubyonrails.org/contributing_to_ruby_on_rails.html#creating-a-bug-report)) -->

```ruby
# frozen_string_literal: true

require "bundler/inline"

gemfile(true) do
  source "https://rubygems.org"
  gem "rails"
  gem "sqlite3"
end

require "active_record/railtie"
require "minitest/autorun"

ENV["DATABASE_URL"] = "sqlite3::memory:"

class TestApp < Rails::Application
  config.load_defaults Rails::VERSION::STRING.to_f
  config.eager_load = false
  config.logger = Logger.new($stdout)
  config.secret_key_base = "secret_key_base"

  config.active_record.encryption.primary_key = "primary_key"
  config.active_record.encryption.deterministic_key = "deterministic_key"
  config.active_record.encryption.key_derivation_salt = "key_derivation_salt"
end
Rails.application.initialize!

ActiveRecord::Schema.define do
  create_table :some_records, force: true do |t|
    t.integer :some_value, null: false
  end
end

class SomeRecord < ActiveRecord::Base
end

class BugTest < ActiveSupport::TestCase
  def test_sql_literal
    value = 42
    cte = Arel.sql("SELECT * FROM some_records WHERE some_value = #{value}")
    assert_instance_of Arel::Nodes::SqlLiteral, cte

    record = SomeRecord.create(some_value: value)
    relation = SomeRecord.with(cte: cte).from("cte AS some_records")
    assert_equal [record], relation.to_a
  end

  def test_bound_sql_literal
    value = 43
    cte = Arel.sql("SELECT * FROM some_records WHERE some_value = ?", value)
    assert_instance_of Arel::Nodes::BoundSqlLiteral, cte

    record = SomeRecord.create(some_value: value)
    relation = SomeRecord.with(cte: cte).from("cte AS some_records")
    assert_equal [record], relation.to_a
  end
end
```

### Expected behavior

The query should return.

### Actual behavior

An `ArgumentError` exception is raised.

### System configuration

**Rails version**: 8.0.3
**Ruby version**: 3.4.7
rails/rails#55917.title: Common Table Expressions raise ArgumentError with bound SQL literal

rails/rails#55935.body:
When resolving an association's class name, if a nested class (e.g., `Company::Department`) shares its demodulized name with a top-level module (e.g., `module Department` ), ActiveRecord raises an `ArgumentError` instead of correctly resolving the nested class.

This appears to happen in `ActiveRecord::Reflection::MacroReflection#_klass`. The code attempts to resolve the class name at the top level (e.g., `::Department`). This finds the top-level `module Department`, which is not an `ActiveRecord::Base subclass`, triggering an `ArgumentError`.

I believe this behavior is a regression introduced by https://github.com/rails/rails/pull/53969. This behavior worked correctly in our application up to Rails 8.0.3, so we would like to resolve this if it's not the intended behavior.

I will open a PR shortly.

### Steps to reproduce

<!-- (Guidelines for creating a bug report are [available
here](https://edgeguides.rubyonrails.org/contributing_to_ruby_on_rails.html#creating-a-bug-report)) -->

<!-- Paste your executable test case created from one of the scripts found [here](https://edgeguides.rubyonrails.org/contributing_to_ruby_on_rails.html#create-an-executable-test-case) below: -->
```ruby
# frozen_string_literal: true

require "bundler/inline"

gemfile(true) do
  source "https://rubygems.org"

  gem "rails", github: "rails/rails", branch: "main"
  gem "sqlite3"
end

require "active_record/railtie"
require "minitest/autorun"

# This connection will do for database-independent bug reports.
ENV["DATABASE_URL"] = "sqlite3::memory:"

class TestApp < Rails::Application
  config.load_defaults Rails::VERSION::STRING.to_f
  config.eager_load = false
  config.logger = Logger.new($stdout)
  config.secret_key_base = "secret_key_base"

  config.active_record.encryption.primary_key = "primary_key"
  config.active_record.encryption.deterministic_key = "deterministic_key"
  config.active_record.encryption.key_derivation_salt = "key_derivation_salt"
end
Rails.application.initialize!

ActiveRecord::Schema.define do
  create_table :departments, force: true do |t|
  end

  create_table :department_hierarchies, force: true do |t|
    t.references :department
  end
end

module Company
end

module Department
end

class Company::Department < ActiveRecord::Base
  has_many :department_hierarchies, class_name: "Company::DepartmentHierarchy"
  has_many :child_departments, through: :department_hierarchies, source: :department
end

class Company::DepartmentHierarchy < ActiveRecord::Base
  belongs_to :department
end

class BugTest < ActiveSupport::TestCase
  def test_association_stuff
    Company::DepartmentHierarchy.create!(department: Company::Department.new)

    assert Company::Department.first.child_departments
  end
end

```

### Expected behavior

The `child_departments` association should be loaded correctly, resolving to `Company::Department`.

### Actual behavior

The test fails with an `ArgumentError`.

Here's a screenshot from when those marks were working: (That example is the 3rd code block in [this section](https://edgeguides.rubyonrails.org/getting_started.html#showing-individual-products). When visiting that page at the time of writing, the blue marks are not working) _I apologise for just complaining about this in comments/issues rather than making a PR myself. But I don't have enough CSS knowledge to know what the one true fix is, that solves both the problems with unnecessary-scroll-bars and lack-of-blue-marks simultaneously._

rails/rails#55984.body:
The new option `action_view.remove_hidden_field_autocomplete` does mostly what it says on the tin, but calling `hidden_field` on a form object appears to be overlooked and still unconditionally produces an autocomplete attribute.

[offending code](https://github.com/rails/rails/blob/7061d801307ed15fab582fc8f139c7a145d5ee0d/actionview/lib/action_view/helpers/tags/hidden_field.rb#L8)
Previous issue & fix: https://github.com/rails/rails/issues/55209 https://github.com/rails/rails/pull/55336

### Steps to reproduce

```ruby
class Application < Rails::Application
  config.action_view.remove_hidden_field_autocomplete = true
end

# in an erb template
<%= form_for :user do |form| %>
  <%= form.hidden_field :name %>
<% end %>
```

### Expected behavior

`<form><input type="hidden" name="user[name]" id="user_name" /></form>`

### Actual behavior

`<form><input autocomplete="off" type="hidden" name="user[name]" id="user_name" /></form>`

### System configuration

**Rails version**: 8.1.0

**Ruby version**: 3.4.4
rails/rails#55984.title: `autocomplete="off"` is not removed from hidden fields everywhere

rails/rails#56005.body:
When a circular reference is triggered, all subsequent attempts to encode any ruby hash fail with the same `JSON::NestingError`

The fix for now is to initialize a new coder each time there is an encoding attempt.

A better fix probably would be on the `json` gem so that the same JSON::Coder instance can be called multiple times even when one call raises a `JSON::NestingError`

cc @byroot 

### Detail

More details can be found here https://github.com/rails/rails/commit/90616277e3d8fc46c9cf35d6a7470ff1ea0092f7#r168752947

### Additional information

In my production app, this made all of my Sidekiq jobs on the same ruby process fail, even internal ActiveStorage job such as preview jobs started failing.
I fixed the issue by restarting the dyno.

- But this issue is really wild. This means if for some reason, my code or any gems in my application triggers a circular reference ==> the entire JSON generation of valid ruby hashes would fail for the entire duration of the ruby process until shutdown or restart!



### Checklist

Before submitting the PR make sure the following are checked:

* [x] This Pull Request is related to one change. Unrelated changes should be opened in separate PRs.
* [x] Commit message has a detailed description of what changed and why. If this PR fixes a related issue include it in the commit message. Ex: `[Fix #issue-number]`
* [x] Tests are added or updated if you fix a bug or add a feature.
* [ ] CHANGELOG files are updated for the changed libraries if there is a behavior change or additional feature. Minor bug fixes and documentation changes should not be included.
rails/rails#56005.title: Fix subsequent call to AS::JSON.encode after a nesting error

rails/rails#56011.body:
### Steps to reproduce

1. Create and deploy a Rails app with Kamal like so:

    ```bash
    rails -v # => Rails 8.1.0
    rails new myapp; cd myapp
    bin/rails generate scaffold Banana name
    sed -i '' 's/192.168.0.1/your_server_ip_here/' config/deploy.yml
    git add --all && git commit -m "Initial commit"
    bin/kamal setup
    ```

2. If the app deployed successfully, go to `http://your_server_ip_here/bananas/new`, fill in a name, and click on "Create Banana".

### Expected behavior

App deploy is successful, and you should be redirected to the `http://your_server_ip_here/bananas` index page.

### Actual behavior

#### 1. Default behavior

You are not redirected to the index page because of a `HTTP Origin header (http://<redacted_ip>) didn't match request.base_url (https://<redacted_ip>)` error.

Details according to `bin/kamal logs -n 1000`:


```
^L2025-10-26T01:29:18.061589870Z [b3c26d1f-105e-4d61-918c-3ff2139b0784] Started POST "/bananas" for 70.181.179.63 at 2025-10-26 01:29:18 +0000
2025-10-26T01:29:18.065376048Z [b3c26d1f-105e-4d61-918c-3ff2139b0784] Processing by BananasController#create as TURBO_STREAM
2025-10-26T01:29:18.065394623Z [b3c26d1f-105e-4d61-918c-3ff2139b0784]   Parameters: {"authenticity_token" => "[FILTERED]", "banana" => {"name" => "aaa"}, "commit" => "Create Banana"}
2025-10-26T01:29:18.066183467Z [b3c26d1f-105e-4d61-918c-3ff2139b0784] HTTP Origin header (http://<redacted_ip>) didn't match request.base_url (https://<redacted_ip>)
2025-10-26T01:29:18.067836134Z [b3c26d1f-105e-4d61-918c-3ff2139b0784] Completed 422 Unprocessable Content in 2ms (ActiveRecord: 0.0ms (0 queries, 0 cached) | GC: 0.0ms)
2025-10-26T01:29:18.070152157Z [b3c26d1f-105e-4d61-918c-3ff2139b0784]
2025-10-26T01:29:18.070169480Z [b3c26d1f-105e-4d61-918c-3ff2139b0784] ActionController::InvalidAuthenticityToken (HTTP Origin header (http://<redacted_ip>) didn't match request.base_url (https://<redacted_ip>)):

```


#### 2. Behavior if you comment out `config.assume_ssl = true` in `config/environments/production.rb`, but keep `config.force_ssl = true`

App deploy eventually fails with the following error message:

```
Releasing the deploy lock...
  Finished all in 115.7 seconds
  ERROR (SSHKit::Command::Failed): Exception while executing on host 5.78.111.110: docker exit status: 1
docker stdout: Nothing written
docker stderr: Error: target failed to become healthy within configured timeout (30s)
```

#### 3. Behavior if you keep `config.assume_ssl = true` in `config/environments/production.rb`, but comment out `config.force_ssl = true`

Same as 1. You are not redirected to the index page because of a `HTTP Origin header (http://<redacted_ip>) didn't match request.base_url (https://<redacted_ip>)` error.

Details according to `bin/kamal logs -n 1000`:

```
^L2025-10-26T01:49:42.724978982Z [3489b80f-3565-4893-97a3-b4deb11746c1] Started POST "/bananas" for 70.181.179.63 at 2025-10-26 01:49:42 +0000
2025-10-26T01:49:42.726951163Z [3489b80f-3565-4893-97a3-b4deb11746c1] Processing by BananasController#create as TURBO_STREAM
2025-10-26T01:49:42.726964689Z [3489b80f-3565-4893-97a3-b4deb11746c1]   Parameters: {"authenticity_token" => "[FILTERED]", "banana" => {"name" => "test"}, "commit" => "Create Banana"}
2025-10-26T01:49:42.727887622Z [3489b80f-3565-4893-97a3-b4deb11746c1] HTTP Origin header (http://<redacted_ip>) didn't match request.base_url (https://<redacted_ip>)
2025-10-26T01:49:42.728315444Z [3489b80f-3565-4893-97a3-b4deb11746c1] Completed 422 Unprocessable Content in 1ms (ActiveRecord: 0.0ms (0 queries, 0 cached) | GC: 0.0ms)
2025-10-26T01:49:42.732193103Z [3489b80f-3565-4893-97a3-b4deb11746c1]
2025-10-26T01:49:42.732233999Z [3489b80f-3565-4893-97a3-b4deb11746c1] ActionController::InvalidAuthenticityToken (HTTP Origin header (http://<redacted_ip>) didn't match request.base_url (https://<redacted_ip>)):

```

### Additional notes

Commenting out both `config.assume_ssl = true` and `config.force_ssl = true` works. Here is a PR to make this the default when Kamal is enabled: https://github.com/rails/rails/pull/56010.

### System configuration

**Rails version**: 8.1.0

**Ruby version**: 3.4.5
