rails/rails#56024.body:
### Steps to reproduce

```sh
rails new folder --name=sample --devcontainer
cd folder
code .
<re-open in devcontainer>
```

After the devcontainer builds, you'll get an error message: "Workspace does not exist. Select another workspace to continue".

The "select" option does not show the `folder` with your rails app.

I think this was adjusted in #54631, and may break devcontainer for users who have an app name different from the folder name they're developing in.

### Expected behavior

The devcontainer starts successfully and shows my files.

### Actual behavior

The devcontainer returns an error that a workspace cannot be found.  It prompts me to select a folder, showing my app name as an option - however that folder is empty when picked.

### System configuration

**Rails version**:  8.1.0

**Ruby version**:  3.3.9
rails/rails#56024.title: devcontainer: workspace does not exist when app name != folder name

rails/rails#56038.body:
### Steps to reproduce
I run this command from CLI:
```bash
bin/rails generate model account identifier:string{32}:uniq name:string{255} join_token:token:uniq
```

> [!NOTE]
> Yes, I use `uniq` on token column definition... Sorry.

and I get this migration file:
```ruby
class CreateAccounts < ActiveRecord::Migration[8.1]
  def change
    create_table :accounts do |t|
      t.string :identifier, limit: 32, null: false
      t.string :name, limit: 255, null: false
      t.string :join_token, limit: 32, null: false

      t.timestamps
    end
    add_index :accounts, :join_token, unique: true, unique: true
    add_index :accounts, :identifier, unique: true
    add_index :accounts, :join_token, unique: true
  end
end
```

> [!NOTE]
> `limit` argument on `join_token` column is touched by myself

> [!WARNING]
> `join_token` has twice `unique` arguments and additional index

### Expected behavior

The command must generate a migration like this:

```ruby
class CreateAccounts < ActiveRecord::Migration[8.1]
  def change
    create_table :accounts do |t|
      t.string :identifier, limit: 32, null: false
      t.string :name, limit: 255, null: false
      t.string :join_token, limit: 32, null: false

      t.timestamps
    end
    add_index :accounts, :identifier, unique: true
    add_index :accounts, :join_token, unique: true
  end
end
```

### Actual behavior

Redundant `join_token` index definition with redundant `unique` argument.

```ruby
class CreateAccounts < ActiveRecord::Migration[8.1]
  def change

rails/rails#56047.body:
### Description

When two models share the same database table and have associations with the same name but different `class_name` values, Rails incorrectly instantiates all records using the first association's class when using nested hash syntax with `includes()`.

### Steps to Reproduce

```ruby
require 'bundler/inline'

gemfile(true) do
  source 'https://rubygems.org'
  gem 'rails', '~> 7.2'
  gem 'sqlite3'
end

require 'active_record'

ActiveRecord::Base.establish_connection(adapter: 'sqlite3', database: ':memory:')
ActiveRecord::Base.logger = nil

ActiveRecord::Schema.define do
  create_table :custom_fields, force: true do |t|
    t.integer :origin_field_id
  end

  create_table :custom_field_options, force: true do |t|
    t.integer :custom_field_id
  end
end

class SurveyField < ActiveRecord::Base
  self.table_name = 'custom_fields'
  has_many :options, foreign_key: :custom_field_id, class_name: 'SurveyFieldOption'
  belongs_to :origin_field, class_name: 'CustomField', optional: true
end

class SurveyFieldOption < ActiveRecord::Base
  self.table_name = 'custom_field_options'
end

class CustomField < ActiveRecord::Base
  self.table_name = 'custom_fields'
  has_many :options, class_name: 'CustomFieldOption'
end

class CustomFieldOption < ActiveRecord::Base
  self.table_name = 'custom_field_options'
end

# Create test data
origin = CustomField.create!
CustomFieldOption.create!(custom_field_id: origin.id)

survey = SurveyField.create!(origin_field_id: origin.id)
SurveyFieldOption.create!(custom_field_id: survey.id)

# This returns WRONG class for origin_field.options
result = SurveyField.includes(options: [], origin_field: { options: [] }).first
puts result.origin_field.options.first.class
# => SurveyFieldOption (WRONG - should be CustomFieldOption)

# This works correctly
result = SurveyField.includes(:options, origin_field: :options).first
puts result.origin_field.options.first.class
# => CustomFieldOption (CORRECT)
```

### Expected Behavior

```ruby
result = SurveyField.includes(options: [], origin_field: { options: [] }).first
result.origin_field.options.first.class
# => CustomFieldOption
```

### Actual Behavior

```ruby
result = SurveyField.includes(options: [], origin_field: { options: [] }).first
result.origin_field.options.first.class
# => SurveyFieldOption (WRONG!)
```

### Root Cause

The bug is in `ActiveRecord::Associations::Preloader::Batch#group_and_load_similar` (activerecord/lib/active_record/associations/preloader/batch.rb:41):

```ruby
def group_and_load_similar(loaders)
  loaders.grep_v(ThroughAssociation).group_by(&:loader_query).each_pair do |query, similar_loaders|
    query.load_records_in_batch(similar_loaders)
  end
end
```

The method groups loaders by `loader_query`, which checks table name, connection, and query values, but **NOT the target class** (`klass`). This causes associations that query the same table to be batched together even when they should instantiate different classes.

The `LoaderQuery#eql?` method (activerecord/lib/active_record/associations/preloader/association.rb) compares:
- `association_key_name`
- `scope.table_name`
- `scope.connection_specification_name`
- `scope.values_for_queries`

But it doesn't compare `scope.klass`, leading to incorrect batching.

### System Information

- Rails version: 7.2.3 (affects 7.x)
- Ruby version: 3.2.9
- **Regression introduced in**: Rails 7.0.0 (works correctly in Rails 6.1.7.10)

### Breaking Commit

This regression was introduced by:

- **Commit**: `20b9bb1de075ebaa17137db2abdd64cc9b394aae`
- **Title**: "Intelligent batch preloading"
- **Date**: April 1, 2021
- **Authors**: John Hawthorn (@jhawthorn) and Dinah Shi
- **PR**: Added intelligent batching to optimize queries by grouping similar associations

The new batching system groups queries by table name and foreign key but doesn't account for different model classes that share the same table (STI scenarios). When `SurveyFieldOption` and `CustomFieldOption` share the `custom_field_options` table, the batcher incorrectly uses the first class encountered for all records in the batch.

### Proposed Fix

The `LoaderQuery#eql?` and `#hash` methods should include the target class in their comparison:

```ruby
def eql?(other)
  association_key_name == other.association_key_name &&
    scope.table_name == other.scope.table_name &&
    scope.klass == other.scope.klass &&  # ADD THIS LINE
    scope.connection_specification_name == other.scope.connection_specification_name &&
    scope.values_for_queries == other.scope.values_for_queries
end

def hash
  [association_key_name, scope.table_name, scope.klass, scope.connection_specification_name, scope.values_for_queries].hash
end
```

Alternatively, modify `group_and_load_similar` to group by both query and klass as shown in the workaround above.

### Full Reproduction Repository

Complete reproduction with tests (both with and without proposed fix) and documentation: https://github.com/d4be4st/rails-includes-issue
rails/rails#56047.title: Incorrect class instantiation with nested includes() on associations with same table name

rails/rails#56065.body:
## Motivation

The `zeitwerk:check` rake task has existed since Rails 6 (April 2019) but is not included in the default CI configuration added in Rails 7.2.

While `config.eager_load = ENV["CI"].present?` causes tests to eager load in CI, a dedicated `zeitwerk:check` provides:

- **Fail-fast feedback**: ~2s check before running the full test suite
- **Explicit validation**: Clear CI step showing autoloading is verified
- **Exhaustive checking**: Tests might not exercise all autoload paths

## Real-World Impact

This check would have caught production crashes we experienced with:
- Missing Zeitwerk inflector rules for acronyms (MCP → Mcp)
- Files not defining expected constants

See upstream PRs for examples:
- https://github.com/parruda/swarm/pull/163 (MCP inflector fix)
- https://github.com/crmne/ruby_llm/pull/486 (Railtie eager loading fix)

## Implementation

Adds `zeitwerk:check` to:
1. **GitHub Actions workflow** (`lint` job, after rubocop)
2. **bin/ci script** (after Style: Ruby step)

Both are structural validation checks that fail fast before running expensive tests.

## Testing

- Added test assertion in `bin_ci_test.rb`
- Updated `CHANGELOG.md`
rails/rails#56065.title: Add zeitwerk:check to default CI workflow and bin/ci

rails/rails#56090.body:
Custom serializers appear to be broken in Rails 8.1. The approach detailed in the Rails guides adds the custom serializers to the config, but does so **after** the Rails initialization process calls `ActiveJob::Serializers.add_serializers Rails.application.config.active_job.custom_serializers`. The result is that `ActiveJob::Serializers` array is out of sync with the config.

### Steps to reproduce

Repro here: https://github.com/brian-kephart/serializers
This repro is a basic implementation of custom serializers per the Rails guides. There are a couple of alternative approaches in comments that can be evaluated as well.

### Expected behavior

Serialization should succeed

### Actual behavior

`ActiveJob::SerializationError` when trying to use custom serializers

### System configuration

**Rails version**: 8.1.0, 8.1.1
rails/rails#56090.title: Custom serializer implementation from guides doesn't work in Rails 8.1

rails/rails#56112.body:
### Steps to reproduce

```
tz = ActiveSupport::TimeZone["America/New_York"]
twz = ActiveSupport::TimeWithZone.new(nil, tz, DateTime.new(2025, 11, 7, 12))
puts twz.xmlschema
```

the script will output `2025-11-07T12:00:00+00:0-05:00`

### Expected behavior

The output will be in proper iso8601 format.

### Actual behavior

The output contains an extra `+00:0` between the time section and the actual timezone offset.

This issue was introduced in https://github.com/rails/rails/pull/55583. That PR assumes that the local `time.xmlschema` will always end in `Z`, which is not true for `DateTime` objects

### System configuration

Rails 8.1.1
Ruby 3.4.7
rails/rails#56112.title: ActiveSupport::TimeWithZone#xmlschema broken when used with utc DateTime

### Steps to reproduce

```ruby
require "bundler/inline"

gemfile(true) do
  source "https://rubygems.org"

  # gem "rails", "~> 8.0.0" # OK
  gem "rails", "~> 8.1.0" # NG
  # If you want to test against edge Rails replace the previous line with this:
  # gem "rails", github: "rails/rails", branch: "main"
  gem "sqlite3"
end

require "action_controller/railtie"
require "active_record/railtie"
require "minitest/autorun"
require "rack/test"

ENV["DATABASE_URL"] = "sqlite3::memory:"

class TestApp < Rails::Application
  config.load_defaults Rails::VERSION::STRING.to_f
  config.root = __dir__
  config.eager_load = false
  config.hosts << "example.org"
  config.secret_key_base = "secret_key_base"

  config.logger = Logger.new($stdout)
end
Rails.application.initialize!


ActiveRecord::Schema.define do
  create_table :posts, force: true do |t|
  end
end

class Post < ActiveRecord::Base
  has_many :comments
end

Rails.application.routes.draw do
  get "/", to: "test#index"
end

class TestController < ActionController::Base
  include Rails.application.routes.url_helpers

  def index
    5.times { Post.where(id: 1).to_a }
    head :ok
  end
end

ActiveSupport::Notifications.subscribe "process_action.action_controller" do |name, start, finish, id, payload|
  $cached_queries_count = payload[:cached_queries_count]
end

class BugTest < ActiveSupport::TestCase
  include Rack::Test::Methods

  def test_cached_queries_count
    get "/"
    assert_equal 4, $cached_queries_count
  end

  private

  def app
    Rails.application
  end
end
```

### Expected behavior

The reported `cached_queries_count` should be incremented each time a query hit the cache. (worked fine on Rails 8.0)

### Actual behavior

`cached_queries_count` is always 0.

rails/rails#56186.body:
### Steps to reproduce
There are two HTTP forwarded headers: [X-Forwarded-For](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Reference/Headers/X-Forwarded-For) and [Forwarded](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Reference/Headers/Forwarded). 
Rack prioritizes the newer [Forwarded](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Reference/Headers/Forwarded) over  [X-Forwarded-For](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Reference/Headers/X-Forwarded-For), so if both headers are set, [Forwarded](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Reference/Headers/Forwarded) is used. 

The remote client IP is currently computed [here](https://github.com/rails/rails/blob/6388aec724df438bb7ed6909a710fcbd88bd45a3/actionpack/lib/action_dispatch/middleware/remote_ip.rb#L129-L169) using the `Client-IP` header and the effective forwarded header.
`ActionDispatch::RemoteIp::IpSpoofAttackError` is raised with the following message if the Client-IP value is absent in the effective forwarded list:

```
IP spoofing attack?! HTTP_CLIENT_IP="1.1.1.1" HTTP_X_FORWARDED_FOR="3.3.3.3, 4.4.4.4, 1.1.1.1"
```

The problem is that [X-Forwarded-For](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Reference/Headers/X-Forwarded-For) is always used in the error message, even when the effective header is [Forwarded](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Reference/Headers/Forwarded). This can be confusing (especially when investigating these errors) if the headers have different values or if only `Forwarded` is set. In the latter case, the following is currently printed:

```
IP spoofing attack?! HTTP_CLIENT_IP="1.1.1.1" HTTP_X_FORWARDED_FOR=nil
```

### Expected behavior
When the [Forwarded](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Reference/Headers/Forwarded) header is present and the `IpSpoofAttackError` condition is met, the `IpSpoofAttackError` error message should include the `Forwarded` header value. So in this case, error should ideally be something like this:

```
IP spoofing attack?! HTTP_CLIENT_IP="1.1.1.1" HTTP_FORWARDED="for=2.2.2.2, for=3.3.3.3"
```
 

### Actual behavior
The `X-Forwarded-For` header value is used in the error message always.

### Executable Test Case

```ruby
# frozen_string_literal: true

require "bundler/inline"

gemfile(true) do
  source "https://rubygems.org"

  gem "rails", "8.1.1"
  gem "debug"
  # If you want to test against edge Rails replace the previous line with this:
  # gem "rails", github: "rails/rails", branch: "main"
end

require "action_controller/railtie"
require "minitest/autorun"
require "rack/test"
require "debug"

class TestApp < Rails::Application
  config.load_defaults Rails::VERSION::STRING.to_f
  config.root = __dir__
  config.eager_load = false
  config.hosts << "example.org"
  config.secret_key_base = "secret_key_base"

  config.logger = Logger.new($stdout)
end
Rails.application.initialize!

Rails.application.routes.draw do
  get "/", to: "test#index"
end

class TestController < ActionController::Base
  include Rails.application.routes.url_helpers

  def index
    render plain: "Home"
  end
end

class BugTest < ActiveSupport::TestCase
  include Rack::Test::Methods

  def test_returns_the_right_error_message
    assert_raises(
      ActionDispatch::RemoteIp::IpSpoofAttackError,
      match: /HTTP_CLIENT_IP="127.0.0.1" HTTP_FORWARDED="for=2.2.2.2, for=3.3.3.3"/
    ) do  
      get("/", {}, {
          "HTTP_X_FORWARDED_FOR" => "1.1.1.1, 2.2.2.2",
          "HTTP_FORWARDED" => "for=2.2.2.2, for=3.3.3.3",
          "HTTP_CLIENT_IP" => "127.0.0.1",
      })
    end
  end

  private
  
  def app
    Rails.application
  end
end
```

### System configuration

**Rails version**:  8.1.1

**Ruby version**: 3.3.5
rails/rails#56186.title: ActionDispatch::RemoteIp::IpSpoofAttackError error message is misleading when the `Forwarded` header is present

rails/rails#56230.body:
In the past, `ActiveRecord::Base.logger = Logger.new($stdout)` would cause SQL queries to be logged to stdout. It seems like that stopped working after 54264bde59c6e327694ad8b3923d4ebda60bd562. Was that intentional? 

(I noticed because I use that setup in test, in order to keep track of what _exact_ SQL queries the system runs.)

### Steps to reproduce

```sh
# Works, prints SQL as expected:
$ SHA=9834097fc6cdbb4aff8185728a2166d25fce867b ruby rails_test.rb
9834097fc6cdbb4aff8185728a2166d25fce867b
D, [2025-11-25T08:03:47.612325 #40396] DEBUG -- :   Book Load (0.0ms)  SELECT "books".* FROM "books"

# Doesn't work, doesn't print anything
$ SHA=54264bde59c6e327694ad8b3923d4ebda60bd562 ruby rails_test.rb
54264bde59c6e327694ad8b3923d4ebda60bd562
```


```ruby
# rails_test.rb
require "bundler/inline"
sha = ENV["SHA"]
puts sha

gemfile do
  gem "rails", github: "rails/rails", ref: sha
  gem "sqlite3"
  gem "logger"
end
require "active_record"

# Set up the database for the example
ActiveRecord::Base.establish_connection({ adapter: "sqlite3", database: ":memory:" })
ActiveRecord::Schema.define do
  self.verbose = false
  create_table :books do |t|
    t.string :title
  end
end

class Book < ActiveRecord::Base
end

ActiveRecord::Base.logger = Logger.new($stdout)

Book.all.to_a

# Should also print the SQL:
Fiber.new { Book.all.to_a }
```

### Expected behavior

I expect SQL to be logged to stdout.

### Actual behavior

Nothing is logged to stdout

### System configuration

**Rails version**: ` gem "rails", github: "rails/rails", ref: "54264bde59c6e327694ad8b3923d4ebda60bd562"`

**Ruby version**:  `ruby 3.4.1 (2024-12-25 revision 48d4efcb85) +PRISM [arm64-darwin24]`
rails/rails#56230.title: ActiveRecord::Base.logger = ... doesn't work on main

If this guide is supposed to no longer exist and should be removed from the website. ### Actual behavior It has the no css look And the copy buttons do not work

rails/rails#56239.body:
### Steps to reproduce

Partial (works when locals listed on one line)
```erb
# app/views/_example.html.erb
<%# locals: (arg_1:, arg_2: nil, arg_3: []) %>

<%= arg_1 %>
<%= arg_2 %>
<%= arg_3 %>
```

Call:
```erb
<%= render 'example', arg_1: 'First' %>
```
Result: renders as expected. ✅

---

Partial (fails when locals are split across lines)
```erb
# app/views/_example.html.erb
<%# locals: (arg_1:,
             arg_2: nil,
             arg_3: []) %>

<%= arg_1 %>
<%= arg_2 %>
<%= arg_3 %>
```

Call:
```erb
<%= render 'example', arg_1: 'First' %>
```
Result: exception raised. ❌

---

Notes:
- Minimal repro uses only ERB partial + render.
- Appears to be a parser/annotation bug in how the `locals:` comment is tokenized when split across lines.

### Expected behavior

comment-parsed locals should behave identically regardless of line breaks; unspecified locals should evaluate to their default values.

### Actual behavior

Raises when evaluating `<%= arg_2 %>` unless all locals are explicitly passed.
```erb
ActionView::Template::Error: undefined local variable or method `arg_2' for #<ActionView::Base:0x0000000098d5f0>
```

### System configuration

**Rails version**: 7.2.2.2

**Ruby version**: 3.2.2 (2023-03-30 revision e51014f9c0) [arm64-darwin22]
rails/rails#56239.title: ERB partial locals parsing fails when locals comment spans multiple lines

rails/rails#56241.body:
`TimeWithZone#as_json` returns US-ASCII encoded strings instead of UTF-8 in Rails 8.1.0, breaking JSON serialization for Active Record timestamp columns.

### Steps to reproduce

<!-- (Guidelines for creating a bug report are [available
here](https://edgeguides.rubyonrails.org/contributing_to_ruby_on_rails.html#creating-a-bug-report)) -->

<!-- Paste your executable test case created from one of the scripts found [here](https://edgeguides.rubyonrails.org/contributing_to_ruby_on_rails.html#create-an-executable-test-case) below: -->

```ruby
# frozen_string_literal: true

require "bundler/inline"

gemfile(true) do
  source "https://rubygems.org"

  # gem "rails", "8.0.4"
  gem "rails", "8.1.0"
end

require "active_support"
require "active_support/core_ext/object/blank"
require "minitest/autorun"

class BugTest < ActiveSupport::TestCase
  def test_active_support_time_with_zone_as_json_encoding
    assert_equal Encoding::UTF_8, Time.now.in_time_zone("Australia/Brisbane").as_json.encoding
  end
end
```

### Expected behavior

<!-- Tell us what should happen -->

Returns a UTF-8 String.

This happens on 8.0.4:
```
# Running:

.

Finished in 0.016225s, 61.6333 runs/s, 61.6333 assertions/s.

1 runs, 1 assertions, 0 failures, 0 errors, 0 skips
```

### Actual behavior

<!-- Tell us what happens instead -->

Returns a US-ASCII String.

This happens on 8.1.0:
```
# Running:

F

Finished in 0.019657s, 50.8725 runs/s, 50.8725 assertions/s.

  1) Failure:
BugTest#test_active_support_time_with_zone_as_json_encoding [test.rb:18]:
Expected: #<Encoding:UTF-8>
  Actual: #<Encoding:US-ASCII>

1 runs, 1 assertions, 1 failures, 0 errors, 0 skips
```

### System configuration

**Rails version**: 8.1.0

**Ruby version**: 3.4.7

cc @byroot I haven't done a proper bisect, but by mucking around in the app at work I'm pretty sure the regression was introduced in https://github.com/rails/rails/pull/55583/commits/4b27a2a498655e96f6f4b5e66b5cbec83191cd98

rails/rails#56277.body:
### Steps to reproduce
1. Create new rails app `rails new testing` or use application with rails version `>= 8.0` .
2. Create new model `rails g model User first_name:string` and migrate `rails db:migrate`
3. Go to `config/application.rb` or `config/development.rb` and set async_query_executor `config.active_record.async_query_executor = :global_thread_pool`
4. Run console and try run `User.limit(1).load_async`

### Expected behavior

Perform query and load user from a database.

### Actual behavior

Raises and exception `Can't perform asynchronous queries without a query session (ActiveRecord::ActiveRecordError)`

### System configuration

Rails version 8.1
Ruby version 3.4

### More info

I do not see this bug in rails 7.1 but It's present in rails 8.0 and 8.1.
Other queries like `async_pluck` etc. not working either.

### Workaround

In console run `ActiveRecord::Base.asynchronous_queries_tracker.start_session` - and async queries will start working.
rails/rails#56277.title: ActiveRecord: async query methods not working in console

rails/rails#56281.body:
### Steps to reproduce

<!-- (Guidelines for creating a bug report are [available
here](https://edgeguides.rubyonrails.org/contributing_to_ruby_on_rails.html#creating-a-bug-report)) -->

```ruby
# frozen_string_literal: true

require "bundler/inline"

gemfile(true) do
  source "https://rubygems.org"

  gem "rails", "~> 8"
  gem "sqlite3"
  gem "debug"
end

require "active_record/railtie"
require "minitest/autorun"

# This connection will do for database-independent bug reports.
ENV["DATABASE_URL"] = "sqlite3::memory:"

class TestApp < Rails::Application
  config.load_defaults Rails::VERSION::STRING.to_f
  config.eager_load = false
  config.logger = Logger.new($stdout)
  config.secret_key_base = "secret_key_base"
end
Rails.application.initialize!

ActiveRecord::Schema.define do
  create_table :posts, force: true
end

class Post < ActiveRecord::Base
end

class BugTest < ActiveSupport::TestCase
  def test_association_stuff
    Post.create!
    Post.create!
    
    begin 
      posts = Post.where("1=1").sole 
    rescue ActiveRecord::SoleRecordExceeded => e
      # assert_equal "ActiveRecord::Relation", e.record.class.name # Works in Rails 7
      assert_equal "Class", e.record.class.name # Works in Rails 8
    end
  end
end
```


<!-- Paste your executable test case created from one of the scripts found [here](https://edgeguides.rubyonrails.org/contributing_to_ruby_on_rails.html#create-an-executable-test-case) below: -->
```ruby
# Before

def something
  items = Item.where(role: :admin).sole
rescue ActiveRecord::SoleRecordExceeded => e
  e.record # Before 8.x, this includes only the first two items from the AR collection
end
```

```ruby
# After

def something
  items = Item.where(role: :admin).sole
rescue ActiveRecord::SoleRecordExceeded => e
  e.record # Now it contains the model itself, so the collection differs
end
```

### Expected behavior

In the latest 7.x release, the `#sole` method raised an `ActiveRecord::SoleRecordExceeded` exception using `self` as the argument, whereas in 8.x it now passes `model` instead.

* 7.x: https://github.com/rails/rails/blob/v7.2.3/activerecord/lib/active_record/relation/finder_methods.rb#L149
* 8.x: https://github.com/rails/rails/blob/v8.0.4/activerecord/lib/active_record/relation/finder_methods.rb#L151

This behavior change was introduced in the following commit: https://github.com/rails/rails/commit/fd5bd98b34774ec8ccb0fc649d5325b9ac9a875e


### Actual behavior

I couldn’t find any mention of this change in the changelogs, and it should be documented because it affects how we use this collection. Also, since we now only receive the model itself, we lose scopes and other collection filters, which could potentially lead to data leaks, what do you think?

### System configuration

**Rails version**: 8.x

**Ruby version**: 3.4.6 (but doesn't matter here)

rails/rails#56284.body:

rails/rails#56291.body:
Hello,

apologies if this is a duplicate, or this is not the correct repository to report this.

I've just run into this issue because of the newly released connection pool 3: https://github.com/mperham/connection_pool/blob/main/Changes.md#300

I've also tested against main branch of redis-client

### Steps to reproduce

1. Ensure connection_pool is >= 3.0
2. Set `config.cache_store = :redis_cache_store`

Relevant commit: https://github.com/diowa/ruby3-rails8-flowbite-render/commit/ace94779a3a1bb58ef4ac30b69ff7591a6fabc5a

### Expected behavior

Rails (`c`onsole or `s`erver) starts

### Actual behavior

Rails does not start (tested also against latest main)

```
~/.rvm/gems/ruby-3.4.7/gems/connection_pool-3.0.1/lib/connection_pool.rb:48:in 'initialize': wrong number of arguments (given 1, expected 0) (ArgumentError)
	from ~/.rvm/gems/ruby-3.4.7/bundler/gems/rails-0f3585ee229f/activesupport/lib/active_support/cache/redis_cache_store.rb:163:in 'Class#new'
	from ~/.rvm/gems/ruby-3.4.7/bundler/gems/rails-0f3585ee229f/activesupport/lib/active_support/cache/redis_cache_store.rb:163:in 'ActiveSupport::Cache::RedisCacheStore#initialize'
	from ~/.rvm/gems/ruby-3.4.7/bundler/gems/rails-0f3585ee229f/activesupport/lib/active_support/cache.rb:91:in 'Class#new'
	from ~/.rvm/gems/ruby-3.4.7/bundler/gems/rails-0f3585ee229f/activesupport/lib/active_support/cache.rb:91:in 'ActiveSupport::Cache.lookup_store'
```

### System configuration

**Rails version**: 8.1.1 / main

**Ruby version**: 3.4.7

### Workaround

Keep connection pool at 2.x
rails/rails#56291.title: Connection Pool >= 3.x, AS's `redis_cache_store` raises an ArgumentError

rails/rails#56301.body:
We're migrating from Rails 8.0 to Rails 8.1

Our application makes use of scenic gem and materialized views in postgres. 

We have noted that with Rails 8.1, indexes dumped by ActiveRecord::SchemaDumper#indexes 
https://github.com/rails/rails/blob/38a74b851cb6cd170cd1c0eea32058857144b6d9/activerecord/lib/active_record/schema_dumper.rb#L231-L242

 are generating invalid ruby syntax


### Steps to reproduce

<!-- (Guidelines for creating a bug report are [available
here](https://edgeguides.rubyonrails.org/contributing_to_ruby_on_rails.html#creating-a-bug-report)) -->

<!-- Paste your executable test case created from one of the scripts found [here](https://edgeguides.rubyonrails.org/contributing_to_ruby_on_rails.html#create-an-executable-test-case) below: -->
```ruby
require "bundler/inline"

gemfile(true) do
  source "https://rubygems.org"

  gem "rails", '~> 8.1.0'
  gem "pg"

  # scenic adds `create_view` to schema and also would dump it to schema.rb,
  # but the issue is reroduceable without it
  # git_source(:github) { |repo| "https://github.com/#{repo}.git" }
  # gem "scenic", github: "scenic-views/scenic"
end

require "active_record"
require "minitest/autorun"
require "logger"

# Example for debian:
# $ sudo -su postgres psql
#   CREATE USER dumper_test WITH PASSWORD '123';
#   CREATE DATABASE dumper_test OWNER dumper_test
# $ export DATABASE_URL=postgres://dumper_test:123@localhost/
ActiveRecord::Base.establish_connection(ENV.fetch('DATABASE_URL'))
ActiveRecord::Base.logger = Logger.new(STDOUT)

module SchemaDumperWithView
  # Stripped down version of scenic dumper: https://github.com/scenic-views/scenic/blob/main/lib/scenic/schema_dumper.rb

  def tables(stream)
    super
    views(stream)
  end

  def views(stream)
    dumpable_views_in_database = %w[cities_v] # Hardcoded from test setup below

    stream.puts

    dumpable_views_in_database.each do |view_name|
      stream.puts("  # Fake view definition for #{view_name}")
      indexes(view_name, stream)
    end
  end
end

ActiveRecord::SchemaDumper.prepend SchemaDumperWithView
ActiveRecord.dump_schemas = %w[public]

class BugTest < Minitest::Test
  def setup
    ActiveRecord::Schema.define do
      create_schema "heroku_ext", force: true

      create_table :addresses, force: true do |t|
        t.string :country_code, limit: 2
        t.string :city, null: false
        t.string :postal_code, limit: 10
        t.string :street_address
      end

      execute "DROP MATERIALIZED VIEW IF EXISTS cities_v"

      execute <<~SQL.squish
        CREATE MATERIALIZED VIEW cities_v AS
        SELECT DISTINCT country_code, city FROM addresses
        WITH NO DATA
      SQL

      add_index :cities_v, %w[country_code city]
    end
  end

  def teardown
    ActiveRecord::Schema.define do
      execute "DROP MATERIALIZED VIEW IF EXISTS cities_v"
      drop_table :addresses
      drop_schema "heroku_ext"
    end
  end

  def test_dump_index
    pool = ActiveRecord::Base.connection_pool
    output, = capture_io {  ActiveRecord::SchemaDumper.dump(pool) }

    # Proper quoting should be "add_index "public.cities_v", ["country_code", "city"], ...
    assert_match %r{add_index "public.cities_v",}, output
  end
end

```

### Expected behavior

The test should pass, and the resulting schema should look like this:
```ruby
ActiveRecord::Schema[8.1].define(version: 0) do
  create_schema "heroku_ext"

  # These are extensions that must be enabled in order to support this database
  enable_extension "pg_catalog.plpgsql"


  # Fake view definition for cities_v
  create_table "public.addresses", force: :cascade do |t|
    t.string "city", null: false
    t.string "country_code", limit: 2
    t.string "postal_code", limit: 10
    t.string "street_address"
  end

  # Fake view definition for cities_v
  # <-- Note that schema and view/table name are quoted together
  add_index "public.cities_v", ["country_code", "city"], name: "index_cities_v_on_country_code_and_city"

end
```

### Actual behavior

The test fails, producing the following output for schema:

```ruby
ActiveRecord::Schema[8.1].define(version: 0) do
  create_schema "heroku_ext"

  # These are extensions that must be enabled in order to support this database
  enable_extension "pg_catalog.plpgsql"


  # Fake view definition for cities_v
  create_table "public.addresses", force: :cascade do |t|
    t.string "city", null: false
    t.string "country_code", limit: 2
    t.string "postal_code", limit: 10
    t.string "street_address"
  end

  # Fake view definition for cities_v
  # <-- Note bare `public` schema name
  add_index public."cities_v", ["country_code", "city"], name: "index_cities_v_on_country_code_and_city"

end
```
<!-- Tell us what happens instead -->

### System configuration

**Rails version**: 
8.0.1

**Ruby version**: 
3.4.7
rails/rails#56301.title: SchemaDumper in Rails 8.1 incorrectly dumps index names in Postgres

rails/rails#56320.body: When creating new Rails 8.1.1 app with GitHub Actions CI & Dependabot flags enabled & pushing the code to GitHub -> Dependabot immediately opens pull request: **_Bump actions/checkout from 5 to 6._** This issue might be confusing to new Rails users. ### Steps to reproduce 1. run rails 8.1.1 new generator without disabling GitHub Actions CI & Dependabot flags: ```ruby rails _8.1.1_ new app_name ``` 2. git commit & push initial version after generation: ```ruby git add . git commit -m "[1] - init: Rails 8.1.1, Ruby 3.4.1," git push -u origin main ``` 3. Dependabot opens Pull Request in fresh repository to update Actions/Checkout version to 6 in **github/workflows/ci.yml:** ### Expected behavior - No Pull Request is open by Dependabot on fresh repository - CI runs on latest Actions/Checkout version ### Actual behavior - Dependabot opens Pull Request in fresh repository to update Actions/Checkout version to 6 - CI runs on outdated Actions/Checkout version ### System configuration **Rails version**: 8.1.1 **Ruby version**: 3.4.1
rails/rails#56320.title: Newly generated Rails 8.1.1 app triggers Dependabot PR to update CI actions/checkout version

rails/rails#56339.body:
### Steps to reproduce

```
require "bundler/inline"

gemfile(true) do
source "https://rubygems.org"
    gem "rails", "~> 8.1.0"
    gem "i18n"
end


require "active_support"
require "active_support/core_ext/string/inflections"
require "action_view"

# Configure I18n with a custom locale that falls back to :en
I18n.available_locales = [:en, :custom]
I18n.fallbacks = I18n::Locale::Fallbacks.new(custom: :en)
I18n.locale = :custom

# Verify fallbacks are configured correctly
puts "I18n.locale: #{I18n.locale.inspect}"
puts "I18n.fallbacks[:custom]: #{I18n.fallbacks[:custom].inspect}"

# Test String#pluralize directly (works correctly)
puts "\nString#pluralize:"
puts "  'applicant'.pluralize => #{('applicant').pluralize.inspect}"
puts "  'applicant'.pluralize(:en) => #{('applicant').pluralize(:en).inspect}"
puts "  'applicant'.pluralize(:custom) => #{('applicant').pluralize(:custom).inspect}"

# Test ActionView pluralize helper (broken)
include ActionView::Helpers::TextHelper

puts "\nActionView pluralize helper:"
puts "  pluralize(1, 'applicant') => #{pluralize(1, 'applicant').inspect}"
puts "  pluralize(2, 'applicant') => #{pluralize(2, 'applicant').inspect}"
puts "  pluralize(2, 'applicant', locale: :en) => #{pluralize(2, 'applicant', locale: :en).inspect}"

# Expected: "2 applicants"
# Actual:   "2 applicant"
```
### Expected behavior

When I18n.locale is set to a custom locale (e.g., :custom) that falls back to :en via I18n.fallbacks,
the pluralize helper should use English pluralization rules:

pluralize(2, 'applicant') => "2 applicants"

### Actual behavior

The pluralize helper returns the singular form regardless of count:

pluralize(2, 'applicant') => "2 applicant"

Possible root cause: 

Commit https://github.com/rails/rails/commit/2ab34cd5141336432c3f56ec2a626a287819f6b6
introduced a performance optimization that stores :en inflections in @__en_instance__ instead of
@__instance__. However, `Inflections.instance_or_fallback` only checks @__instance__ when iterating
through fallbacks:

```
def self.instance_or_fallback(locale)
return @__en_instance__ ||= new if locale == :en

    I18n.fallbacks[locale].each do |k|
      return @__instance__[k] if @__instance__.key?(k)
    end
    instance(locale)
end
```

System configuration

Rails version: 8.1.1

Ruby version: 3.4.1
rails/rails#56339.title: pluralize helper ignores fallback to :en for custom locales since 8.1

rails/rails#56350.body:
### Motivation / Background

CSRF protection has always been a source of issues related to cached pages, stale tokens, the session being somehow cleared, all resulting in false positives. We still get these in our apps. 

This PR proposes to modernise this, with the goal of eventually deprecating and getting rid of CSRF tokens completely. It takes advantage of the `Sec-Fetch-Site` header, which is a fetch metadata request header that modern browsers send, and that indicates whether a request for a resource is coming from the same origin, the same site or a different site. The server can use this to decide whether the request should be allowed or not.

In this PR, this approach is used by default, in a very conservative and strict way: only requests with a `Sec-Fetch-Site` value of `same-site` or `same-origin` are allowed. Requests with a value of `cross-site` are rejected unless they come from a configured trusted origin (see below, in the Detail section). Requests that don't include a `Sec-Fetch-Site` header or that have it set to `none` are rejected as well. Assuming a modern browser, user-initiated requests (like direct navigation or clicking a bookmark) shouldn't cause a non-GET/HEAD request, so these wouldn't be subject to the CSRF check in any case. Requests that don't include the header could be:
- Not from a browser, in which case they wouldn't include the authenticity token either. These would be excluded from the check in another way.
- From a browser, but having the header stripped by some middle proxy or firewall (we've seen this in the wild). In this case, we can't allow the request without making the user vulnerable to CSRF.

This is what we're currently using in HEY, with a custom strategy class for unverified request handling. 

For apps that can't use this approach yet because they need to support older browsers that don't send the `Sec-Fetch-Site` header, the usual authenticity token approach is kept as a fallback. When a decision can't be clearly made with the header value because it's missing or 'none', the token is checked as before.

The `Origin` check is not changed; it continues to work in the same way, combined with the `Sec-Fetch-Site `or authenticity token checks.

### Detail
This PR introduces a new option, `using`, that can be passed to `protect_from_forgery`, to indicate the approach used for verifying requests. The possible values are `header_only`, which relies exclusively on the `Sec-Fetch-Site` header, or `header_or_legacy_token`, which uses the authenticity token when a decision can't be made with the header alone. The default would be `header_only` for Rails 8.2. Example: 

```ruby
class ApplicationController < ActionController::Base
  protect_from_forgery using: :header_or_legacy_token, with: :exception
```

When using the `header_or_legacy_token` approach, a warning is logged when the fallback is necessary. In this way, developers can check which requests don't include valid `Sec-Fetch-Site` headers and progressively fix them so that they can eventually stop using the CSRF token. 

A new option, `trusted_origins`, is also added to set trusted origins for legitimate cross-site requests (OAuth callbacks, third-party embeds):
 
```ruby
protect_from_forgery trusted_origins: %w[ https://accounts.google.com ]
```

I'll include some comments in the PR to point out other important details. 

### Additional information
Some references about this: 
- https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Reference/Headers/Sec-Fetch-Site
- https://web.dev/articles/fetch-metadata
- https://github.com/golang/go/issues/73626
- https://www.alexedwards.net/blog/preventing-csrf-in-go

### Open questions
Besides the obvious question of whether this is an acceptable approach for Rails and whether this makes sense with the current implementation, a possible open question is: should the values allowed for `Sec-Fetch-Site` be configurable? This would allow for an even stricter policy where `same-site` requests aren't allowed, only `same-origin`. This would prevent requests coming from a different subdomain, for example. It would also allow for a more relaxed policy where `none` or missing values are allowed because the risk of people running older browsers or some kind of proxy is deemed acceptable. 


### Checklist

Before submitting the PR make sure the following are checked:

* [x] This Pull Request is related to one change. Unrelated changes should be opened in separate PRs.
* [x] Commit message has a detailed description of what changed and why. If this PR fixes a related issue include it in the commit message. Ex: `[Fix #issue-number]`
* [x] Tests are added or updated if you fix a bug or add a feature.
* [x] CHANGELOG files are updated for the changed libraries if there is a behavior change or additional feature. Minor bug fixes and documentation changes should not be included.
rails/rails#56350.title: Use a modern approach for cross-site request forgery protection

rails/rails#56378.body:
### Steps to reproduce
The following standalone Ruby script reproduces the issue without requiring a full Rails application.
It establishes a PostgreSQL connection using Active Record with schema_search_path configured, verifies that the search path is correctly applied, then calls reconnect! and checks the search path again.

<!-- (Guidelines for creating a bug report are [available
here](https://edgeguides.rubyonrails.org/contributing_to_ruby_on_rails.html#creating-a-bug-report)) -->

<!-- Paste your executable test case created from one of the scripts found [here](https://edgeguides.rubyonrails.org/contributing_to_ruby_on_rails.html#create-an-executable-test-case) below: -->
```ruby
# Prerequisites:
# - PostgreSQL running
# - A database named `rails81_reconnect_test`
# - A schema named `foo`
require "bundler/inline"

gemfile(true) do
  source "https://rubygems.org"
  gem "rails", "8.1.1"
  gem "pg"
end

require "active_record"

ActiveRecord::Base.establish_connection(
  adapter: "postgresql",
  database: "rails81_reconnect_test",
  schema_search_path: "public,foo",
  host: 'localhost',
  username: 'postgres',
)

puts "Initial search_path:"
p ActiveRecord::Base.connection.execute("SHOW search_path").to_a

ActiveRecord::Base.connection.reconnect!

puts "Search_path after reconnect:"
p ActiveRecord::Base.connection.execute("SHOW search_path").to_a
```

### Expected behavior

After calling `ActiveRecord::Base.connection.reconnect!`, the PostgreSQL connection should reapply all connection-level configuration defined in database.yml, including schema_search_path.
I should get the same result.
```
Initial search_path:
[{"search_path" => "public, foo"}]
```

### Actual behavior

After calling reconnect!, the connection’s search_path resets to PostgreSQL’s default:
```
Search_path after reconnect:
[{"search_path" => "\"$user\", public"}]
```

The schema_search_path defined in database.yml is not reapplied to the new connection.

### System configuration

**Rails version**: 8.1.1

**Ruby version**: 3.4.5
rails/rails#56378.title: PostgreSQL schema_search_path from database.yml is not reapplied after reconnect! (Rails 8.1)

rails/rails#56388.body:
Not really a bug, but the guides mention to open an issue when something is wrong, so hope this is ok.

### Problem

The guide [Tuning Performance for Deployment](https://guides.rubyonrails.org/tuning_performance_for_deployment.html) refers to [jemalloc](https://github.com/jemalloc/jemalloc) for memory optimization, but its repository was archived on 2025-06-02.
The alternative way is described as "less efficient alternative", which brings up the question if it's still the correct way to use `jemalloc` or something alternative is suggested from now on?

### Expected behavior

The guide is updated according to new suggestion.

### References
- https://jasone.github.io/2025/06/12/jemalloc-postmortem/
rails/rails#56388.title: jemalloc still recommended?

rails/rails#56446.body:
## Problem
Rails 8.1.1 raises an `ArgumentError` when defining an enum with Float values, even though this worked in Rails 7.0.6, 7.2.3, 8.0.4
This appears to be an undocumented breaking change that affects existing applications with float enum values stored in the database.

This appears to have been added in 8.1.0
https://github.com/rails/rails/blob/1cdd190a25e483b65f1f25bbd0f13a25d696b461/activerecord/lib/active_record/enum.rb#L350-L357


I checked the changelog but couldn't find any description of changes related to enum.
https://github.com/rails/rails/releases/tag/v8.1.0
https://github.com/rails/rails/releases/tag/v8.1.1


## Expected behavior
Enum definitions with Float values should work as they did in previous versions (tested up to Rails 8.0.4), or at minimum, this breaking change should be documented in the CHANGELOG.


## Actual behavior
Rails 8.1.1 raises the following error:
ArgumentError: Enum values {foo: 0, bar: 0.5, baz: 1} must be only booleans, integers, symbols or strings, got: Float


## Steps to reproduce
```ruby
enum :option, {foo: 0, bar: 0.5, baz: 1}, prefix: true
```
This is a sample, but in my project, each option has a score assigned to it, and the scores are expected to include cases involving floating-point numbers.

When loading this model (e.g., during `rails db:migrate` or `rails db:seed`), the error occurs.

```console
ArgumentError: Enum values {foo: 0, bar: 0.5, baz: 1} must be only booleans, integers, symbols or strings, got: Float (ArgumentError)
```


## Environment
- Rails version: 8.1.1
- Ruby version: 3.4.8
- Database: SQLite3 (though this likely affects all databases)


## Impact
This is a breaking change that affects:
1. **Existing databases**: We have existing records with float values (0.5) stored in the database that cannot be changed
2. **Migration path**: There's no clear migration path documented for applications using float enum values
3. **Documentation**: This change is not mentioned in the Rails 8.1 CHANGELOG


## Questions
1. Is this an intentional breaking change?
2. If so, could this be documented in the CHANGELOG?
3. Is there a recommended migration path for applications with existing float enum values in the database?
4. Would it be possible to support Float values in enum, or provide a deprecation warning before removing support?


## Note
I am not fluent in English, and I had generative AI assist me in creating this issue.
If you need any additional information, I would be happy to help.
rails/rails#56446.title: Enum with Float values raises ArgumentError in Rails 8.1

rails/rails#56473.body:
As a consequence of [Wrap console command with an executor by default](https://github.com/rails/rails/pull/56297), console sessions are using query_cache enabled by default.

I know it can be disabled passing `--skip_executer` to console, or setting `ApplicationRecord.connection.query_cache.enabled = false` in a console initializer, but I wonder if this was expected or a side effect.
rails/rails#56473.title: Rails edge is enabling query_cache by default for console sessions. I wonder if it is intentional or a side effect

rails/rails#56481.body:
### Steps to reproduce

I've migrated from Rails 7.1.3 to 8.1.1 and I've now an error in a very specific case

When I combine `merge` and `none` and a filter on dates and the 2 dates are the same.

If I replace `none` by `where(id: [])` it works.


The error:

```
NoMethodError: undefined method 'equality?' for an instance of Arel::Nodes::SqlLiteral (NoMethodError)

            if !non_attrs.empty? && node.equality? && node.left.is_a?(Arel::Predications)
                                        ^^^^^^^^^^
Did you mean?  equal?
    from active_record/relation/where_clause.rb:185:in 'block in ActiveRecord::Relation::WhereClause#except_predicates'
    from active_record/relation/where_clause.rb:184:in 'reject'
    from active_record/relation/where_clause.rb:184:in 'except_predicates'
    from active_record/relation/where_clause.rb:27:in 'merge'
    from active_record/relation/merger.rb:179:in 'merge_clauses'
    from active_record/relation/merger.rb:75:in 'merge'
    from active_record/relation/spawn_methods.rb:47:in 'merge!'
    from active_record/relation/spawn_methods.rb:37:in 'merge'
    from lib/analytic/app/services/analytic/product_sales_service.rb:255:in 'perform'
```

Test case:

```
# frozen_string_literal: true

require "bundler/inline"

gemfile(true) do
  source "https://rubygems.org"

  gem "rails"
  # If you want to test against edge Rails replace the previous line with this:
  # gem "rails", github: "rails/rails", branch: "main"

  gem "sqlite3"
end

require "active_record/railtie"
require "minitest/autorun"

# This connection will do for database-independent bug reports.
ENV["DATABASE_URL"] = "sqlite3::memory:"

class TestApp < Rails::Application
  config.load_defaults Rails::VERSION::STRING.to_f
  config.eager_load = false
  config.logger = Logger.new($stdout)
  config.secret_key_base = "secret_key_base"

  config.active_record.encryption.primary_key = "primary_key"
  config.active_record.encryption.deterministic_key = "deterministic_key"
  config.active_record.encryption.key_derivation_salt = "key_derivation_salt"
end
Rails.application.initialize!

ActiveRecord::Schema.define do
  create_table :orders, force: true do |t|
    t.date :sent_date
    t.date :delivery_date
  end

  create_table :order_items, force: true do |t|
    t.belongs_to :order, null: false
  end
end

class Order < ActiveRecord::Base
  has_many :order_items
end

class OrderItem < ActiveRecord::Base
  belongs_to :order
end

class BugTest < ActiveSupport::TestCase
  # don't crash if start_date and end_date are different or if `none` is replaced by `where(id: [])`
  def test_association_stuff
    start_date = Date.parse("2025-12-30")
    end_date = Date.parse("2025-12-30")

    order = Order.create!(sent_date: start_date, delivery_date: end_date)
    order.order_items.create!

    orders_scope = Order.all
    orders_scope = orders_scope.where(
      Arel::Nodes::NamedFunction.new(
      'COALESCE',
      [Order.arel_table[:posted_date], Order.arel_table[:published_date]]
      ).between(start_date..end_date)
    )
    orders_scope = orders_scope.none

    assert_equal 0, OrderItem.joins(:order).merge(orders_scope).pluck(:id).size
  end
end
```

### Expected behavior

Not to raise an exception

### Actual behavior

Raise an exception

### System configuration

**Rails version**: 8.1.1

**Ruby version**: 3.4.8
rails/rails#56481.title: ActiveRecord - NoMethodError: undefined method 'equality?' for an instance of Arel::Nodes::SqlLiteral (NoMethodError)

rails/rails#56485.body:
### Steps to reproduce

```ruby
# frozen_string_literal: true

require "bundler/inline"

gemfile(true) do
  source "https://rubygems.org"

  gem "rails"
  # If you want to test against edge Rails replace the previous line with this:
  # gem "rails", github: "rails/rails", branch: "main"

  gem "sqlite3"
end

require "active_record/railtie"
require "minitest/autorun"

# This connection will do for database-independent bug reports.
ENV["DATABASE_URL"] = "sqlite3::memory:"

class TestApp < Rails::Application
  config.load_defaults Rails::VERSION::STRING.to_f
  config.eager_load = false
  config.logger = Logger.new($stdout)
  config.secret_key_base = "secret_key_base"
end
Rails.application.initialize!

class CreatePaymentsTable < ActiveRecord::Migration::Current # or use a specific version via `Migration[number]`
  def change
    create_table :payments, id: :integer, default: nil
  end
end

class Payment < ActiveRecord::Base
end

class BugTest < ActiveSupport::TestCase
  i_suck_and_my_tests_are_order_dependent!

  def test_1_migration_up_generate_the_expected_table_structure
    CreatePaymentsTable.migrate(:up)

    Payment.reset_column_information
    id_column = Payment.columns.find { it.name == "id" }

    assert id_column.rowid
    assert_not id_column.auto_increment?
  end

  def test_2_dump_output_include_default_null_for_integer_id_non_auto_increment_pk
    pool = ActiveRecord::Base.connection_pool
    output, = capture_io { ActiveRecord::SchemaDumper.dump(pool) }

    assert_match %r{create_table "payments".*(, default: nil).*}, output
  end
end
```

### Expected behavior

The dump file should have `default: nil` option.

### Actual behavior

The `default: nil` is ignored, making the DB structure inconsistent when restored from the schema file.

### System configuration

**Rails version**: 8.1.1

**Ruby version**: 4.0.0
rails/rails#56485.title: rails db:schema:dump is ignoring default: nil for non-autoincrement integer pks on Sqlite3 adapter

rails/rails#56520.body:
Hello Rails team,

First, thank you for the excellent guides! They've been incredibly helpful for learning Rails.

I'm writing about a small issue I encountered while following the product inventory management section. When adding the inventory_count field to existing products, the migration with default: 0 didn't update my existing records - they remained as nil.

Here's what I did:

Created Products without inventory_count

Added migration: add_column :products, :inventory_count, :integer, default: 0

Existing products kept inventory_count: nil while new products correctly got 0

The example code product.inventory_count.positive? failed with NoMethodError for existing products

The code I used (from guide):

erb
<% if product.inventory_count.positive? %>
I temporarily fixed it with:

erb
<% if product.inventory_count.to_i.positive? %>
I might be missing something - perhaps there's a recommended way to handle this that I overlooked? If so, I'd appreciate guidance.

Suggestion: Could the documentation either:

Mention that existing records need manual updates, or

Use nil-safe approaches like &.positive? or .to_i.positive?

This might help other beginners who encounter the same situation.

Thank you for your time and for maintaining such valuable resources!

Best regards,

Berkay Aksu
rails/rails#56520.title: Potential Documentation Improvement: Migration default values behavior with existing records

rails/rails#56537.body:
### Steps to reproduce

When `protect_from_forgery` is called with no argument, [`:null_session`](https://github.com/rails/rails/blob/f7008177810741cf992b0791b9dcfd2bf17abcc3/actionpack/lib/action_controller/metal/request_forgery_protection.rb#L268) is used, while by default every controller gets [`:exception`](https://github.com/rails/rails/blob/f7008177810741cf992b0791b9dcfd2bf17abcc3/actionpack/lib/action_controller/railtie.rb#L106-L108).

### Expected behavior

I'd expect both defaults to be the same.

### Actual behavior

Defaults are different, depending which part of the source code you are reading and might cause trouble when relying on one or the other default.

### System configuration

**Rails version**: edge

**Ruby version**: 3.4


### Notes

I don't know what is the better default, but I think it's worth unifying them. It's also worth mentioning the latest work done on https://github.com/rails/rails/pull/56350.
rails/rails#56537.title: Unify the default strategy for CSRF protection

rails/rails#56555.body:
### Steps to reproduce

Create tables and models where the `table_name` is provided with schemas and the schemas differ:

```ruby
class UserProfile < ApplicationRecord
  self.table_name = "usr.user_profiles" # defining the table_name is not required to reproduce
  ...
end

class EventLogAttribute < ApplicationRecord
  self.table_name = "hst.event_log_attributes"
  ...
end

class EventLog < ApplicationRecord
  self.table_name = "hst.event_logs"
  ...
end
```

Create migration
```ruby
class SomeMigration < ActiveRecord::Migration[8.1]
  create_table "usr.user_profiles" do |t|
    t.string "name"
  end

  create_table "hst.event_log" do |t|
    t.uuid "created_by_id"
    t.uuid "updated_by_id"
  end

  create_table "hst.event_log_attributes" do |t|
    t.uuid "created_by_id"
    t.uuid "updated_by_id"    
  end

  add_foreign_key "hst.event_log_attributes", "hst.event_logs"
  add_foreign_key "hst.event_log_attributes", "usr.user_profiles", column: "created_by_id"
  add_foreign_key "hst.event_log_attributes", "usr.user_profiles", column: "updated_by_id"
  add_foreign_key "hst.event_logs", "usr.user_profiles", column: "created_by_id"
  add_foreign_key "hst.event_logs", "usr.user_profiles", column: "updated_by_id"
end
```

Run migration and check schema.rb

### Expected behavior
`to_table` param should have correct name with schema:

```ruby
  add_foreign_key "hst.event_log_attributes", "hst.event_logs"
  add_foreign_key "hst.event_log_attributes", "usr.user_profiles", column: "created_by_id"
  add_foreign_key "hst.event_log_attributes", "usr.user_profiles", column: "updated_by_id"
  add_foreign_key "hst.event_logs", "usr.user_profiles", column: "created_by_id"
  add_foreign_key "hst.event_logs", "usr.user_profiles", column: "updated_by_id"
```

### Actual behavior
`to_table` param has schema from `from_table` appended before the `to_table` schema and table name.

```ruby
  add_foreign_key "hst.event_log_attributes", "hst.event_logs" # emits correctly because they're in the same schema (hst)
  add_foreign_key "hst.event_log_attributes", "hst.usr.user_profiles", column: "created_by_id" # emits hst.user.* when user.* is expected
  add_foreign_key "hst.event_log_attributes", "hst.usr.user_profiles", column: "updated_by_id"
  add_foreign_key "hst.event_logs", "hst.usr.user_profiles", column: "created_by_id"
  add_foreign_key "hst.event_logs", "hst.usr.user_profiles", column: "updated_by_id"
```

### System configuration

**Rails version**: 8.1.1
**Postgres version**: 15.2
**pg version**: 1.6.3
**Ruby version**: 3.3.4
rails/rails#56555.title: Rails 8.1.1 with pg 1.6.3 emits a schema.rb with malformed foreign keys when keys span tables in different schemas.

rails/rails#56573.body:
### Steps to reproduce

Before running the below script, you'll need to do a bit of local setup first to make sure MySQL is installed and the expected DB exists.
1. Ensure mysql is available and running .
   - I'm on Mac and `$ > brew install mysql && mysql.server start` worked for me.
2. Ensure the database expected by the test exists in mysql (or update the repro script to use an already existing database).
    ```
    $ > mysql -u root
    $ mysql > create database bulk_alter_rollback_test;
    ```

<!-- (Guidelines for creating a bug report are [available
here](https://edgeguides.rubyonrails.org/contributing_to_ruby_on_rails.html#creating-a-bug-report)) -->

<!-- Paste your executable test case created from one of the scripts found [here](https://edgeguides.rubyonrails.org/contributing_to_ruby_on_rails.html#create-an-executable-test-case) below: -->
```ruby
# frozen_string_literal: true

require "bundler/inline"

gemfile(true) do
  source "https://rubygems.org"

  gem "rails"
  gem "mysql2"
end

require "active_record/railtie"
require "minitest/autorun"

# This connection will do for database-independent bug reports.
ENV["DATABASE_URL"] = "mysql2://root@localhost/bulk_alter_rollback_test"

class TestApp < Rails::Application
  config.load_defaults Rails::VERSION::STRING.to_f
  config.eager_load = false
  config.logger = Logger.new($stdout)
  config.secret_key_base = "secret_key_base"
  config.active_record.table_name_prefix = "repro_"
end
Rails.application.initialize!


ActiveRecord::Schema.define do
  create_table :payments, force: true do |t|
    t.decimal :amount, precision: 10, scale: 0, default: 0, null: false
  end
end

class Payment < ActiveRecord::Base
end

class AddNoteToPayments < ActiveRecord::Migration::Current
  def change
    change_table :payments, bulk: true do |t|
      t.string :note
      t.index :note
    end
  end
end

class BulkChangeTableRollbackWithTablePrefixTest < ActiveSupport::TestCase
  def setup
    AddNoteToPayments.migrate(:up)
    Payment.reset_column_information
    assert Payment.column_names.include?("note")
  end

  def test_db_rollback
    AddNoteToPayments.migrate(:down)
    Payment.reset_column_information

    refute Payment.column_names.include?("note")
  end
end
```

### Expected behavior

`db:rollback` should work with the combination of a `table_name_prefix` and a migration using `change_table bulk: true`.  `db:migrate` works, and I would expect reverts to work as well.

### Actual behavior

<!-- Tell us what happens instead -->

The rollback fails because the table name prefix is not applied on the bulk table change statement.

```
Error: BulkChangeTableRollbackWithTablePrefixTest#test_db_rollback: ArgumentError: No indexes found on payments with the options provided.
```

The rollback in the test is trying to update the `payments` table, but should be referencing the `repro_payments` table.

I've done some digging on this and I believe [this line](https://github.com/rails/rails/blob/d7c8ae65b7045490965218a994c300aea8dbb079/activerecord/lib/active_record/migration.rb#L1051) which checks for revert is skipping the logic that would otherwise create the full table name including any configured table name prefix and suffix.  That was originally introduced [a good long time ago](https://github.com/rails/rails/commit/c5ba4896ab4b5d5c8c3d5f3eaf970b600c7a2017), and it's unclear if it's still needed or what might happen if it were removed.

**Why does this error occur specifically with `change_table bulk: true`?**

When the command recorder [executes a bulk change in change_table](https://github.com/rails/rails/blob/3b5b9a96a83c14a3fd9f0bc0a256e2084997d54c/activerecord/lib/active_record/migration/command_recorder.rb#L140-L145), it adds [a bulk_change_table command using the table name from closure](https://github.com/rails/rails/blob/3b5b9a96a83c14a3fd9f0bc0a256e2084997d54c/activerecord/lib/active_record/migration/command_recorder.rb#L145), which does not include the configured table_name_prefix due to the ☝️ exclusion when reverting.  When the recorder is replayed, the `bulk_change_table` command is then replayed and executed using the unprefixed (and unsuffixed) table name.

### System configuration

**Rails version**: Our production app hitting this problem is running Rails 7.2.3, but the test case is also failing locally with rails 8.1.2

**Ruby version**:  3.3.6
rails/rails#56573.title: Rolling back a migration with `change_table bulk:true` fails when `table_name_prefix` is configured

rails/rails#56608.body:
### Steps to reproduce

<!-- (Guidelines for creating a bug report are [available
here](https://edgeguides.rubyonrails.org/contributing_to_ruby_on_rails.html#creating-a-bug-report)) -->

<!-- Paste your executable test case created from one of the scripts found [here](https://edgeguides.rubyonrails.org/contributing_to_ruby_on_rails.html#create-an-executable-test-case) below: -->
```ruby
# frozen_string_literal: true

require "bundler/inline"

gemfile(true) do
  source "https://rubygems.org"

  gem "rails"
  # If you want to test against edge Rails replace the previous line with this:
  # gem "rails", github: "rails/rails", branch: "main"
end

require "active_support"
require "active_support/core_ext/array/grouping"
require "minitest/autorun"

class InGroupsValidationBugTest < ActiveSupport::TestCase
  def test_in_groups_with_zero_should_raise_argument_error
    error = assert_raises(ArgumentError) { [1, 2, 3].in_groups(0) }
    assert_match(/positive integer/, error.message)
  end

  def test_in_groups_with_negative_should_raise_argument_error
    error = assert_raises(ArgumentError) { [1, 2, 3].in_groups(-1) }
    assert_match(/positive integer/, error.message)
  end

  def test_in_groups_with_nil_should_raise_argument_error
    error = assert_raises(ArgumentError) { [1, 2, 3].in_groups(nil) }
    assert_match(/positive integer/, error.message)
  end

  def test_in_groups_with_float_should_convert_to_integer
    result = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5].in_groups(2.9) # 2.9.to_i => 2 groups
    assert_equal 2, result.size
  end
end
```

### Expected behavior

`Array#in_groups` should validate its input the same way `Array#in_groups_of` does.

<!-- Tell us what should happen -->

### Actual behavior

```ruby
in_groups(0)       # => ZeroDivisionError: divided by 0
in_groups(-1)      # => Returns [] silently (no error)
in_groups(nil)     # => TypeError: nil can't be coerced into Integer
in_groups(2.9)    # => NoMethodError: undefined method 'times' for an instance of Float
```

<!-- Tell us what happens instead -->

### System configuration

**Rails version**: 8.1.2

**Ruby version**: 3.3.4
rails/rails#56608.title: Array#in_groups missing input validation

### Steps to reproduce

here](https://edgeguides.rubyonrails.org/contributing_to_ruby_on_rails.html#creating-a-bug-report)) -->

<!-- Paste your executable test case created from one of the scripts found [here](https://edgeguides.rubyonrails.org/contributing_to_ruby_on_rails.html#create-an-executable-test-case) below: -->
```ruby
# frozen_string_literal: true

require "bundler/inline"

gemfile(true) do
  source "https://rubygems.org"
  gem "rails", github: "rails/rails", branch: "main"
end

require "action_view"
require "action_view/test_case"
require "minitest/autorun"

class TestView
  def self.render(context)
    user = context.instance_variable_get(:@user)
    if user.nil?
      "ERROR: @user is nil!"
    else
      "Hello, #{user[:name]}!"
    end
  end
end

class UserViewTest < ActionView::TestCase
  setup do
    @user = { name: "Alice" }
  end

  test "instance variable from setup is available in view" do
    # Simulate what render() does: pass view_assigns to the view
    view_context = Object.new
    view_assigns.each do |name, value|
      view_context.instance_variable_set(:"@#{name}", value)
    end

    result = TestView.render(view_context)
    puts "\n=== Result ==="
    puts result

    if result.include?("ERROR")
      puts "\n@user from setup was not passed to the view" # This is because _user_defined_ivars now subtracts @__setup_ivars
    end
  end
end

class UserViewTestWithWorkaround < ActionView::TestCase
  setup do
    @user = { name: "Bob" }
  end

  # Workaround: override _user_defined_ivars to restore old behavior
  def _user_defined_ivars
    instance_variables - ActionView::TestCase::Behavior::INTERNAL_IVARS
  end

  test "workaround: override _user_defined_ivars" do
    puts "\n=== With Workaround ==="
    puts "view_assigns: #{view_assigns.inspect}"

    view_context = Object.new
    view_assigns.each do |name, value|
      view_context.instance_variable_set(:"@#{name}", value)
    end

    result = TestView.render(view_context)
    puts result

    assert_equal "Hello, Bob!", result
  end
end
```

### Expected behavior

@user is available in the rendered partial (pre-#56476 behavior)

### Actual behavior

@user is nil in the view

cc: @byroot
rails/rails#56619.title: ActionView::TestCase no longer passes setup-defined instance variables to views

rails/rails#56642.body:

I've used minitest-bisect to find these issues, but it was removed in a recent pull request (https://github.com/rails/rails/pull/56207/changes). I attempted to use minitest-bisect to investigate a potentially order-dependent failure in build #125728 (https://buildkite.com/rails/rails/builds/125728#019bd05e-c299-4807-b4e4-675059a3d7fd/L1552), but it resulted in the following error:

rails/rails#56662.body:
### Steps to reproduce

```ruby
# frozen_string_literal: true

require "bundler/inline"

gemfile(true) do
  source "https://rubygems.org"

  # Activate the gem you are reporting the issue against.
  gem "activerecord", "~> 8.1" # Also happens on 7.2 (didn't test earlier as Trilogy wasn't available)
  gem "trilogy"
  gem "debug"
end

require "active_record"
require "logger"

module ActiveRecordCompositeKeyInversePatch
  private

  def matches_foreign_key?(record)
    fk = reflection.foreign_key

    unless fk.is_a?(Array)
      return super
    end

    pk = Array(reflection.association_primary_key)

    owner_fk_values = fk.map { |key| owner._read_attribute(key) }
    record_pk_values = pk.map { |key| record._read_attribute(key) }

    owner_fk_values == record_pk_values
  end
end

# Patching with the above module fixes the issue as composite keys
# ActiveRecord::Associations::Association.prepend(ActiveRecordCompositeKeyInversePatch)

logger = Logger.new(STDOUT)
ActiveRecord::Base.logger = logger

ActiveRecord::Base.establish_connection(
  adapter: "trilogy",
  database: "test",
  host: ENV.fetch("MYSQL_HOST"),
  port: ENV.fetch("MYSQL_PORT"),
  password: ENV.fetch("MYSQL_PASSWORD"),
  username: ENV.fetch("MYSQL_USER")
)

ActiveRecord::Schema.define do
  create_table :parents, force: true, primary_key: [:store_id, :id] do |t|
    t.integer :store_id, null: false
    t.integer :id, null: false
    t.string :last_name
  end

  create_table :children, force: true do |t|
    t.integer :store_id, null: false
    t.integer :parent_id, null: false
  end
end

class Parent < ActiveRecord::Base
  self.primary_key = :id

  has_many :children, inverse_of: :parent, foreign_key: :parent_id, primary_key: :id

  query_constraints :store_id, :id

  validates :id, presence: true
  validates :store_id, presence: true
end

class Child < ActiveRecord::Base
  belongs_to :parent, inverse_of: :children, foreign_key: [:store_id, :parent_id], primary_key: [:store_id, :id]

  validates :parent_id, presence: true
  validates :store_id, presence: true
end

parent = Parent.new
parent.store_id = 1
parent.id = 1
parent.last_name = "Smith"
parent.save!
parent.reload
logger.info("Parent: #{parent.to_json}")

child = Child.new
child.store_id = parent.store_id
child.parent_id = parent.id
child.save!

child = Child.first!
logger.info("Parent object_id: #{child.parent.object_id}")
logger.info("Navigation parent object_id: #{child.parent.children.first.parent.object_id}")
unless child.parent.object_id == child.parent.children.first.parent.object_id
  logger.error "inverse_of isn't set on child.parent.children.first.parent"
end

```

### Expected behavior

The `ActiveRecord::Associations::Association#inversable?` method works with composite keys, resulting in re-using the already loaded object instance instead of doing a new query and instantiating a new object.

The following queries would be executed:
```
D, [2026-01-22T12:31:49.505020 #53610] DEBUG -- :   Child Load (0.2ms)  SELECT `children`.* FROM `children` ORDER BY `children`.`id` ASC LIMIT 1
D, [2026-01-22T12:31:49.507674 #53610] DEBUG -- :   Parent Load (0.4ms)  SELECT `parents`.* FROM `parents` WHERE `parents`.`store_id` = 1 AND `parents`.`id` = 1 LIMIT 1
I, [2026-01-22T12:31:49.507980 #53610]  INFO -- : Parent object_id: 1960
D, [2026-01-22T12:31:49.509779 #53610] DEBUG -- :   Child Load (0.3ms)  SELECT `children`.* FROM `children` WHERE `children`.`parent_id` = 1 ORDER BY `children`.`id` ASC LIMIT 1
I, [2026-01-22T12:31:49.509846 #53610]  INFO -- : Navigation parent object_id: 1960
D, [2026-01-22T12:31:49.510247 #53610] DEBUG -- :   Child Load (0.3ms)  SELECT `children`.* FROM `children` WHERE `children`.`parent_id` = 1 ORDER BY `children`.`id` ASC LIMIT 1
```

### Actual behavior

The `ActiveRecord::Associations::Association#inversable?` method calls `matches_foreign_key?(record)`, but `matches_foreign_key?(record)` doesn't take into account composite keys:

```ruby
        def inversable?(record)
          record &&
            ((!record.persisted? || !owner.persisted?) || matches_foreign_key?(record))
        end

        def matches_foreign_key?(record)
          if foreign_key_for?(record)
            record.read_attribute(reflection.foreign_key) == owner.id ||
              (foreign_key_for?(owner) && owner.read_attribute(reflection.foreign_key) == record.id)
          else
            owner.read_attribute(reflection.foreign_key) == record.id
          end
        end
```

`record.read_attribute` ends up receiving `["store_id", "parent_id"]` which doesn't exist and returns `nil`:
```ruby
(rdbg) fk
=> ["store_id", "parent_id"]
(ruby) record.read_attribute(fk)
=> nil
```


ActiveRecord fails to determine the relationship is `inversable?`, and ends up triggering a new query for the Parent, and loads a new instance:
```
D, [2026-01-22T12:31:58.173841 #53687] DEBUG -- :   Child Load (0.2ms)  SELECT `children`.* FROM `children` ORDER BY `children`.`id` ASC LIMIT 1
D, [2026-01-22T12:31:58.177039 #53687] DEBUG -- :   Parent Load (0.4ms)  SELECT `parents`.* FROM `parents` WHERE `parents`.`store_id` = 1 AND `parents`.`id` = 1 LIMIT 1
I, [2026-01-22T12:31:58.177438 #53687]  INFO -- : Parent object_id: 1960
D, [2026-01-22T12:31:58.179559 #53687] DEBUG -- :   Child Load (0.2ms)  SELECT `children`.* FROM `children` WHERE `children`.`parent_id` = 1 ORDER BY `children`.`id` ASC LIMIT 1
D, [2026-01-22T12:31:58.180296 #53687] DEBUG -- :   Parent Load (0.2ms)  SELECT `parents`.* FROM `parents` WHERE `parents`.`store_id` = 1 AND `parents`.`id` = 1 LIMIT 1
I, [2026-01-22T12:31:58.180342 #53687]  INFO -- : Navigation parent object_id: 1968
D, [2026-01-22T12:31:58.180618 #53687] DEBUG -- :   Child Load (0.1ms)  SELECT `children`.* FROM `children` WHERE `children`.`parent_id` = 1 ORDER BY `children`.`id` ASC LIMIT 1
D, [2026-01-22T12:31:58.180886 #53687] DEBUG -- :   Parent Load (0.1ms)  SELECT `parents`.* FROM `parents` WHERE `parents`.`store_id` = 1 AND `parents`.`id` = 1 LIMIT 1
E, [2026-01-22T12:31:58.180913 #53687] ERROR -- : inverse_of isn't set on child.parent.children.first.parent
```

Interestingly, `ActiveRecord::Associations::Preloader.new(records: [parent], associations: [:children]).call` makes it work properly.

### System configuration

**Rails version**: 7 and 8 (latest in rubygems)

**Ruby version**:  3.4.7
rails/rails#56662.title: ActiveRecord::Associations::Association#inversable? does not work with composite keys

rails/rails#56675.body:
### Description

``` ruby

  ActiveSupport::Inflector.inflections(:en) do |inflect|
    inflect.acronym "USD"
    inflect.acronym "USDC"
  end 

  "USDC".underscore # => Actual "usd_c", expected "usdc" 
```

The behavior is correct when acronyms are defined in different order

 ### Steps to reproduce

``` ruby
  # frozen_string_literal: true

  require "bundler/inline"

  gemfile(true) do
    source "https://rubygems.org"

    git_source(:github) { |repo| "https://github.com/#{repo}.git" }

    gem "rails"
    # If you want to test against edge Rails replace the previous line with this:
    # gem "rails", github: "rails/rails", branch: "main"
  end

  require "active_support"
  require "active_support/core_ext/string"
  require "minitest/autorun"

  class BugTest < Minitest::Test
    def test_overlapping_acronyms_order_affects_underscore
      ActiveSupport::Inflector.inflections(:en) do |inflect|
        inflect.acronym "USD"
        inflect.acronym "USDC"
      end

      # With overlapping acronyms, "USDC" is split as "usd_c" when "USD" is defined first.
      assert_equal "usdc", "USDC".underscore
    ensure
      ActiveSupport::Inflector.inflections(:en).clear(:acronyms)
    end

    def test_overlapping_acronyms_reverse_order
      ActiveSupport::Inflector.inflections(:en) do |inflect|
        inflect.acronym "USDC"
        inflect.acronym "USD"
      end

      assert_equal "usdc", "USDC".underscore
    ensure
      ActiveSupport::Inflector.inflections(:en).clear(:acronyms)
    end
  end
```

  ### Expected behavior

  Overlapping acronyms should be handled consistently regardless of definition order, ideally preferring the longest match so "USDC".underscore is "usdc" in both
  cases.

  ### Actual behavior

  When "USD" is defined before "USDC", "USDC".underscore returns "usd_c". If "USDC" is defined first, "USDC".underscore returns "usdc".

  ### System configuration

  Rails version: 8.1.0

  Ruby version: 3.4.3
rails/rails#56675.title: Overlapping acronyms (like USD and USDC) definition order gives different inflection results
