rails/rails#56766.body:
ActiveRecord::Type::Json caches a no-escape encoder on class load, which prevents being able to change the JSON encoder later. Seems to have been broken in 8.1 by https://github.com/rails/rails/pull/54748/

### Steps to reproduce
1) Preload ActiveRecord (some Gems might do this to include themselves)
2) Override the ActiveSupport JSON Encoder
3) Notice the precached value in ActiveRecord::Type::Json::JSON_ENCODER is using the `json` gem instead of the specified one

```ruby
# frozen_string_literal: true

require "bundler/inline"

ENV["DATABASE_URL"] = "sqlite3::memory:"

gemfile(true) do
  source "https://rubygems.org"

  gem "rails"
  gem "json"
  # If you want to test against edge Rails replace the previous line with this:
  # gem "rails", github: "rails/rails", branch: "main"

  gem "sqlite3"
end

require "active_record/railtie"
require "minitest/autorun"

class NoOpJsonEncoder
  def initialize(**); end

  def encode(_value)
    ""
  end
end

ActiveRecord::Base # preload ActiveRecord like some gems might do

class TestApp < Rails::Application
  config.load_defaults Rails::VERSION::STRING.to_f
  config.eager_load = false
  config.logger = Logger.new($stdout)
  config.secret_key_base = "secret_key_base"

  config.active_record.encryption.primary_key = "primary_key"
  config.active_record.encryption.deterministic_key = "deterministic_key"
  config.active_record.encryption.key_derivation_salt = "key_derivation_salt"
  ActiveSupport::JSON::Encoding.json_encoder = NoOpJsonEncoder
end
Rails.application.initialize!

class BugTest < ActiveSupport::TestCase
  def test_correct_encoder
    assert_equal NoOpJsonEncoder, ActiveSupport::JSON::Encoding.json_encoder
    assert_equal NoOpJsonEncoder, ActiveRecord::Type::Json::JSON_ENCODER.class
  end
end
```

### Expected behavior

`Type::Json` uses my specified encoder rather than what it deemed available at class load.

`Type::Json` probably shouldn't cache an encoder at all, since `ActiveSupport::JSON::Encoding` already caches a no-escape encoder when the encoder is changed.

### Actual behavior

`Type::Json` uses whatever encoder it found in ActiveSupport whenever it happened to be initialized first.
<!-- Tell us what happens instead -->

### System configuration

**Rails version**: 8.1

**Ruby version**: N/A
rails/rails#56766.title: ActiveRecord::Type::Json too eagerly caches its JSON_ENCODER

rails/rails#56784.body:
### Steps to reproduce

```
#!/usr/bin/env ruby
# frozen_string_literal: true

# Standalone script to verify the Rails 8 JSONGemCoderEncoder hash key bug
# Run with: ruby verify_bug.rb [rails_version]
# Examples:
#   ruby verify_bug.rb          # defaults to Rails 8.1.2
#   ruby verify_bug.rb 7.2.3    # test with Rails 7.2.3
#   ruby verify_bug.rb 8.0.4    # test with Rails 8.0.4

require "bundler/inline"

rails_gem_version = ARGV[0] || "8.1.2"

gemfile do
  source "https://rubygems.org"
  gem "activesupport", rails_gem_version
  gem "json", "~> 2.18.0"
end

require "active_support/all"

# Custom key class that mimics real-world domain objects
class CustomKey
  def initialize(id)
    @id = id
    @metadata = { created_at: Time.now.iso8601 }
  end

  # we do something like this in our codebase. Basically let this object "stringify" itself.
  def to_s
    "custom_#{@id}"
  end

  def as_json(options = nil)
    { id: @id, metadata: @metadata }
  end
end


puts "Rails #{ActiveSupport.gem_version} - JSONGemCoderEncoder Hash Key Bug Test"
puts "Testing with ActiveSupport gem version constraint: #{rails_gem_version}"

key = CustomKey.new(123)
hash = { key => "some_value" }
encoder = ActiveSupport::JSON::Encoding.json_encoder
result = hash.to_json
parsed = JSON.parse(result)

puts "\nCustom key:"
puts "  key.to_s: #{key.to_s}"
puts "  key.as_json: #{key.as_json.inspect}"

puts "\nEncoder being used: #{encoder}"

puts "\nSerialization results:"
puts "  hash.to_json: #{result}"
puts "  Parsed keys: #{parsed.keys.inspect}"

if parsed.key?("custom_123")
  puts "\n✅ PASS: Key correctly serialized as 'custom_123'"
  exit 0
else
  puts "\n❌ FAIL: Key incorrectly serialized as: #{parsed.keys.first}"
  puts "\nExpected: {\"custom_123\":\"value\"}"
  puts "Got:      #{result}"
  exit 1
end
```

**How to run:**
1. Save the script as `verify_bug.rb`
2. Run: `ruby verify_bug.rb 8.1.2` (fails, showing the bug)
3. Run: `ruby verify_bug.rb 7.2.3` (passes, showing it worked before on Rails 7.2)
4. Run: `ruby verify_bug.rb 8.0.4` (passes, showing it worked before on Rails 8.0)

### Expected behavior

When a hash uses a custom object as a key, JSON serialization should call `to_s` on the **original key object** to convert it to a string key.

**Expected output:**
```json
{"custom_123":"some_value"}
```

This is the behavior in Rails 8.0.x and earlier.

### Actual behavior

In Rails 8.1.x, when a custom object's `as_json` method returns a Hash (a common Rails pattern), the encoder incorrectly calls `to_s` on the **Hash returned by `as_json`** instead of on the original object.

**Actual output:**
```json
{"{:id=>123, :metadata=>{:created_at=>\"2026-02-12T...\"}}":"some_value"}
```

The hash key becomes Ruby's string representation of a Hash (`"{:id=>123, ...}"`) instead of the object's `to_s` value (`"custom_123"`).

Per this commit around lines 149-150, there's a guard clause introduced:
```
# Keep compatibility by calling to_s on non-String keys
next json_value.to_s if is_key
```
But this calls `.to_s` on `value.as_json` instead of just `value`. Thus, if you have a custom object that handles it's own key definition (see script above), the json encoding incorrectly stringifies the whole hash.

**Location**: `activesupport/lib/active_support/json/encoding.rb` line ~150

**Introduced**: Rails 8.1.0 (Sept 22, 2025) via [commit 2761020](https://github.com/rails/rails/commit/2761020ddd1e9675d5f4d4573f2bf31b9decdebb)

**Still Present**: Rails 8.1.2, Rails main branch (as of Feb 12, 2026)

### System configuration

**Rails version**: 8.1.2, but introduced in 8.1.0

**Ruby version**: 3.3.6, but most likely independent of Ruby version

---
**Note:** I am happy to submit a PR with the fix if this is confirmed as a bug. All materials are ready (test, fix, CHANGELOG entry).

**Later Note**: Submitted PR here: https://github.com/rails/rails/pull/56785
rails/rails#56784.title: JSONGemCoderEncoder incorrectly serializes custom object hash keys

rails/rails#56795.body:
### Steps to reproduce

Create two tables where one has a SQLite GENERATED column and the other has a regular column with the same name and type.

```ruby
# frozen_string_literal: true

require "bundler/inline"

gemfile(true) do
  source "https://rubygems.org"

  gem "rails", "~> 8.1"
  # If you want to test against edge Rails replace the previous line with this:
  # gem "rails", github: "rails/rails", branch: "main"

  gem "sqlite3"
end

require "active_record/railtie"
require "minitest/autorun"

ENV["DATABASE_URL"] = "sqlite3::memory:"

class TestApp < Rails::Application
  config.load_defaults Rails::VERSION::STRING.to_f
  config.eager_load = false
  config.logger = Logger.new($stdout)
  config.secret_key_base = "secret_key_base"

  config.active_record.encryption.primary_key = "primary_key"
  config.active_record.encryption.deterministic_key = "deterministic_key"
  config.active_record.encryption.key_derivation_salt = "key_derivation_salt"
end
Rails.application.initialize!

ActiveRecord::Schema.define do
  # Parent table has a GENERATED column named "name"
  execute <<~SQL
    CREATE TABLE parents (
      id integer PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT NOT NULL,
      raw_config json,
      name varchar GENERATED ALWAYS AS (json_extract(raw_config, '$.name')) STORED,
      created_at datetime(6) NOT NULL,
      updated_at datetime(6) NOT NULL
    )
  SQL

  # Child table has a regular column also named "name"
  create_table :children, force: true do |t|
    t.integer :parent_id
    t.string :name
    t.timestamps
  end
end

class Parent < ActiveRecord::Base
  has_many :children
end

class Child < ActiveRecord::Base
  belongs_to :parent, optional: true
end

class BugTest < ActiveSupport::TestCase
  # Ensure Parent schema is loaded first so its GENERATED "name" column
  # enters the Deduplicable registry before Child's regular "name" column.
  def setup
    Parent.columns_hash
  end

  def test_child_name_persists_when_created_directly
    child = Child.create!(name: "hello")
    child.reload
    assert_equal "hello", child.name, "Child name should be persisted"
  end

  def test_child_name_persists_via_association
    parent = Parent.create!(raw_config: { name: "parent_name" }.to_json)
    child = parent.children.create!(name: "child_name")
    child.reload
    assert_equal "child_name", child.name, "Child name should be persisted via association"
  end

  def test_child_name_persists_via_update_with_association_replacement
    parent = Parent.create!(raw_config: { name: "parent_name" }.to_json)
    new_children = [Child.new(name: "replaced_child")]
    parent.update!(children: new_children)

    saved_child = parent.children.first
    saved_child.reload
    assert_equal "replaced_child", saved_child.name,
      "Child name should be persisted when set via association replacement"
  end

  def test_child_name_column_is_not_virtual
    col = Child.column_for_attribute("name")
    assert_not col.virtual?,
      "Child's 'name' column should not be virtual — it is a regular column, " \
      "not a GENERATED column. Parent's 'name' is GENERATED, but that should " \
      "not affect Child."
  end
end
```

### Expected behavior

`Child.create!(name: "my_child_name")` should persist the `name` column. A regular column on one table should never be affected by a GENERATED column on a different table.

### Actual behavior

The `name` column is silently excluded from INSERT (and UPDATE) statements on `children`, causing NULL values in the database. No error or warning is raised.

## Fix

`SQLite3::Column#==` and `#hash` should include `@generated_type`:

```ruby
def ==(other)
  other.is_a?(Column) &&
    super &&
    auto_increment? == other.auto_increment? &&
    virtual? == other.virtual?
end
alias :eql? :==

def hash
  Column.hash ^
    super.hash ^
    auto_increment?.hash ^
    rowid.hash ^
    virtual?.hash
end
```

## Workaround

An initializer that patches the equality methods and clears the registry:

```ruby
require "active_record/connection_adapters/sqlite3/column"

module ActiveRecord
  module ConnectionAdapters
    module SQLite3
      class Column < ConnectionAdapters::Column
        def ==(other)
          other.is_a?(Column) &&
            super &&
            auto_increment? == other.auto_increment? &&
            virtual? == other.virtual?
        end
        alias :eql? :==

        def hash
          Column.hash ^
            super.hash ^
            auto_increment?.hash ^
            rowid.hash ^
            virtual?.hash
        end
      end
    end
  end
end

ActiveRecord::ConnectionAdapters::SQLite3::Column.registry.clear
```

### System configuration

- **Rails version**: 8.1.2
- **Ruby version**: 3.4.7
- **Database**: SQLite3
rails/rails#56795.title: SQLite3 Column deduplication ignores `generated_type`, causing silent data loss across tables

rails/rails#56819.body:
### Steps to reproduce

```ruby
# With more records than the limit in the database:
Post.limit(5).in_batches(of: 3, use_ranges: true) { |batch| batch.count }
```

### Expected behavior

Iterates over 5 records in batches of 3 (batch of 3, then batch of 2).

### Actual behavior

Raises `PG::InvalidRowCountInLimitClause: ERROR: LIMIT must not be negative` (or equivalent on other adapters).

### Root cause

In the `use_ranges` code path of `batch_on_unloaded_relation`, `values_size` is set to `batch_limit` rather than the actual number of records fetched. When `remaining < batch_limit`, the relation's limit is updated via `relation = relation.limit(remaining)`, but `batch_limit` is not updated. On the next iteration, `remaining -= values_size` subtracts the stale (larger) `batch_limit`, causing `remaining` to go negative, which results in `relation.limit(-1)`.

### System configuration

**Rails version**: main (also affects 7.2+, since `use_ranges` was introduced)

**Ruby version**: all supported versions

### Fix

A fix is available in #56803.
rails/rails#56819.title: in_batches with use_ranges: true and limit raises negative LIMIT error

rails/rails#56869.body:
### Steps to reproduce
Some ActiveRecord class Foo with `datetime` field `bar`

```ruby
ranges = (0...2000).map { |i| (Date.new(2024, 1, 1) + i).all_day }
Foo.where(bar: ranges).to_sql
```

### Expected behavior

We expect to receive no exceptions, but normal (long) SQL string

### Actual behavior

It raises `SystemStackError: stack level too deep (SystemStackError)` 

```ruby
SystemStackError: stack level too deep (SystemStackError)
  arel/visitors/to_sql.rb:888:in `each_with_index'
    list.each_with_index do |x, i|
  arel/visitors/to_sql.rb:888:in `inject_join'
    list.each_with_index do |x, i|
  arel/visitors/to_sql.rb:617:in `visit_Arel_Nodes_Or'
    inject_join o.children, collector, " OR "
  arel/visitors/visitor.rb:30:in `visit'
    send dispatch_method, object, collector
  arel/visitors/to_sql.rb:328:in `visit_Arel_Nodes_Grouping'
    visit(o.expr, collector) << ")"
```

### System configuration

**Rails version**: 8.1.2

**Ruby version**:  4.0.1

Root cause: When we pass an array of Range objects to where(foo: ranges), ActiveRecord/Arel converts each range into a BETWEEN condition and joins them with OR. Internally, Arel builds this as a deeply nested binary tree of Or nodes:                                                                                                                                                                                            
                                                                                                                                                                                                                               
    Or(                                                                                                                                                                                                                        
      Grouping(Between(t, range1)),                                                                                                                                                                                            
      Or(                                                                                                                                                                                                                      
        Grouping(Between(t, range2)),                                                                                                                                                                                          
        Or(                                                                                                                                                                                                                    
          Grouping(Between(t, range3)),                                                                                                                                                                                        
          ...                                                                                                                                                                                                                  
        )                                                                                                                                                                                                                      
      )                                                                                                                                                                                                                        
    )                                                                                                                                                                                                                          
                                                                                                                                                                                                                               
The SQL generator (Arel::Visitors::ToSql) traverses this tree recursively — exactly the cycle we see in the stack trace:                                                                                                  
                                                                                                                                                                                                                               
   visit_Arel_Nodes_Or → inject_join → visit → visit_Arel_Nodes_Grouping → visit → visit_Arel_Nodes_Or → ...
rails/rails#56869.title: 'SystemStackError: stack level too deep' for '.to_sql' call on 'where' condition with long array of ranges

rails/rails#56890.body:
### Steps to reproduce
```
# frozen_string_literal: true

require "bundler/inline"

gemfile(true) do
  source "https://rubygems.org"

  # gem "rails"
  # If you want to test against edge Rails replace the previous line with this:
  gem "rails", github: "rails/rails", branch: "main"
end

require "action_controller/railtie"
require "action_view/railtie"
require "minitest/autorun"

class TestApp < Rails::Application
  config.load_defaults Rails::VERSION::STRING.to_f
  config.eager_load = false
  config.logger = Logger.new($stdout)
  config.secret_key_base = "secret_key_base"
  config.action_controller.perform_caching = true
end
Rails.application.initialize!

Customer = Struct.new(:name, :id)

class BugTest < ActionView::TestCase
  setup do
    @cache_store_was = ActionView::PartialRenderer.collection_cache
    ActionView::PartialRenderer.collection_cache = ActiveSupport::Cache::MemoryStore.new(expires_in: 1.hour)

    @view_path = Pathname.new(Dir.mktmpdir)
    ActionController::Base.prepend_view_path @view_path
    controller.perform_caching = true
  end

  teardown do
    @view_path.rmtree
    ActionView::PartialRenderer.collection_cache = @cache_store_was
  end

  helper do
    def upcase(value)
      value.upcase
    end
  end

  def test_collection_caching_without_expires_in_overwrites_the_default_expires_in
    file_path = "customers/_customer.html.erb"
    view_file file_path, "<%= greeting %>: <%= customer.name %>"

    customer = Customer.new("david", 1)
    key = cache_key(customer, virtual_path: "customers/_customer")
    assert_nil ActionView::PartialRenderer.collection_cache.read(key)
    render partial: "customers/customer", collection: [customer], cached: true, locals: { greeting: "Hello" }
    assert_equal "Hello: david", ActionView::PartialRenderer.collection_cache.read(key)

    travel 2.hours

    assert_nil ActionView::PartialRenderer.collection_cache.read(key)
  end

  private
    def cache_key(*names, virtual_path:)
      digest = ActionView::Digestor.digest(
        name: virtual_path,
        format: :html,
        finder: lookup_context,
        dependencies: []
      )
      view.combined_fragment_cache_key([ "#{virtual_path}:#{digest}", *names ])
    end

    def view_file(filename, contents)
      pathname = @view_path.join(filename)
      pathname.dirname.mkpath
      pathname.write(contents.chomp)
    end

end

```

### Expected behavior

When using collection caching with `cached: true`, `expires_in` should not be passed to `write_multi` if it is not explicitly provided. The cache store’s default expiration should remain in effect.

If `cached: { expires_in: ... }` is explicitly specified, that value should be honored.

### Actual behavior

`expires_in: nil` is passed to `write_multi` when `expires_in` is not provided in the cache options. This overrides the cache store’s default expiration.

### System configuration

**Rails version**: main branch (after #51579)

**Ruby version**: 3.x

I will add a PR directly after adding this issue
rails/rails#56890.title: Regression: ActionView collection caching always overrides cache store default expires_in

rails/rails#56904.body:
### Steps to reproduce When a template uses strict locals with non-ASCII default values (e.g. `café`) and the template body also contains non-ASCII characters, rendering raises `Encoding::CompatibilityError`. ```ruby # frozen_string_literal: true require "bundler/inline" gemfile(true) do source "https://rubygems.org" gem "rails" # If you want to test against edge Rails replace the previous line with this: # gem "rails", github: "rails/rails", branch: "main" end require "action_controller/railtie" require "action_view/railtie" require "minitest/autorun" class TestApp < Rails::Application config.load_defaults Rails::VERSION::STRING.to_f config.eager_load = false config.logger = Logger.new($stdout) config.secret_key_base = "secret_key_base" end Rails.application.initialize! class BugTest < ActionView::TestCase setup do @view_path = Pathname.new(Dir.mktmpdir) ActionController::Base.prepend_view_path @view_path end teardown do @view_path.rmtree end def test_strict_locals_with_non_ascii_default view_file "posts/show.html.erb", <<~ERB <%# locals: (label: "caf\u00E9") -%>

<%= label %> \u00FC

ERB render template: "posts/show" assert_includes rendered.to_s, "caf\u00E9" end private def view_file(filename, contents) pathname = @view_path.join(filename) pathname.dirname.mkpath pathname.write(contents.chomp) end end ``` ### Expected behavior The template renders successfully, producing `

café ü

`. ### Actual behavior Raises `Encoding::CompatibilityError: incompatible character encodings: BINARY (ASCII-8BIT) and UTF-8` during template compilation. The root cause is in `ActionView::Template#strict_locals!`. When a template is loaded via `File.binread`, the source is tagged as ASCII-8BIT. `strict_locals!` extracts the locals declaration via regex capture (`$1`), which inherits the ASCII-8BIT tag. The subsequent `encode!` call re-tags the source body as UTF-8, but the already-extracted `@strict_locals` string remains ASCII-8BIT. When `compiled_source` builds the method definition heredoc, the ASCII-8BIT `@strict_locals` (containing bytes > 0x7F) is interpolated alongside the UTF-8 ERB handler output (also containing bytes > 0x7F), triggering the error. ### System configuration **Rails version**: 8.1.2 **Ruby version**: 3.3 / 3.4 / 4.0
rails/rails#56904.title: Encoding::CompatibilityError when strict locals have non-ASCII default values

rails/rails#56960.body:
### Steps to reproduce

You can run the script below. By changing `rails_version`, you can see the test passes in 7.2.0 but fails in 8.0.3.

```ruby
# frozen_string_literal: true

# rails_version = "7.2.0"
rails_version = "8.0.3"

require "bundler/inline"

gemfile(true) do
  source "https://rubygems.org"
  gem "rails", rails_version
end

require "minitest/autorun"
require "action_controller"

class ParametersTransformKeysTest < Minitest::Test
  def test_transform_keys_behavior_change
    params = ActionController::Parameters.new(xxYy: { a: 1 }, xx_yy: {})
    params.transform_keys!(&:underscore)

    # In Rails 7.2, the camel case key's value was preserved.
    # In Rails 8.0.3, this test fails because the snake case key overwrites the camel case one (returns {"xx_yy"=>{}}).
    assert_equal({"xx_yy"=>{"a"=>1}}, params.to_unsafe_h)
  end
end

```

### Expected behavior

In Rails 7.2, when transforming keys causes a key collision, the earlier key's value was preserved. The script above passes in `7.2.0`.

### Actual behavior

In Rails 8.0.3, the later key now correctly overwrites the earlier key (matching standard Ruby `Hash` behavior), causing the script above to fail.

While the new behavior in 8.0.3 seems correct, it is an undocumented breaking change for existing applications. I suspect this is an intentional bug fix (perhaps related to the recent fixes for `ActiveSupport::HashWithIndifferentAccess#transform_keys!` in v8.0.3).

It would be highly appreciated if this change could be explicitly documented in the Changelog or Release Notes to prevent confusion during upgrades.

### System configuration

**Rails version**: 8.0.3 (changed from 7.2)

**Ruby version**: 3.3.7
rails/rails#56960.title: Undocumented behavior change in `ActionController::Parameters#transform_keys!` (Rails 7.2 vs 8.0.3)

rails/rails#56969.body:
### Steps to reproduce

This bug occurs when using [SpatiaLite](https://www.gaia-gis.it/fossil/libspatialite/index) with GpkgMode & spatial metadata. Note that you must have Spatialite installed on your system so the extension is available. 

```bash
# Debian / Ubuntu / Linux Mint
sudo apt-get update && sudo apt-get install -y libsqlite3-mod-spatialite

# Fedora / RHEL / CentOS / Rocky Linux / AlmaLinux
sudo dnf install -y libspatialite

# Arch Linux / Manjaro
sudo pacman -S libspatialite
 ```

```ruby
require "bundler/inline"

gemfile(true) do
  source "https://rubygems.org"
  gem "rails"
  gem "sqlite3"
end

require "active_record/railtie"
require "minitest/autorun"
require "stringio"

# This connection will do for database-independent bug reports.
ENV["DATABASE_URL"] = "sqlite3::memory:"

class TestApp < Rails::Application
  config.load_defaults Rails::VERSION::STRING.to_f
  config.eager_load = false
  config.logger = Logger.new($stdout)
  config.secret_key_base = "secret_key_base"

  config.active_record.encryption.primary_key = "primary_key"
  config.active_record.encryption.deterministic_key = "deterministic_key"
  config.active_record.encryption.key_derivation_salt = "key_derivation_salt"
end
Rails.application.initialize!

# Configure SQLite with SpatiaLite extension
ActiveRecord::Base.establish_connection(
  adapter: "sqlite3",
  database: ":memory:",
  extensions: ["mod_spatialite"]
)

# Initialize SpatiaLite (creates virtual tables with empty parentheses)
connection = ActiveRecord::Base.connection
connection.execute("SELECT EnableGpkgMode();")
connection.execute("SELECT InitSpatialMetadata(1);")

class BugTest < ActiveSupport::TestCase
  def test_schema_dump_with_spatialite_virtual_tables
    output = StringIO.new
    ActiveRecord::SchemaDumper.dump(ActiveRecord::Base.connection.pool, output)
  end
end
```

### Expected behavior

Schema dump should produce valid Ruby code and not result in an invalid `schema.rb`

### Actual behavior

Running `bin/rails db:migrate` with these virtual tables crashes the schema dumper, which  produces a broken `db/schema.rb` missing its closing `end`. That's not valid Ruby code (duh) and causes all kinds of follow-up problems. The user has to go in and manually fix the `schema.rb` (by adding the missing end) after each migration.

#### Root Cause

SpatiaLite registers three virtual tables with **empty parentheses**:

```sql
CREATE VIRTUAL TABLE SpatialIndex USING VirtualSpatialIndex()
CREATE VIRTUAL TABLE ElementaryGeometries USING VirtualElementary()
CREATE VIRTUAL TABLE KNN2 USING VirtualKNN2()
```

Rails 8.1 added virtual table support for SQLite (primarily for FTS5) and parses them with:

```ruby
# activerecord/lib/active_record/connection_adapters/sqlite3_adapter.rb:348
VIRTUAL_TABLE_REGEX = /USING\s+(\w+)\s*\((.+)\)/i
```

The `.+` requires **at least one character** inside the parentheses. SpatiaLite's tables have empty parens `()`, so the regex doesn't match. `.match()` returns `nil`, `.to_a` gives `[]`, and both `module_name` and `arguments` end up `nil`, which causes the crash. 

#### Proposed Fix

The fix is trivial. Change the Regex that parses Virtual tables to match empty parenthesis.

```ruby
VIRTUAL_TABLE_REGEX = /USING\s+(\w+)\s*\((.*)\)/i
```

The new regex `(.*)` matches zero or more characters.  Matching  `()` returns empty string `""`, which is fine because  `"".split(", ")` returns `[]` without error. 

### System configuration

**Rails version**: 8.1

**Ruby version**:  Tested with `4.0.1` but should apply to any
rails/rails#56969.title: SQLite virtual tables without parenthesis produces broken `schema.rb`

rails/rails#56976.body:
### Steps to reproduce

```ruby
# frozen_string_literal: true

require "bundler/inline"

gemfile(true) do
  source "https://rubygems.org"

  gem "rails"
  gem "sqlite3"
end

require "active_record/railtie"
require "minitest/autorun"

ENV["DATABASE_URL"] = "sqlite3::memory:"

class TestApp < Rails::Application
  config.load_defaults Rails::VERSION::STRING.to_f
  config.eager_load = false
  config.logger = Logger.new($stdout)
  config.secret_key_base = "secret_key_base"

  config.active_record.encryption.primary_key = "primary_key"
  config.active_record.encryption.deterministic_key = "deterministic_key"
  config.active_record.encryption.key_derivation_salt = "key_derivation_salt"

  config.active_record.schema_format = :ruby
end
Rails.application.initialize!

ActiveRecord::SchemaDumper.ignore_tables << "fts_docs"

connection = ActiveRecord::Base.connection
connection.execute <<~SQL
  CREATE VIRTUAL TABLE fts_docs USING fts5(content)
SQL

class BugTest < ActiveSupport::TestCase
  def test_ignore_tables_applies_to_virtual_tables_in_schema_dump
    stream = StringIO.new
    ActiveRecord::SchemaDumper.dump(ActiveRecord::Base.connection_pool, stream)
    dump = stream.string

    assert_not_includes dump, 'create_virtual_table "fts_docs"'
  end
end
```

### Expected behavior

Schema dump should respect `ActiveRecord::SchemaDumper.ignore_tables` for SQLite virtual tables and omit ignored virtual tables from `schema.rb` output. This is relevant for generated virtual tables (e.g. Spatialite, see #56969).

### Actual behavior

Ignored virtual tables are still emitted in the schema dump. For the script above, `fts_docs` is still included as:

```ruby
create_virtual_table "fts_docs", "fts5", ["content"]
```

#### Proposed Fix

Check if virtual tables are ignored. 

```ruby
# activerecord/lib/active_record/connection_adapters/sqlite3/schema_dumper.rb
def virtual_tables(stream)
  virtual_tables = @connection.virtual_tables.reject { |name, _| ignored?(name) }
  # ....
end
```

### System configuration

**Rails version**: 8.1

**Ruby version**: 4.0.1, but shouldn't matter
rails/rails#56976.title: SQLite virtual tables do not respect `ignore_tables`

rails/rails#57052.body:
### Steps to reproduce

#55794 (fixing #55513) added a `Process.waitpid(pid, WNOHANG)` sweep in `Parallelization#shutdown` to detect dead workers **before** calling `Server#shutdown`. This helps when workers are already dead at shutdown time, but doesn't cover the case where workers exit **during** `Server#shutdown`'s `while active_workers?` loop.

If a worker finishes its tests and exits without calling `stop_worker` over DRb (e.g. a `parallelize_teardown` hook raises, the DRb connection drops, or the worker is OOM-killed mid-cleanup), and this happens **after** the initial WNOHANG sweep, `Server#shutdown` loops forever.

We're hitting this intermittently in CI with system tests (Capybara/Cuprite). Workers become zombies, but the parent hangs until a watchdog kills it ~90s later.

```ruby
# frozen_string_literal: true

require "bundler/inline"

gemfile(true) do
  source "https://rubygems.org"

  gem "rails", "~> 8.1.0"
  # If you want to test against edge Rails replace the previous line with this:
  # gem "rails", github: "rails/rails", branch: "main"
end

require "active_support"
require "active_support/testing/parallelization"
require "minitest/autorun"

class ParallelShutdownBugTest < ActiveSupport::TestCase
  # Demonstrates that a worker exiting without calling stop_worker
  # AFTER Parallelization#shutdown's initial WNOHANG sweep causes
  # Server#shutdown to loop forever on `while active_workers?`.
  #
  # This is the gap left by rails/rails#55794 which only sweeps once
  # before entering Server#shutdown.
  def test_shutdown_detects_workers_that_die_during_server_shutdown
    parallelization = ActiveSupport::Testing::Parallelization.new(1)

    # Access internals to set up the scenario
    server = parallelization.instance_variable_get(:@queue_server)
    url = parallelization.instance_variable_get(:@url)

    # Push one unit of work so the worker has something to do
    server << ["BugTest", "test_stuff", Minitest::CompositeReporter.new]

    # Fork a worker that registers, does work, but exits without
    # calling stop_worker (simulates cleanup hook raising or DRb failure)
    worker_pid = fork do
      DRb.stop_service
      queue = DRbObject.new_with_uri(url)
      queue.start_worker(0, Process.pid)
      queue.pop
      sleep 0.3 # simulate test execution
      exit!(0)  # exit without stop_worker
    end

    # Small delay so worker registers but hasn't exited yet
    sleep 0.1

    # This is Parallelization#shutdown (with #55794 fix):
    # 1. WNOHANG sweep — worker is still alive, finds nothing
    dead_worker_pids = [worker_pid].filter_map do |pid|
      Process.waitpid(pid, Process::WNOHANG)
    rescue Errno::ECHILD
      pid
    end
    server.remove_dead_workers(dead_worker_pids)

    assert_equal 0, dead_worker_pids.size, "Worker should still be alive during sweep"

    # 2. Server#shutdown — worker exits during this wait, hangs forever
    assert_raises(Timeout::Error) do
      Timeout.timeout(3) { server.shutdown }
    end

    # Clean up zombie
    Process.waitpid(worker_pid, Process::WNOHANG) rescue nil
  end
end
```

### Expected behavior

`Server#shutdown` should detect workers that die during its `while active_workers?` loop and exit cleanly.

A fix would be to move dead-worker detection **into** the loop:

```ruby
def shutdown
  sleep 0.1 until @queue.empty?
  @queue.close

  while active_workers?
    dead_pids = @worker_pids.each_value.select do |pid|
      Process.waitpid(pid, Process::WNOHANG)
    rescue Errno::ECHILD
      true
    end
    remove_dead_workers(dead_pids) if dead_pids.any?
    sleep 0.1
  end

  # ... report in-flight tests
end
```

### Actual behavior

`Server#shutdown` loops forever on `while active_workers?`. The worker is a zombie (exited), but the server never checks whether worker PIDs are still alive inside the loop.

### System configuration

**Rails version**: 8.1.2 (also affects 8.0.4 and any version with #55794 applied)

**Ruby version**: 3.3.10
rails/rails#57052.title: Parallelization::Server#shutdown hangs when workers die after initial WNOHANG sweep (#55794 gap)

rails/rails#57092.body:
### Steps to reproduce

When a query contains many `select ` and is extremely long, [regexp](https://github.com/rails/rails/blob/706b9daab37664710de20cca273eb9ffa5361ec3/activerecord/lib/active_record/log_subscriber.rb#L87) evaluation inside `ActiveRecord::LogSubscriber#sql_color` takes an excessive amount of time.

```ruby
# frozen_string_literal: true

require "bundler/inline"

gemfile(true) do
  source "https://rubygems.org"

  gem "rails"
end

require "active_record"

very_long_body = "select #{'a' * 1000}" * 100_000
puts "bytesize: #{very_long_body.bytesize / 1024 / 1024} MB"

started_at = Time.now
ActiveRecord::LogSubscriber.allocate.send(:sql_color, very_long_body)
puts "elapsed: #{Time.now - started_at}"
```

Result:

```
$ ruby benchmark.rb
...snip...
bytesize: 96 MB
elapsed: 3.320321
```

Since Rails sets `Regexp.timeout = 1` by [default](https://github.com/rails/rails/blob/706b9daab37664710de20cca273eb9ffa5361ec3/railties/lib/rails/application/configuration.rb#L344), this results in timeout error during instrumentation.
When this timeout occurs, the query is not logged at all, and instead, the following error is logged:

```
Could not log "sql.active_record" event. Regexp::TimeoutError: regexp match timeout
```

The example code is artificial; however, we have use cases where very long texts or binaries are bulk-inserted, causing the regexp to time out and making those queries invisible in the logs.

### Expected behavior

The query should be logged even if it's too large to be colorized efficiently.
LogSubscriber should rescue `Regexp::TimeoutError` and log the original uncolorized string as a fallback.

### Actual behavior

The query is lost. Only the following error is recorded:

```
Could not log "sql.active_record" event. Regexp::TimeoutError: regexp match timeout
```

This makes it difficult to debug issues involving large payloads.

### System configuration

**Rails version**: 8.1.3

**Ruby version**: 4.0.1
rails/rails#57092.title: Regexp in `ActiveRecord::LogSubscriber` times out on extremely long queries, preventing them from being logged

rails/rails#57093.body:
## Summary

When a Rails application is configured with multiple PostgreSQL schemas via
`schema_search_path`, the schema dumper writes table names qualified with their
schema into `schema.rb`:

```ruby
create_table "public.my_table_name", force: :cascade do |t|
```

When Rails loads this schema back (`db:schema:load`, `db:test:prepare`), the
`validate_table_length!` check introduced in Rails 7.1 measures the length of
the full `"public.my_table_name"` string instead of just the bare identifier
`"my_table_name"`. This causes valid table names to be rejected with an
`ArgumentError`, making `db:test:prepare` permanently broken for apps that use
multiple schemas and have table names near the length limit.

## Steps to reproduce

Run the attached reproduction script against any PostgreSQL database:

```bash
DATABASE_URL=postgresql://user:pass@localhost/mydb ruby rails_schema_prefix_length_bug.rb
```

The script demonstrates:

1. A 52-char table name is created without issue (52 ≤ 58-char limit).
2. The same name prefixed with `public.` (59 chars total) raises `ArgumentError`
   when passed to `create_table` — exactly as the schema dumper would write it.
3. PostgreSQL itself accepts the schema-prefixed DDL via raw SQL with no error,
   proving the problem is in Rails' validation alone.

<details>
<summary>Reproduction script</summary>

```ruby
# frozen_string_literal: true
require "bundler/inline"

gemfile(true) do
  source "https://rubygems.org"
  gem "activerecord", "~> 7.1"
  gem "pg"
  gem "minitest"
end

require "active_record"
require "minitest/autorun"

ActiveRecord::Base.establish_connection(
  ENV["DATABASE_URL"] || {
    adapter:  "postgresql",
    database: ENV.fetch("PGDATABASE", "rails_bug_repro"),
    host:     ENV.fetch("PGHOST",     "localhost"),
    username: ENV.fetch("PGUSER",     nil),
    password: ENV.fetch("PGPASSWORD", nil),
  }
)

# PostgreSQL limit = max_identifier_length(63) - "_pkey"(5) = 58 chars.
# 52-char name passes bare (52 <= 58) but fails schema-prefixed (59 > 58).
BARE_TABLE_NAME     = "t" * 52
PREFIXED_TABLE_NAME = "public.#{BARE_TABLE_NAME}"

class SchemaPrefixTableNameLengthBugTest < Minitest::Test
  def setup
    connection.execute("DROP TABLE IF EXISTS #{PREFIXED_TABLE_NAME}")
    connection.drop_table(BARE_TABLE_NAME, if_exists: true)
  end

  def teardown
    connection.execute("DROP TABLE IF EXISTS #{PREFIXED_TABLE_NAME}")
    connection.drop_table(BARE_TABLE_NAME, if_exists: true)
  end

  def test_bare_table_name_within_limit_succeeds
    connection.create_table(BARE_TABLE_NAME, force: true)
    assert connection.table_exists?(BARE_TABLE_NAME)
  end

  def test_schema_prefixed_table_name_incorrectly_raises
    error = assert_raises(ArgumentError) do
      connection.create_table(PREFIXED_TABLE_NAME, force: true)
    end
    assert_match(/too long/i, error.message)
    puts "\n[BUG] #{error.message}"
    puts "  Bare identifier : #{BARE_TABLE_NAME.length} chars (limit #{connection.table_name_length})"
    puts "  String checked  : #{PREFIXED_TABLE_NAME.length} chars (\"#{PREFIXED_TABLE_NAME}\")"
  end

  def test_postgresql_accepts_schema_prefixed_table_via_raw_sql
    connection.execute("CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS #{PREFIXED_TABLE_NAME} (id bigserial PRIMARY KEY)")
    result = connection.execute(
      "SELECT tablename FROM pg_tables WHERE schemaname='public' AND tablename='#{BARE_TABLE_NAME}'"
    )
    assert_equal 1, result.ntuples
    puts "\n[CONTROL] PostgreSQL created #{PREFIXED_TABLE_NAME} without errors"
  end

  private
  def connection = ActiveRecord::Base.connection
end
```

</details>

## Expected behavior

`create_table "public.my_table_name"` should succeed when the bare identifier
`my_table_name` is within the PostgreSQL identifier length limit. The schema
qualifier is not part of the identifier that PostgreSQL enforces the limit on.

## Actual behavior

```
ArgumentError: Table name 'public.tttt...t' is too long (59 characters); the limit is 58 characters
```

The error is raised even though the actual table identifier (`"t" * 52`) is
only 52 characters — well within the 58-character limit.

## System configuration

- **Rails version:** 7.1.0+ (introduced in commit `1ec879d02b`)
- **Ruby version:** any
- **Database:** PostgreSQL (any version)
- **Adapter:** `pg`

Not affected: Rails 7.0.x and below (`validate_table_length!` did not exist).
rails/rails#57093.title: `create_table` raises ArgumentError for valid table names when schema-prefixed (PostgreSQL multiple schemas)

rails/rails#57098.body:
### Steps to reproduce

1. Create a minimal Rails app or test file that requires `active_storage` directly:

```ruby
require "active_storage"
```

2. Run the file

### Expected behavior

Active Storage loads successfully.

### Actual behavior

```
NameError: uninitialized constant ActiveStorage::OpenSSL
```

### Root cause

PR #54040 (commit e64e5d31) added `checksum_implementation` to support FIPS compliance. The code in `lib/active_storage.rb` references `OpenSSL::Digest::MD5` at module load time (line 368) but never requires `openssl`:

```ruby
singleton_class.attr_accessor :checksum_implementation
@checksum_implementation = OpenSSL::Digest::MD5
begin
  @checksum_implementation.hexdigest("test")
rescue # OpenSSL may have MD5 disabled
  require "digest/md5"
  @checksum_implementation = Digest::MD5
end
```

This works in most Rails apps because something else (credentials, message verifier, etc.) typically requires `openssl` before Active Storage loads. But when Active Storage is required in isolation, it fails.

### Suggested fix

Add `require "openssl"` to `lib/active_storage.rb` before the `checksum_implementation` code.

### System configuration

- **Rails version**: 8.1.3
- **Ruby version**: 3.4.8
rails/rails#57098.title: ActiveStorage 8.1.3: NameError uninitialized constant ActiveStorage::OpenSSL

rubysec/bundler-audit#405.body:
Since bundler versions bumped to 4.0.0dev via https://github.com/ruby/ruby/commit/afe40df42331e338411c84714ca5d21383dac3d4 it is unable to install bundler-audit .

## Description
Rails uses the bundler audit https://github.com/rails/rails/blob/0550d7bd8e217eee8e1b24f9905b3a89894a641d/Gemfile#L145 and Rails runs CI against Ruby master branch aka 3.5.0dev. Today, it does not install bundler-audit gem as follows.

https://buildkite.com/rails/rails-nightly/builds/2964#0199cac6-9634-4a4f-b049-9340551022fe/157-210


## Steps To Reproduce
Here are steps to reproduce.

1. Install Ruby 3.5.0dev including https://github.com/ruby/ruby/commit/afe40df42331e338411c84714ca5d21383dac3d4
```ruby
$ ruby -v
ruby 3.5.0dev (2025-10-09T21:11:53Z master 83d0b064c8) +PRISM [x86_64-linux]
$ gem -v
4.0.0.dev
$ bundler -v
4.0.0.dev
```
2. Create a install-bundler-audit-with-ruby350.rb 
```ruby
require "bundler/inline"

gemfile(true) do
  source "https://rubygems.org"
  gem "bundler-audit"
end
```
3. Run install-bundler-audit-with-ruby350.rb 
```
$ ruby install-bundler-audit-with-ruby350.rb 
```

## Expected Behavior
It should install the bundler-audit gem.

## Actual Behavior
It raises the following error.

```ruby
$ ruby install-bundler-audit-with-ruby350.rb 
Fetching gem metadata from https://rubygems.org/..
Resolving dependencies...
/home/yahonda/.local/share/mise/installs/ruby/trunk/lib/ruby/3.5.0+4/bundler/resolver.rb:123:in 'Bundler::Resolver#solve_versions': Could not find compatible versions (Bundler::SolveFailure)

So, because bundler-audit >= 0.6.1 depends on bundler >= 1.2.0, < 3

Your bundle requires a different version of Bundler than the one you're running.
Install the necessary version with `gem install bundler:1.2.0.rc.2` and rerun bundler using `install-bundler-audit-with-ruby350.rb _1.2.0.rc.2_`
```

```txt
[DEP0169] DeprecationWarning: `url.parse()` behavior is not standardized and prone to errors that have security implications. Use the WHATWG URL API instead. CVEs are not issued for `url.parse()` vulnerabilities.
```
